8.22: YHWH qanani reshit darko qedem mifalav meaz: 'The LORD possessed/created me at the beginning of his work, the first of his acts of old.' The verb qanah is disputed: it can mean 'to acquire/possess' (so most LXX manuscripts, Aquila, Theodotion) or 'to create' (so the Arian controversy reading, applied to prove the Son was a created being). The Nicene theology responded: even if qanah means 'created', Proverbs 8 is personified Wisdom literature — a poetic device, not a literal description of a divine person's ontology. The NT applies Prov 8's Wisdom-portrait to the eternal Son (Col 1:15-17; Heb 1:2-3; John 1:1-3) not to prove the Son is created, but to show that the pre-existent Son is the referent of the Wisdom-personification: the figure the wisdom tradition was groping toward in poetic imagery became flesh in Christ.
8.1: halo'-chokmah tiqra' — “does wisdom not call out?” The rhetorical question marks the shift from the father’s warning about the seductress (ch 7) to Wisdom’s own voice. Chokmah (H2451) and tevunah (understanding, H8394) are the two primary wisdom terms in Proverbs, frequently paired. Their personification as a woman calling in public inverts the adulteress’s lurking presence in ch 7: Wisdom is not secret but public, not hidden but crying aloud at the highest and busiest points of the city.
8.2: be-rosh meromim 'alei-derekh — “on the heights above the road.” Wisdom positions herself at the meromim (heights, elevated places), the road intersections, and the city gates — the three most public locations in an ancient city. The gate (sha'ar, v3) is the site of legal proceedings, commercial transactions, and public assemblies. Wisdom addresses the city at its points of highest traffic and greatest civic importance.
8.3: le-yad she'arim le-fi-qaret — “beside the gates, at the entrance to the city.” She'arim (gates) in the plural encompasses the multiple city gates; pi-qaret (lit. ‘the mouth of the city’) is a phrase for the main city gate as the public mouth through which the city speaks and conducts its life. Wisdom plants herself at the ‘mouth of the city’ — occupying the position of authoritative public speech.
8.4: 'aleichem 'ishim 'eqra' — “to you, O men ('ishim), I am calling.” 'Ishim (H376 plural) is the masculine human; the parallel bene 'adam (sons/children of Adam, H120) broadens the address to all humanity without restriction. The universal address of ch 8 is in deliberate contrast to the adulteress’s targeted seduction of one vulnerable young man; Wisdom calls everyone, without discriminating by social status.
8.5: havu peta'im 'ormah — “O simple ones, learn prudence.” Pethi (simple, H6612) and 'evil (fool, H191) are addressed directly; Wisdom does not exclude the most morally unformed. 'Ormah (prudence, H6195) is the quality of practical wisdom that distinguishes the skilled from the naive; lev (heart, understanding) is what the morally empty are called to receive. The invitation is to transformation, not merely to information.
8.6: shim'u ki-negidim 'adaber — “hear, for I will speak excellent/noble things.” Negidim (H5057) normally means ‘leaders, princes, nobles’; here as an adverb it means ‘excellent, princely, worthy things.’ The opening of wisdom’s discourse claims for itself the dignity of princely speech. Umiftach sefatai mesharim — “and the opening of my lips will be right things” (mesharim, uprightness/equity). The twin claim: noble content and right character.
8.7: emet yehegeh chikki — “my palate meditates truth.” 'Emet (H571, truth/faithfulness) is the first named quality of wisdom’s speech. The verb hagah (H1897, to meditate, mutter, moan — used of the sound a lion makes over its prey and of one who meditates the Torah day and night in Ps 1:2) implies that truth is not merely spoken but internalized and continually turned over. To'avat sefatai resha' — “wickedness is an abomination to my lips”: to'avah (abomination) is the strongest term for moral revulsion in the OT.
8.8: be-tzedeq kol-'imrei-fi — “all the words of my mouth are in righteousness.” Tzedeq (H6664, righteousness, justice) applied to speech means that wisdom’s discourse aligns with the moral order of the created world. 'Ein bahem niftal ve-'iqesh — “there is nothing twisted or crooked in them.” Niftal (twisted, H6617) and 'iqesh (H6141, crooked, perverse) are the two terms for speech that deviates from straight moral truth. Wisdom’s self-description as the straight-speaking voice is the counterpart to the seductress’s chelqat sefatayim (smooth/flattering lips, 7:21).
8.9: kullam nekochim le-meivin — “they are all straightforward to the one who understands.” Nakach (H5228, straight, correct, right before one) is used elsewhere of direct, unmediated confrontation with reality. The condition “to the one who understands” (meivin) introduces the epistemological circularity of wisdom: wisdom’s speech is plain to those who already have wisdom, and opaque to those who do not. This is the wisdom tradition’s version of the Johannine “whoever is of the truth hears my voice” (John 18:37).
8.10: qechu musari ve-'al-kasef — “take my instruction and not silver.” The comparison of wisdom to precious metals is a sustained motif (8:10, 19; 3:14-15; 16:16). Musar (H4148, discipline/instruction, often painful) is paired with da'at (knowledge, H1847) as the two forms of wisdom acquisition: the formative discipline of instruction and the cognitive grasp of knowledge. The claim that these exceed silver (keseph) and gold (charuts, refined/choice gold) is a value-claim about the hierarchy of human goods.
8.11: ki tovah chokmah mipeniniym — “for wisdom is better than jewels.” Peninim (H6443) are the most precious gems — red corals or pearls (also in 3:15 and 31:10, where the excellent wife is “more precious than peninim”). The linking of the wisdom comparison to the excellent wife comparison is not accidental: the man who has wisdom has the equivalent of the best possible wife. Kol-chafatzim lo' yishvu-vah — “and nothing desirable compares to her”: the comprehensive claim.
8.12: 'ani chokmah shakhanti 'ormah — “I, wisdom, have made prudence my dwelling.” The verb shakan (to dwell, to tabernacle — the root of mishkan, the tabernacle of Moses) applied to wisdom’s co-habitation with 'ormah (prudence) describes an intimate domestic relationship. Wisdom and prudence share the same house. Da'at mezimmot emtza' — “and I find knowledge and discretion.” Mezimmot (H4209, discretion, prudent planning) is the wisdom capacity to plan carefully.
8.13: yir'at YHWH seno'-ra' — “the fear of the LORD is hatred of evil.” The definition of yir'at YHWH (fear of the LORD, the most common wisdom formula) here is behavioral rather than affective: it consists in hating evil (ra', H7451). The three parallel objects of hatred — ge'ah (pride), ga'on (arrogance), derekh ra' (the evil way), and peh tahpukhot (the perverse mouth) — identify the enemies of wisdom. Hatred of pride is the disposition that characterizes those who truly fear YHWH.
8.14: li 'etzah ve-tushiyah — “with me are counsel and wisdom.” Tushiyah (H8454) is a term for effective wisdom, sound judgment, or perhaps the deeper “abiding reality” (BDB) of wisdom — the stable foundation that makes it competent. The accumulation of wisdom-terms in vv14-16 ('etzah, tushiyah, binah, gevurah) amounts to a comprehensive claim that all forms of effective human thinking and power belong to wisdom.
8.15: bi melakhim yimloku — “by me kings reign.” The political application of wisdom claims that legitimate rule is founded on wisdom rather than on force or heredity. Choqeqim (rulers who decree, H2710) and rozenim (princes, H7336) are the two categories of human authority; both exercise their authority properly only through wisdom. The grounding of political order in divine wisdom is the OT’s consistent teaching (cf. Rom 13:1’s governance language has this wisdom tradition as background).
8.16: bi sarim yasoru — “by me princes govern.” The parallel to v15 extends the claim from kings to the wider class of officials and nobles. Kol shoftei tzedeq — “all just judges” — identifies the criterion of legitimate judicial authority as tzedeq (justice/righteousness): the judiciary that aligns with wisdom’s moral order is the genuinely authoritative judiciary.
8.17: 'ani 'ohavai 'ehav — “I love those who love me.” The reciprocal love-language of wisdom is striking: 'ahav (H157, love) is used mutually. Wisdom does not merely reward or recognize her seekers — she loves them. The parallel meshachamai yimtza'uni — “those who seek me earnestly will find me” — uses shachar (to seek at dawn, to seek diligently, H7836), the same verb that the seductress in 7:15 used to describe her diligent search for the young man. Wisdom claims back the vocabulary of diligent seeking for herself.
8.18: 'osher ve-khavod 'itti — “wealth and honor are with me.” Wisdom’s material claims are not ethereal: she offers 'osher (wealth, H6239) and kavod (honor/glory, H3519) as real goods that her presence brings. Hon 'atiq ve-tzedaqah — “enduring riches and righteousness”: 'atiq (H6276, enduring, ancient) qualifies the wealth as lasting rather than transient. The combination of material wealth and righteousness in wisdom’s offer is the OT’s consistent portrait of the flourishing life.
8.19: tov peri 'mitzahav ufaz — “my fruit is better than gold, even fine gold.” Paz (H6337, fine gold, refined gold) is the highest quality of gold in the OT vocabulary. The metaphor of wisdom as a tree bearing fruit was established in 3:18 (“she is a tree of life to those who grasp her”); here the fruit claim is made comparatively: wisdom’s fruit exceeds the highest material wealth. The MKT’s translation “my fruit” captures the organic growth-metaphor of wisdom’s productive output.
8.20: be-'orach tzedaqah 'ahalekh — “in the path of righteousness I walk.” Wisdom personalizes the path-metaphor: she herself walks the righteous path, which is why following her constitutes walking it. Derakhei mishpat — “in the ways of justice” — the MKT renders mishpat (H4941, justice, just ordinance) as its legal-relational meaning: the structuring of reality according to right relationship. Wisdom’s path is not merely good advice but the alignment of one’s life with the moral order of creation.
8.21: le-hanchil 'ohavai yesh — “giving a real inheritance to those who love me.” Yesh (H3426) means ‘substance, that which is’ — the concrete reality of existence as opposed to illusion or vanity. Wisdom’s gift to those who love her is substantial existence: 'otzaroteihem 'amalle' — “and filling their storehouses.” The image of filled treasuries as wisdom’s result is the OT’s way of saying that wisdom produces a life of genuine abundance, not the false abundance promised by the adulteress.
8.23: me-'olam nissakhti me-rosh — “I was set up from of old, from the beginning.” Nassakh (H5258 in Niphal = to be poured, established, installed) may draw on the imagery of a king’s installation; the parallel me-rosh (from the beginning) and the preceding verse’s claim to pre-creational existence make this the formal claim to eternal appointment. Before the first act of creation, wisdom was installed in her office. The parallel with Ps 2:6 (“I have installed my king on Zion”) suggests a royal installation metaphor.
8.24: be-ein tehomot cholalti — “when there were no depths, I was brought forth.” Cholal (H2342 Polal = to be brought forth, to writhe in labor — a birthing verb) introduces the birth metaphor that dominates vv24-25: wisdom is not merely appointed (v23) but born. Tehomot (the deep waters, H8415 — the term of Gen 1:2’s tehom) are absent at wisdom’s birth; the primordial ocean has not yet been created when wisdom comes into being. The absence of springs (niv'ei maim) and the unformed mountains confirm this pre-creational setting.
8.25: lifnei gevaot chollalti — “before the hills, I was brought forth.” The birth-verb cholal repeats from v24, framing vv24-25 as a birth-diptych. Gevaot (hills, H1389) before they existed: the mountain landscape of the earth is post-wisdom in terms of temporal priority. The hills that are among the most ancient features of the physical world are themselves newer than wisdom’s coming-into-being.
8.26: 'ad lo' 'asah 'eretz ve-chutzot — “before he had made the earth and the fields.” The verse catalogs what had not yet been made at wisdom’s appearance: the earth ('eretz), the open fields (chutzot), and the beginning of the dust of the world (ro'sh 'afrot tevel). Tevel (H8398) is the inhabited world, the productive earth — its first dust is described as younger than wisdom. The comprehensiveness of the pre-creational claim reaches its fullest expression in this verse.
8.27: be-hakino shamayim sham 'ani — “when he established the heavens, I was there.” The eyewitness claim (“I was there”) sets wisdom as the observer-participant of creation. Chug (H2329, circle/vault — the circle drawn on the face of the deep) is the term for the vault of the sky; wisdom watches the creative act of inscribing the cosmic circle. The combination of “was there” and the active watching establishes wisdom as not merely pre-existent but present-at-creation.
8.28: be-'ammetz shechaqim mima'al — “when he made the skies firm above.” 'Ametz (H553, to make strong, confirm) applied to the clouds above establishes the structural stability of the atmosphere; 'ain 'ain tehom — “when the fountains of the deep were fixed.” The tehom of v24 reappears: the deep that did not exist when wisdom was born is now being organized and fixed. Wisdom witnesses the taming of the primordial waters.
8.29: be-sumu la-yam chuqo — “when he set a boundary for the sea.” Choq (H2706, the fixed boundary, the statute/decree) applied to the sea is the decree-metaphor for the order imposed on chaos: the waters must not transgress their boundary (pi lo' ya'avru — “that the waters should not transgress his command”). Be-chuqqo mosedei 'aretz — “when he marked out the foundations of the earth.” The same decree-term (choqo) governs both sea and earth-foundation: creation is ordered by statutory decree.
8.30: 'ehyeh 'etzlo 'amon — “I was beside him, the master craftsman.” 'Amon (H525) is the most debated term in Proverbs 8. The two primary options are: (a) 'amon as craftsman/artisan/skilled worker (cognate with Akkadian ummanu, ‘craftsman’; cf. Jer 52:15 use of the related 'aman); (b) 'amon as ward/nursling, the little child being reared (cf. Num 11:12; the MT pointing would require 'amun for this reading). The MKT adopts ‘master craftsman’ as fitting the active participatory role in creation. Va-'ehyeh sha'ashu'im yom yom — “I was his daily delight”: sha'ashu'im (H8191, delights/joys) is a plural of intensity; wisdom is what delights YHWH in the act of creation.
8.31: mesacheqet be-tevel 'artzo — “rejoicing in the world he had made.” Sachaq (to play, rejoice, dance — used of children at play and of David dancing before the ark in 2 Sam 6:21) describes wisdom’s disposition at creation as joyful delight in the work. Ve-sha'ashu'ai 'et bene 'adam — “my delight was in the children of humanity.” The final clause shifts wisdom’s delight from the creation (tevel) to the creature (bene 'adam): the world delights wisdom, but the human beings in it are the specific object of her delight. This is the wisdom tradition’s most direct statement of divine love for humanity as such.
8.32: ve-'ata banim shim'u-li — “and now, sons, listen to me.” The transition from the cosmic Wisdom-poem (vv22-31) back to direct address is abrupt and deliberate: the eternal and cosmic character of wisdom makes the father’s instruction more urgent, not less. The word 'ashrei (blessed, H835 — the same term that opens the Psalter in Ps 1:1) appears twice in vv32-34, framing the invitation to wisdom as access to the blessed life that the Psalter describes.
8.33: shim'u musar ve-chakemu — “hear instruction and be wise.” Musar (H4148, discipline, instruction through correction) is the term for the formative, often painful, process of wisdom acquisition. Ve-'al-tifrau' — “and do not reject it.” The negative command (para', H6544, to let loose, to neglect, to become unruly) frames wisdom-rejection as a loosening of the formation that wisdom provides — the wisdom tradition’s anthropology of instruction as constraint that liberates.
8.34: 'ashrei 'adam shome'a li — “blessed is the man who listens to me.” The second 'ashrei clause specifies the practice: daily attendance at wisdom’s doors (meshoqed 'al-daltotai yom yom, “watching daily at my doors”). Meshaqed (H8245, to watch, to keep vigil, to be sleepless) is the intensity-verb of diligent waiting; wisdom’s blessing is given to those who wait at her threshold every day. The image of wisdom’s house with gates and doorposts anticipates ch 9’s Wisdom-house with its seven pillars.
8.35: ki motz'i matza' chayyim — “for the one who finds me finds life.” Motze'i (to find, to encounter) and chayyim (life, H2416) form the central equation of Proverbs’ wisdom theology: wisdom = life. Va-yafoq ratzon mei-YHWH — “and obtains favor from the LORD”: ratzon (H7522, acceptance, favor, will) from YHWH is the theological goal toward which wisdom mediates. Finding wisdom is finding life; finding life is finding YHWH’s favor. The three terms (wisdom, life, divine favor) are presented as a single package.
8.36: ve-chot'i chomes nafsho — “but he who misses me harms himself.” Chata' (H2398) is typically ‘to sin’ but here means ‘to miss, to fail to hit the target’ — the concrete, pre-theological meaning of the sin vocabulary. The one who misses wisdom does violence (chamas, H2554) to his own life (nafsho). Kol-mesana'ai 'ahavu mavet — “all those who hate me love death.” The antithesis of the love-and-find-life equation (v35): hatred of wisdom is equivalent to love of death. The stark binary of wisdom/life versus folly/death is the pervasive structure of Proverbs’ moral universe.

1.1: The book opens with the standard Solomonic attribution formula: “proverbs of Solomon (miš‘lê Šᵉlômôh), son of David, king of Israel.” The Hebrew māšāl (rendered “proverb”) is a broader term than the English suggests — it encompasses short aphorisms, extended discourses, riddles, and taunt songs. The book’s title thus signals a collection of diverse wisdom forms united by Solomonic sponsorship. The lineage “son of David” ties wisdom to the Davidic covenant tradition (1 Kgs 3:3–14; 4:29–34).
1.2: Verses 2–6 constitute the prologue: a purpose statement using a series of infinitive constructs. “Learning (lādaʿat) wisdom (ḥokmāh) and discipline (mûsār)” — the two terms are frequently paired in Proverbs. Ḥokmāh denotes the practical skill of living well within divine order; mûsār carries the sense of corrective training or discipline, not merely instruction. The MKT renders it “discipline” consistently to preserve the formative, sometimes painful, quality of the process (cf. 3:11–12; Heb 12:5).
1.3: “Wise conduct (haśkêl)” translates haśkîl, the hiphil infinitive of śākal — to act prudently, prosper through skillful behavior. The triad “righteousness (ṣedeq), justice (mišpāṭ), and integrity (mêšārîm)” appears together as the standard of covenant social order (cf. Deut 16:18–20; Ps 99:4). The book’s opening thus grounds practical wisdom in covenant ethics.
1.4: “The naive (petaʿîm)” are those who are not yet hardened in foolishness — the young, the untested, the open (the root pth means “open, wide”; susceptible in both directions). “Shrewdness (ʿormāh)” is savvy discernment — the ability to see through situations — a virtue the serpent possesses (Gen 3:1) but here redeemed by being channeled through wisdom. “Knowledge (daʿat) and discernment (mᵉzimmāh)” round out the gift to youth.
1.5: The prologue turns from the young to the already-wise: wisdom is not only for the naive but for those who already possess it. “Skill in guidance (taḥbulôt)” — the nautical metaphor of ropes and steering (from ḥōbel, rope/cable) — refers to the ability to navigate complex situations and provide strategic counsel. It appears in 11:14; 20:18; 24:6 in military and leadership contexts.
1.6: “Enigmas (ḥîdôt)” are riddles, puzzles — the same word used of Samson’s riddle (Judg 14:12) and the Queen of Sheba’s “hard questions” (1 Kgs 10:1). Proverbs itself is a collection of compressed wisdom where meaning is not always transparent; understanding proverbs’ “hidden layer” is part of the goal. The wise person can move from the surface form to the deeper application.
1.7: The programmatic thesis of Proverbs and of the broader wisdom tradition: yirʾat-YHWH rêʾšît daʾat — “fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge.” The term yirʾāh (fear) in the wisdom books is not mere terror but the foundational disposition of the covenant person: reverence, trust, and submission that keeps life properly oriented. Rêʾšît means both “beginning” (temporal first) and “first principle” (qualitative foundation) — wisdom does not start from neutral reason but from covenant relation. “Fools (ʾewîlîm) despise wisdom and discipline” — the fool is not the ignorant but the willfully rejecting person.
1.8: The main body of chapters 1–9 begins here with the first “my son” address — bᵉnî is the standard didactic address of the wisdom teacher (occurs 15 times in chs. 1–9). The dual instruction from “father” (ʾābîkā) and “mother” (ʾimmekā) is notable: both parents’ teaching carries authority (cf. 6:20; 31:1–9). The word for “teaching” here is tôrāh — which can mean instruction broadly or the Torah specifically; the context is parental formation, not yet Mosaic code, though the verbal resonance with Deuteronomy is intentional.
1.9: The rewards of parental wisdom are expressed as jewelry and garland — external adornment marking the person who has internalized the teaching. “A graceful garland (liwyat ḥēn)” on the head and “a chain of honor (ʿᵃnāqôt)” around the neck. The image recurs in 4:9 and parallels the garland of honor bestowed on a student by wisdom herself. In a world where social standing was partly expressed through dress and ornament, wisdom’s adornment marks its bearer as honored.
1.10: The first temptation scene begins — the father warns against the seduction of ḥaṭṭāʾîm (sinners, those who miss the mark) who try to “entice” (pittîkā, hiphil of pth — the same root as “naive/open” in v4). The condensed command “do not yield (ʾal-tōʾbeh)” — the jussive negative — draws on the authority of the father’s voice to create a decisive moment.
1.11: The sinners’ speech begins (vv11–14) — remarkably, the teacher gives voice to the temptation so the student can recognize its shape. The ambush imagery — “lie in wait (nᵉʾorbāh)” and “ambush (niṣpᵉnāh)” — recalls the language of warfare and predation (cf. Ps 10:8–9). The phrase “for no reason (ḥinnām)” echoes the description of the Satan’s persecution of Job: “without reason” (Job 1:9; 2:3).
1.12: “Swallow them alive like Sheol” — the sinners invoke the underworld image: Sheol as the insatiable gullet that swallows whole (cf. Num 16:30–33; Hab 2:5). The comparison “whole (tᵉmîmîm) like those who descend to the grave (bôr)” uses tāmîm (complete, whole — the same word as “blameless” for Noah and Abraham), ironically: they will swallow the innocent whole, as Sheol swallows the dead.
1.13: The appeal to material gain — “every kind of treasure (hôn yāqār)” and “loot (šālāl).” Šālāl is the technical term for war plunder or spoil; the violence implied is military-scale predation on the innocent. The fantasy of easy riches through violence is the trap’s bait.
1.14: “Throw in your lot (gôrālᵉkā)” — a technical term; the gôrāl was the lot cast to divide property or assign duties (cf. Josh 18:6; Prov 16:33). “A common purse (kîs ʾeḥād)” — shared funds, suggesting gang solidarity. The appeal is to belonging and communal gain.
1.15: The father’s counter-command uses the journey metaphor central to Proverbs: “their road (derek) ... their path (nātîb).” Two different words for path — derek (highway, the main way) and nātîb (bypath, side trail). The father urges separation from the way entirely: “keep your feet far (mᵉnaʿ ...)” — do not even get close.
1.16: The rationale for avoidance: their feet “run to evil (rāṣāh lāraʿ)” and “rush (ûyᵉmaharû) to shed blood.” The double speed — run, rush — marks the eagerness of the wicked for violence. This verse is partially quoted in Isa 59:7 and Rom 3:15, where it describes universal human sin.
1.17: An intrusive proverbial aside: “How futile (kî-ḥinnām) to spread a net in full view (bᵉʿênê = in the eyes of) of every bird (kol-baʿal kānāp = every master of a wing).” The crux is interpretive — two main readings: (a) Even a bird knows to avoid a visible net, but sinners are stupider than birds; (b) the sinners’ ambush is as futile as a visible net — the prey (God, justice) sees it. The MKT follows reading (a), with v18–19 confirming: the sinners are the bird that walks into the net they themselves set.
1.18: The reversal: “they lie in wait for their own blood (dāmām)” — the same word used of innocent blood in v11. The sinners who planned to shed blood end up shedding their own. The ambush turns back on the ambushers — the poetic justice that Proverbs often exploits.
1.19: The moralizing summary: “everyone who is greedy for unjust gain (bōṣēaʿ bāṣaʿ)” — bāṣaʿ (gain, profit, cut off) is consistently negative in wisdom literature; it denotes predatory profit at another’s expense (cf. 28:16; Isa 56:11; Jer 6:13). “It costs them their lives (nepeš bᵉʿālāyw yiqqāḥ)” — literally “takes the life of its owners.” Nepeš here is the full self, not merely breath.
1.20: Wisdom (Ḥokmôt) now appears as a personified woman crying in public (vv20–33). Ḥokmôt is a plural form of ḥokmāh — possibly an intensive (“Wisdom itself”) or reflecting an older plural-of-majesty usage. Her cry in “the streets (bāḥûṣ)” and “public squares (bārrᵉḥōbôt)” — the busiest places — contrasts with the hidden ambush of the sinners (v11). Wisdom is not secret but public and insistent.
1.21: “At the head of the busy thoroughfares (bᵉrōʾš hōmiyyôt)” — literally “at the head of the noisy streets.” “At the gates of the city (šaʿărê qārāʾ’)” — the gate was the city’s judicial and commercial center, the place where all civic life was transacted (cf. Ruth 4:1; Amos 5:10–15). Wisdom speaks where power is exercised and decisions are made.
1.22: Wisdom’s opening question addresses three types: “the naive (pᵉtāyîm)” (open to wisdom or to folly — morally undetermined), “scoffers (lēṣîm)” (the intensely mocking, actively hostile to wisdom — a key Proverbs type), and “fools (kᵉsîlîm)” (the thick-skulled, the complacent). The three represent a spectrum from potentially reachable to probably hardened.
1.23: “Turn back at my rebuke (tāšûbû lᵉtôkaḥtî)” — šûb (turn, return) is the standard repentance word; wisdom uses it. The promise: “I will pour out (ʾabbîʿāh) my spirit (rûḥî) to you.” Nābîʾ (the root of ʾabbîʿāh) means to spring up, bubble over (cf. Ps 19:2; 119:171). The outpouring of wisdom’s rûaḥ echoes later prophetic promises of the divine Spirit (Isa 44:3; Ezek 36:27; Joel 2:28).
1.24: The hinge verse — Wisdom’s call was rejected (past tense in Hebrew): “I called (qārāʾtî)” and “I stretched out my hand (nāṭîtî yādî).” The outstretched hand is the gesture of divine invitation and prophetic summons (Isa 65:2; 66:4). The rejection has already occurred; the consequences follow.
1.25: Three parallel rejections: “you rejected (tiprᵉʿû) all my advice,” “you would accept none of my correction (tôkaḥtî).” Pāraʿ (to let loose, reject, disregard) is used of Israel’s rejection of God in Exod 32:25; the moral recklessness of “letting go” of all restraint. The refusal of tôkaḥat (correction, reproof) is the defining marker of the fool throughout Proverbs (9:7–8; 12:1; 15:10).
1.26: Wisdom’s terrifying reversal: “I will laugh (ʾeśḥaq) at your calamity (bᵉʾêdkem); I will mock (ʾelʾag) when terror (paḥadkem) strikes.” God himself is said to laugh at the wicked in Ps 2:4; 37:13; 59:8 — the sovereign irony of the one rejected. The terms ʾêd (calamity, ruin) and paḥad (sudden terror) appear again in the disaster lists of Job and the prophets.
1.27: The disaster imagery — “like a storm (kᵉsûpāh)” and “like a whirlwind (sᵉʿārāh)” — is standard prophetic language for divine judgment (Isa 29:6; Nah 1:3; Job 38:1). The pairing of ṣārāh (distress, trouble — the standard affliction word) and ṣûqāh (anguish, straits) underscores the total encompassing of the disaster.
1.28: The reversal of v23: “then they will call (yiqrāʾûnnî) on me but I will not answer.” The same verb as wisdom’s call in v24 — now reversed: the rejectors call, but wisdom does not answer. The motif of unanswered prayer in judgment contexts appears in Mic 3:4; Jer 11:11–12; Lam 3:44.
1.29: The reason given for wisdom’s withdrawal: “they hated (śānᵉʾû) knowledge (daʿat) and did not choose (lōʾ bāḥărû) to fear the LORD.” Bāḥar (to choose, elect) is used of God’s election of Israel (Deut 7:6) and of human moral choice (Deut 30:19). Rejecting wisdom’s instruction is characterized as an act of will, not ignorance.
1.30: “They wanted none of my advice (lōʾ-ʾābû lᵉʿᵉṣātî)” — the verb ʾābāh (to be willing, to consent) appears in Deuteronomy’s covenant lawsuit texts (Deut 1:26; 9:23; Isa 1:19–20). The rejection is one of consent, not capacity — they were capable of choosing but refused.
1.31: The retributive principle expressed as eating: “they will eat the fruit of their own path (yōkᵉlû mippᵉrî darkām).” The derek (way, path) produces fruit — the moral-agricultural metaphor connects actions to consequences organically (cf. Gal 6:7–8). “Gorged on their own schemes (mimoʿaṣôtêhem yiśbāʾû)” — śābēaʿ (to be sated, filled to excess) captures the bitter irony of getting exactly what they planned.
1.32: “The waywardness (mᵉšûbāh) of the naive will kill them” — mᵉšûbāh is the noun form of šûb (to turn), meaning “turning away, apostasy, waywardness” — the same root as repentance but in the opposite direction (cf. Jer 2:19; Hos 14:4). “The complacency (šalwāh) of fools” — šalwāh means ease, prosperity, false security — the fool’s fatal contentment with their current state.
1.33: The closing promise seals the speech: “whoever listens to me will live in security (yiškon-beṭaḥ).” Bāṭaḥ (to be secure, trust) is a covenantal security word: the land promised to the patriarchs is to be inhabited “in security” (Lev 26:5; Deut 12:10; Jer 23:6). “Free from the dread (paḥad) of harm (rāʿāh)” — the paḥad that fell on fools (v26) is absent for wisdom’s followers. Security is the fruit of listening, not the default condition.

2.1: Chapter 2 is a single extended sentence in Hebrew — the longest in Proverbs — with a conditional structure: if (vv1–4) → then (vv5–11) → to deliver from (vv12–19) → so that (vv20–22). “Accept (tiqqaḥ) my words” and “store up (tiṣpōn) my commands” — ṣāpan (to hide, store, treasure) indicates active hoarding of the teaching, not passive reception (cf. 7:1; Ps 119:11).
2.2: “Turning your ear (haqšîb ʾoznᵉkā)” — the hiphil of qāšab is to give careful, attentive listening (cf. 4:20; Ps 10:17). “Directing your heart (tаṭṭeh libbᵉkā)” — the heart (lēb) in Hebrew anthropology is the seat of will, understanding, and decision — not merely emotion. The ear hears; the heart internalizes and acts.
2.3: The imperative “call out (tiqrāʾ)” for discernment and “cry aloud (titten qôlekā) for understanding” — the same verbs used of wisdom’s public call in 1:20–21. Now the student is urged to call for wisdom as wisdom called to the student. The symmetry is deliberate: wisdom cries in the street; the seeker must cry back in prayer.
2.4: The treasure-hunting similes: “search for it like silver (kakkᵉsep)” and “dig for it like buried treasure (kammᵃṭmônîm).” Silver was the primary monetary metal in the ANE; buried treasure was found through persistent searching. The two images escalate: seeking as commerce → seeking as excavation. The same silver-seeker image recurs in 3:14 and 8:10–11 where wisdom’s value exceeds silver.
2.5: The first “then (ʾāz)” clause: understanding of yirʾat-YHWH (fear of the LORD — the same phrase as 1:7) and daʿat ʾĕlōhîm (knowledge of God) are the paired result of the diligent search. The two expressions are near-synonyms here: both name the foundational covenant relationship. “Knowledge of God” (daʿat ʾĕlōhîm) is a key prophetic phrase (Hos 4:1–2; 6:6) — not just information about God but relational knowing through covenant faithfulness.
2.6: The theological underpinning of the search: “the LORD gives wisdom (yitten ḥokmāh).” Despite the vigorous human seeking of vv1–4, wisdom is ultimately divine gift, not human achievement (cf. 1 Kgs 3:12; Job 28:23–28). “From his mouth (mippîhû) flow knowledge and understanding” — wisdom is the speech of God, his verbal self-disclosure (cf. 8:22–31).
2.7: “He stores up (yiṣpōn) proven wisdom (tûšîyyāh) for the upright (layyᵉšārîm).” Tûšîyyāh is a difficult wisdom term: “sound wisdom, effectiveness, abiding success” — the real working out of wisdom in life (cf. 3:21; 8:14; Job 5:12; 6:13). The same root ṣāpan (to store/hide) used of the seeker in v1 is now used of God — both seek and store. “Shield (māgēn)” for the upright — God’s protection is expressed in military imagery (cf. Gen 15:1; Ps 3:3).
2.8: “He guards (nōṣēr) the paths of justice” — the participle form suggests ongoing action. Nāṣar (to guard, watch, keep) is the same root as the divine attribute “keeping steadfast love” (Exod 34:7) and the word for the “keeper” in Ps 121. “Those who are faithful (ḥᵃsîdāyw) to him” — the ḥāsîd (covenant-loyal one, “godly”) are those who embody ḥesed in response to God’s ḥesed.
2.9: The second “then” clause: “you will understand what is right (ṣedeq) and just (mišpāṭ) and fair (mêšārîm) — every good way of living (kol-maʿgal-ṭôb).” The three-term ethical triad of 1:3 recurs, now as the consequence of pursuing wisdom. The “way of living” (maʿgal = the track made by a wheel, a well-worn path) appears again in 4:11; 5:6–21.
2.10: “Wisdom will enter (tābôʾ) your heart (bᵉlibbᵉkā)” — the movement is from the outside (teaching, speech) to the inside (heart, will). “Knowledge (daʿat) will delight (yinʿam) your inner being (napsᵉkā).” Nāʿēm (to be pleasant, delightful) — wisdom becomes not an imposed duty but an internalized delight; the word recurs in 9:17 and Ps 133:1.
2.11: “Discretion (mᵉzimmāh) will watch over you; understanding (tᵉbûnāh) will protect you.” Mᵉzimmāh (discretion, strategic thinking — the ability to plan wisely; cf. 1:4; 5:2) and tᵉbûnāh (understanding as the faculty that perceives underlying patterns) are now personified as guardian figures. The wisdom that was sought becomes the protector of the seeker.
2.12: “Delivering you (lᵉhaṣṣîlᵉkā) from the path of evil (derek rāʿ)” — nāṣal (hiphil: to rescue, deliver) is a deliverance verb (cf. Exod 3:8; 18:4; Ps 18:17). “From those who speak twisted things (miʾîš mᵉdabbēr tahpukôt)” — tahpukôt (perversions, inversions, upside-down speech) names speech that reverses the moral order (cf. 6:14; 8:13; Deut 32:20).
2.13: “Who abandon the straight paths (ʾorḥôt yōšer) and choose to walk in darkness.” Yōšer (straightness, uprightness) and darkness are the standard two-way framework of Proverbs: the way of wisdom is light and straightness; the way of folly is crooked and dark. The choice to walk in darkness is a volitional act — “choose (lāleket)” — not an accident.
2.14: “Who take pleasure (śᵉmēḥîm) in evil (laʿᵃśôt rāʿ) and celebrate (yāgîlû) every kind of wrongdoing.” The intensification here is crucial: these are not reluctant sinners but those who find joy in evil. The same vocabulary of rejoicing (śimḥāh, gîl) that describes worship of God is here applied to evil — a perverse inversion of the proper orientation of delight.
2.15: “Their ways (ʾorḥōtêhem) are crooked (ʿiqqᵉšîm) and their paths (nᵉtîbôtām) are twisted (nᵉlôzîm).” Three different Hebrew words for path are used in this section — derek, maʿgal, ʾōraḥ, nātîb — Proverbs’ path vocabulary is remarkably rich. ʿIqqēš (crooked, perverse — from a root meaning to twist) and lûz (to turn aside, deviate) both describe moral deviation from the straight path.
2.16: The second deliverer: wisdom saves from “the immoral woman (ʾiššāh zārāh) — the seductive stranger (nāk­rîyyāh) with smooth words (ʾᵃmārêhā heḥᵉlîqāh).” Zārāh (strange, foreign) and nokrîyyāh (foreign woman, outsider) may describe a literal foreign woman, a cult prostitute, or simply the adulteress who has placed herself outside the covenant community. The moral category of “outside” is more important than ethnicity. Ḥālaq (smooth) describes the flattering, slippery speech of seduction (cf. 5:3; 6:24; Ps 55:21).
2.17: “She has abandoned (ʿāzᵉbāh) the partner (ʾallûp) of her youth (nᵉʿûrêhā)” — ʾallûp (intimate companion, close friend) is a rare but intense term for a covenant partner. “The covenant she made before God (bᵉrît ʾĕlōhêhā)” — this is almost certainly the marriage covenant witnessed by God (cf. Mal 2:14 where marriage is explicitly called a bᵉrît with YHWH as witness). The woman’s sin is not merely sexual but covenantal.
2.18: “Her house leads down to death (mōwet); her paths go to the spirits of the dead (rᵉpāʾîm).” Rᵉpāʾîm are the shades of the dead, the weak and powerless inhabitants of Sheol (cf. Isa 14:9; 26:14; Ps 88:10). The adulteress’s house is a gateway to Sheol — the path of sexual transgression ends in the underworld. The contrast with wisdom (2:20–22: land, dwelling, life) is stark.
2.19: “None who enter her house return (lōʾ yᵉšûbûn); they never find their way back to the paths of life (ʾorḥôt ḥayyîm).” The finality is emphatic — there is no return from this path. The contrast with v12–15 is precise: those paths are twisted and crooked; the paths of life are the alternative wisdom provides. The irreversibility echoes Sheol imagery: those who descend do not return (Job 7:9; 10:21).
2.20: “All this so that (lᵉmaʿan) you will walk in the path of the good (derek ṭôbîm)” — the purpose clause gathers the entire chapter’s protection toward a positive end. The double “path of the good” and “road of the righteous (ʾorḥôt ṣaddîqîm)” forms a positive endpoint that mirrors the book’s opening concern (1:3).
2.21: “The righteous (yᵉšārîm) will live in the land (yišᵉkᵉnû-ʾāreṣ)” — the land-inheritance promise of Deuteronomy (Deut 11:8–9; Ps 37:9–11, 29) applied to personal wisdom. “Those of integrity (tᵉmîmîm) will remain (yiwwātᵉrû) in it” — tāmîm (complete, blameless, whole) is the covenant-integrity term (Gen 6:9 for Noah; Gen 17:1 for Abraham). Land-inheritance is the covenant outcome of righteous living.
2.22: The counterpart: “the wicked (rᵉšāʿîm) will be cut off (yikkārētû) from the land, and the unfaithful (bôgᵉdîm) will be uprooted (yissᵉḥû) from it.” Kārat (cut off) is a covenantal punishment term — “cut off from the people” appears throughout Leviticus for covenant violations (Lev 7:20–21; 18:29). Bôgēd (the treacherous, the faithless covenant-breaker) provides the ethical antonym to tāmîm. The chapter ends with creation-level consequences.

3.1: Chapter 3 returns to the direct “my son (bᵉnî)” address (cf. 1:8; 2:1). “My instruction (tôrātî)” — tôrāh here is parental teaching, but the word is the same as the Mosaic Torah; the wisdom teacher’s instruction participates in the same authority structure. “Let your heart hold on (yiṣṣōr) to my commands” — nāṣar (to guard, keep carefully) is the same verb used of God’s keeping steadfast love (Exod 34:7) and the Psalmist keeping the law (Ps 119:2).
3.2: “They will add to you a long life (ʾōrek yāmîm), many years of living (šᵉnôt ḥayyîm), and peace (šālôm).” Long life and peace are the standard covenant-blessing terms: ʾōrek yāmîm appears in the Aaronic blessing-promise context (Ps 21:4; 23:6); šālôm is the comprehensive covenant wellbeing (completeness, flourishing, not just absence of conflict). These are the outcomes of wisdom that motivate the son to listen.
3.3: “Let steadfast love (ḥesed) and faithfulness (ʾĕmet) never leave you.” The paired covenant attributes ḥesed wĕʾĕmet are the core description of YHWH’s character at Sinai (Exod 34:6–7) and appear throughout the Psalms (Ps 25:10; 40:10–11; 85:10). The student is called to embody the divine character. “Wear them like a necklace (qašrēm ʾal-gargᵉrōtekā)” — bind them on your throat (cf. 1:9; 6:21; Deut 6:8). “Inscribe them on your heart (kātᵉbēm ʾal-lûaḥ libbekā)” — the language of Deut 6:6 (“these words... shall be on your heart”) and Jer 31:33 (the new covenant written on hearts).
3.4: “You will find favor (ḥēn) and a good reputation (śēkel-ṭôb) in the eyes of God and people (bᵉʿênê ʾĕlōhîm wᵉʾādām).” The dual approval — divine and human — recurs in describing Joseph (Gen 39:3–4, 21), Samuel (1 Sam 2:26), and Jesus (Luke 2:52). Śēkel (prudence, good sense, insight) is the practical wisdom that makes one well-regarded (cf. 1 Sam 25:3).
3.5: “Trust in the LORD (beṭaḥ ʾel-YHWH) with all your heart (bᵉkol-libbekā).” One of the most cited verses in Proverbs. Bāṭaḥ (to trust, be secure) is a covenantal security term (cf. 1:33; Ps 22:4–5; Isa 26:3–4). “Do not depend on your own insight (ʾal-tiššāʿēn ʾel-bînātekā)” — šāʿan (to lean on, rely on) occurs with negative force in Isa 31:1 (“who rely on horses”) and Ezek 29:7. The contrast is not between reason and faith but between self-sufficient reasoning and God-dependent wisdom.
3.6: “Acknowledge him (dāʿēhû) in everything you do (bᵉkol-dᵉrākekā), and he will direct your steps (yᵉyaššēr ʾorḥōtekā).” Yādaʾ (to know) here is covenantal knowing — to acknowledge, reckon with, give full weight to (cf. 1 Sam 2:12; Hos 4:1). The promise is that God will “make straight (yāšar, piel)” the paths — removing obstacles, straightening the crooked way.
3.7: “Do not think of yourself as wise in your own eyes (ʾal-tᵉhî ḥākām bᵉʿênekā).” The precise phrasing recurs in 26:12 (“do you see a person wise in their own eyes? There is more hope for a fool”) and Isa 5:21 (“woe to those who are wise in their own eyes”). Self-assessed wisdom is the root of arrogant folly. “Fear the LORD and turn from evil” — the same pairing as the programmatic verse (1:7 + 3:7b) grounds the ethical turn in covenant piety.
3.8: “This will bring health (ripʾût) to your whole body (lᵉšârekkā) and strength (šiqwî) to your bones.” Šōr (navel, or possibly the body’s whole frame — the word is rare) is sometimes translated “navel” or “body.” Šiqwî is also rare — “refreshment, moisture” — suggesting the bones being moistened rather than dry (cf. Prov 17:22; Job 21:24). The physical dimension of wisdom’s blessings is consistent throughout Proverbs: wisdom is embodied.
3.9: “Honor the LORD with your wealth (hôn)” and “the first portion (rēʾšît) of all you produce.” Rēʾšît (firstfruits, best portion — the same word as “beginning” in 1:7) links wisdom’s foundation to the concrete act of giving God the first and best of one’s produce. The firstfruits legislation (Exod 23:19; Deut 26:1–11) is here applied to income broadly: the principle generalizes beyond agriculture.
3.10: “Your barns (ʾᵃsāmekā) will overflow with abundance (šābāʿ) and your wine vats (yᵉqābêkā) will brim with new wine (tîrôš).” The agricultural blessing-language of Deuteronomy (Deut 28:8; Mal 3:10) — full storehouses and overflowing wine vats as the material expression of divine favor. Tîrôš is the fresh-pressed grape juice / must, one of the grain-wine-oil triad of covenant prosperity.
3.11: “Do not reject (ʾal-timʾas) the LORD’s discipline (mûsar-YHWH)” and “do not resent (wᵉʾal-tāqōṣ) his correction (tôkaḥtô).” The strong imperatives guard against two reactions to hardship: rejecting (treating as contemptible) and resenting (feeling weary and disgusted — qûṣ means to feel loathing, cf. Gen 27:46; Exod 1:12). Discipline and correction are not signs of divine rejection but of divine engagement.
3.12: “For the LORD corrects (yôkîaḥ) those he loves (yᵉʾāhab), just as a father disciplines the son he takes delight in (yirṣeh).” Yakach (hiphil: to reprove, correct, argue a case) carries a forensic dimension — it is the word for presenting evidence and making one accountable (cf. Job 9:33; 16:21; Isa 2:4). God’s correction is the action of a parent who takes pleasure (rāṣāh) in the son’s flourishing. This verse is directly quoted in Heb 12:6.
3.13: “How happy (ʾašrê) is the person who finds wisdom (māṣāʾ ḥokmāh), the one who gains understanding (yāpîq tᵉbûnāh).” The ʾašrê (beatitude) formula — “O the happiness of the one who...” — is used extensively in Psalms (Ps 1:1; 2:12; 34:8) and opens the Sermon on the Mount (Matt 5:3–11). It is exclamatory, not conditional — declaring the blessed state of the one who already possesses wisdom.
3.14: “Her return (sāḥrāh) is better than the return on silver (kesef), and her profit (tᵉbûʾātāh) better than fine gold (ḥārûṣ).” Sāḥar is trading, commerce — wisdom measured as a return on investment. Ḥārûṣ (fine, mined gold) is the most refined and valuable metal, the standard of ultimate value (cf. Ps 19:10; Prov 8:10–11). The merchant-language deliberately transposes wisdom into economic categories to communicate her surpassing value.
3.15: “She is more valuable than precious gems (mippᵉnînîm).” Pᵉnînîm (probably rubies or corals — the exact stone is debated) represents the most precious of luxury goods. “Nothing you could want (kol-ḥᵃpāṣekā) measures up to her.” Ḥāpēṣ (to delight in, desire) — the strongest human wanting. The verse recurs almost verbatim in 8:11 in Wisdom’s self-speech.
3.16: “In her right hand is long life (ʾōrek yāmîm); in her left hand are wealth (ʿōšer) and honor (kābôd).” The two-handed image is a visual anthropomorphism: wisdom as a figure who holds gifts. The right hand held the greater gift in OT symbolism (Gen 48:14; Ps 110:1). Long life ranks above wealth and honor — wisdom’s hierarchy of values. All three are explicitly the gifts Solomon received for asking wisdom rather than wealth (1 Kgs 3:11–14).
3.17: “Her ways (dᵉrākêhā) are pleasant (dᵉrākê-nōʿam) and all her paths (nᵉtîbôtêhā) lead to peace (šālôm).” Nōʿam (pleasantness, loveliness, delightfulness — root of the name Naomi, Ruth 1:20) characterizes wisdom’s way as intrinsically good to travel. Šālôm (comprehensive wellbeing) is the destination, not just the absence of conflict. The paths of wisdom are categorically different from the twisted paths of the wicked (2:13–15).
3.18: “She is a tree of life (ʿēṣ ḥayyîm) to those who grasp her.” The ʿēṣ ḥayyîm (tree of life) recalls Gen 2:9; 3:22–24 — the source of immortality from which humanity was cut off after the fall. Proverbs applies the image to wisdom: she restores access to what sin forfeited. The tree of life recurs in 11:30; 13:12; 15:4 and finally in Rev 22:2. “Those who hold on to her (tōmᵉkêhā) are called blessed (mᵉʾuššār)” — the participle of the beatitude-root.
3.19: “By wisdom (bᵉḥokmāh) the LORD laid the foundation (yāsad) of the earth; by understanding (bîn) he set the heavens in place.” The cosmological grounding of wisdom: it was the instrument of creation (cf. 8:22–31; Job 28:23–27; Ps 104:24). The same three wisdom-terms — ḥokmāh, tᵉbûnāh, daʿat — appear in vv19–20 as the tools of creation, tying the student’s pursuit of wisdom to participation in the order built into creation itself.
3.20: “Through his knowledge (bᵉdaʿtô) the deep waters (tᵉhōmôt) opened up and the clouds let fall their dew (ʾᵃbāpîm yirᵃʿᵃpû-ṭāl).” Tᵉhôm (the deep, the primeval ocean) is the chaos-water of Gen 1:2 that God separated at creation. The “falling of dew” may allude to the second-day separation of waters and the natural water cycle. Creation is the domain of the wisdom the student is being invited to pursue.
3.21: “My son, keep these in view (ʾal-yālûzû mêʿênekā = do not let them escape from your eyes); hold on to sound wisdom (tûšîyyāh) and discernment (mᵉzimmāh).” Lûz (to turn aside, deviate — as in 2:15) is used negatively: do not let the teaching deviate from your sight. Tûšîyyāh (sound, effective wisdom — 2:7) and mᵉzimmāh (prudent planning — 2:11) return from ch2 to form a bracket.
3.22: “They will bring life (ḥayyîm) to your soul (napsᵉkā) and beauty (ḥēn) to your neck.” The same jewelry-and-garland imagery as 1:9; beauty to the neck denotes social honor. Nepeš (throat, soul, self) is where life is centered; wisdom gives life to the whole person.
3.23: “Then you will go on your way (derkᵉkā) in security (lābeṭaḥ), without stumbling (wᵉraglᵉkā lōʾ-tāgôp).” The journey-security promise — the way (derek) is safe for the wisdom-keeper. Nāgap (to stumble, trip) is used of military defeat and stumbling over obstacles (Ps 91:12; Isa 8:14). The protected path is the opposite of the crooked paths of ch2.
3.24: “When you lie down (tiškeab), you will not be afraid (lōʾ-tîrāʾ); your sleep (šᵉnātekā) will be peaceful (wᵉʿārebāh).” Peaceful sleep as the sign of security appears also in Ps 4:8; Lev 26:6 (the covenant-blessing land where “you will lie down with no one to make you afraid”). ʿāreb (pleasant, sweet) — the taste of undisturbed rest that only comes with cleared conscience and divine protection.
3.25: “Do not fear sudden catastrophe (paḥad pittʾōm) or the destruction (šōʾāh) that falls on the wicked.” Paḥad pittʾōm (sudden dread) is the unexpected disaster that cannot be planned against (cf. Ps 91:5; Job 22:10). Šōʾāh (desolation, wasteland — from a root meaning to be devastated) is the most complete destruction-word in Hebrew, used of the cosmic judgment in Zeph 1:15. The righteous need not fear what overtakes the wicked.
3.26: “For the LORD will be your security (ûbᵉkîslᵉkā)” — literally “and in your side/hip will be the LORD” — kesil (hip, side, confidence; cf. Job 31:24 where it means “confidence”). “He will keep your foot (raglᵉkā) from every snare (milhāqed).” The LORD as the guardian of the foot is the language of Ps 121:3; 91:12. The snare (mōqēš variant: milhāqed = trap/net) is the enemy’s hidden trap.
3.27: “Do not withhold (ʾal-timnaʿ) good (ṭôb) from those who deserve it (mibbᵉʿālāyw = from its owners)” — baʿal (owner, master) applied to “good”: those to whom good-doing properly belongs as their due. The ethical demand now turns outward to neighbor ethics. “When you have the ability (bᵉhyôt lᵉʾēl yādᵉkā = when it is in the power of your hand)” — the qualification is not ability but will.
3.28: “Do not tell your neighbor, ‘Come back later — I’ll help you tomorrow,’ when you can help now (wᵉyēš-ʾittāk = and it is with you).” The deferral-of-good is its own form of withholding. The neighbor (rēaʿ) is the one with whom you share life and community. The command presupposes the capacity to help — the sin is procrastination, not poverty. Jas 2:15–16 applies this principle to the Christian community.
3.29: “Do not plot (tāḥarōš) harm (rāʿāh) against your neighbor, who lives beside you in trust (lābeṭaḥ).” Ḥāraš (to plow, devise, plot — Ps 10:2; Mic 2:1) — the agricultural metaphor of plowing transferred to the deliberate scheming of harm. “Who lives beside you in trust (bōṭēaḥ)” — the neighbor’s security (bāṭaḥ) is based on trust; plotting destroys the social fabric of the community built on that trust.
3.30: “Do not pick a fight (ʾal-tārîb) without cause (ḥinnām) with someone who has done you no harm (lōʾ-gᵉmālaʿkā rāʿāh).” Rîb (lawsuit, quarrel, legal dispute) — the legal register: do not open a case against someone without grounds. Ḥinnām (for nothing, without cause) — the gratuitous quality of the aggression. The repeated use of ḥinnām (also in 1:11) marks unmotivated harm as particularly condemned.
3.31: “Do not envy (qannēʾ) the violent person (ʾîš ḥāmās) or choose to follow any of his paths.” Qānāʾ (to be jealous, zealously desire — root of the divine epithet ʾēl qannāʾ, jealous God, Exod 20:5) — the covenantal emotion of jealousy / zeal turned toward the violent person’s apparent success. Ḥāmās (violence, injustice) is the word used for the wickedness that precipitated the flood (Gen 6:11–13). Envy of the violent is its own form of moral corruption.
3.32: “For the LORD detests (tôʿᵃbat-YHWH) the devious (naʿᵃlôz).” Tôʿēbāh (abomination, what is detested) is one of the strongest negative assessment terms in Proverbs — it appears 22 times in the book, marking the most offensive transgressions (cf. 6:16–19; 11:1; 12:22). Lûz (to deviate, be crooked — cf. 2:15) defines the devious person. “But the upright (yᵉšārîm) are in his confidence (sôdô).” Sôd (intimate counsel, secret circle — the divine council, Ps 25:14; Jer 23:18; Amos 3:7) — the upright are admitted to God’s inner circle.
3.33: “The LORD’s curse (mᵉʾērat-YHWH) rests on the house of the wicked, but he blesses (yᵉbārek) the home (nᵉwēh) of the righteous.” Mᵉʾērāh (curse) is the covenantal curse-word of Deuteronomy (Deut 28:20; Mal 2:2; 3:9). Nāweh (home, pasture, settled dwelling) suggests the home as a place of pastoral security. The contrast between cursed house and blessed home is the Deuteronomic two-ways applied to domestic life.
3.34: “He mocks the mockers (lēṣîm yālîṣ) himself, but shows favor (ḥēn) to the humble (ʿᵃnāwîm).” Lêṣ (mocker, scorner — cf. 1:22) is repaid in kind: God meets the scorner’s mockery with his own sovereign mockery (cf. Ps 2:4). ʿĀnāw (humble, afflicted, meek — the socially low and spiritually receptive) receives ḥēn (grace, favor). This verse is directly quoted in Jas 4:6 and 1 Pet 5:5 via the LXX, and is the fountainhead of the NT teaching on grace to the humble.
3.35: “The wise (ḥᵃkāmîm) will inherit (yinḥᵃlû) honor (kābôd), but fools (kᵉsîlîm) are left with disgrace (mārîm qālôn).” Nāḥal (to inherit as a lasting possession — the language of land-inheritance) applied to honor: wisdom’s honor is a permanent inheritance, not a temporary reputation. The contrast of kābôd (glory, weighty honor) with qālôn (shame, dishonor — the light and trivial) closes ch3’s positive portrait of wisdom with the two ultimate destinies: permanent honor or disgrace.

4.1: The address shifts to the plural 'sons' (bānim), suggesting a wisdom teacher addressing students, though the singular 'father's instruction' (mūsar 'āb) maintains the family model. Mūsar is Proverbs' governing term for educational discipline — encompassing instruction, correction, and the character formed by it; Greek paideia captures it best.
4.2: Leqaḥ (teaching/what is received) is what the student takes away; tōrāh here means instruction or guidance rather than the Mosaic law specifically. Both terms denote received wisdom rather than innate insight.
4.3: Yāḥîd (only/tender) is rare in this sense; the parallel 'beloved' (rāk, tender) creates a picture of childhood cherishing. The father recalls being his father's treasured pupil — wisdom is transmitted person to person, not merely text to reader.
4.4: Yiḥzaq — 'lay hold of' — is the same root used of Boaz 'catching' Ruth's hand; the grip on wisdom must be as firm as a physical hold. The negative command 'do not let your heart let go' (al-yirepaḥ libbẹkā) frames wisdom as something that can slip away.
4.5: Qenēh (get/acquire) appears in both v5 and v7 — wisdom is framed as a commodity to be obtained with effort. The same verb in v7 creates wordplay: 'the beginning of wisdom: get wisdom.' This does not mean wisdom is for sale but that it requires active pursuit.
4.6: Personified wisdom (ḥokmāh) becomes a guardian: 'she will keep you' (tiŝmerẹkā). The protective-companion imagery begins here and culminates in ch8's full personification. The condition is simply 'do not forsake her' — wisdom is lost by neglect, not by attack.
4.7: 'The beginning of wisdom: get wisdom' (rē'šît ḥokmāh qenēh ḥokmāh) is intentionally circular and emphatic: the first, most important thing you can do is pursue wisdom itself. Rē'šît can mean 'beginning', 'first in importance', or 'principal thing' — likely all three here.
4.8: Salselehā (exalt her) is a hapax in the Piel, probably meaning 'lift up' or 'honor' — what you prize, you are prized by. The reciprocity of the wisdom relationship (honor wisdom → be honored by wisdom) runs through Proverbs 1–9's extended wisdom-personification.
4.9: Wisdom gives a 'garland of grace' (livyat ḥēn) and a 'crown of glory' (keteret tipʾāret) — both royal-martial imagery applied to the wise person. The MKT preserves 'garland' and 'crown' for these concrete terms rather than abstracting to 'honor.'
4.10: The father promises that receiving (qabbēl) his words will multiply the son's years. Long life as the reward of wisdom is a recurring Proverbs motif; Deuteronomy uses the same promise for Torah observance, grounding the wisdom tradition in covenant theology.
4.11: 'I have taught you' (hôrêtîkā) and 'I have led you' (hidractîkā) use different verbs for instruction and guidance — the combination covers both theoretical teaching and practical mentoring, both knowing the way and walking it.
4.12: Tsārar (be hampered/narrow) — 'your step will not be cramped/hampered.' The image is of unimpeded walking: wisdom gives sure footing and spacious movement. The antithesis — stumbling (tikasel) — describes the gait of the wicked in v19.
4.13: 'Hold fast to discipline' (hāzēq bammûsār) — the same grip vocabulary as v4. The climactic identification: hî' ḥayyêkā = 'she is your life.' Wisdom is not merely instrumental to good living; she is life itself. The NT identification of Christ as the wisdom of God (1 Cor 1:24) takes up this life-identification directly.
4.14: The father turns to negative commands: 'do not enter the path of the wicked' (al-tābô' bĕ'oraḥ rĕšā'îm). The two-way motif — wisdom's path vs. the path of the wicked — structures all of ch4.
4.15: Perā'ēhû — 'shun it/avoid it' — is intensive: not just 'don't go there' but 'actively turn away from it.' The fourfold imperative in v15 (shun, do not travel it, turn away, pass on) underscores the urgency of avoidance.
4.16: The portrait of the wicked is relentlessly active: they cannot sleep unless they do evil — wickedness is compulsive, not merely chosen. Nigrĕlāh šēnātam = 'their sleep is robbed/taken away' if they have not caused someone to stumble. This describes addiction to wrongdoing.
4.17: 'They eat the bread of wickedness and drink the wine of violence' (leḥem riš'āh... yêyn ḥamāsîm) — the dietary metaphor describes evil as sustenance: the wicked are nourished by what destroys others. Ḥāmās (violence) is the word used for the wickedness that prompted the flood (Gen 6:11).
4.18: Nōgah (brightness/gleaming) and the participle hālōk wā'ōr (going and shining/brightening) describe the progressive illumination of the righteous path. The comparison to 'the full day' (kēdōn hayyôm) — perhaps 'midday' — captures the trajectory: not a fixed light but an increasing one.
4.19: The wicked path is 'ăpēlâ (deep darkness/gloom) — the antithesis of the brightening light of v18. Bammeh yikkāšēlû lō' yāde'û — 'on what they stumble they do not know' — captures the moral blindness of those who have chosen darkness: they cannot even identify their own stumbling blocks.
4.20: The father returns to the positive imperatives: haqšēb (incline/attend) and hiṭṭēh (turn/bow). Hearing wisdom requires physical posture — ears inclined, heart directed. The teaching presupposes not passive reception but active, directed attention.
4.21: 'Let them not escape from your eyes' (al-yāliṣûm mê'êneyḵā) — keep wisdom in sight. The counterpart to the eyes not straying (v25) is wisdom staying within your field of vision. Šomerem bĕtôk libbẹḵā = 'guard them in the center of your heart.'
4.22: Ḥayyîm hēm lĕmōṣê'êhem — 'life they are for those who find them' — repeating the life-identification of v13 but now applied to the specific 'words' and 'sayings' of v20. Wisdom's words have life-giving power; marpe' (healing/health) points to their curative as well as preserving function.
4.23: 'Guard your heart above all else' (mikkol-mišmār nĕṣōr libbẹkā) — the most concentrated command in the father's discourse. Tôṣe'ôt ḥayyîm = 'the outflowings/issues of life' — the heart is the source from which all of life flows. MKT 'source from which life flows' captures both the outflow image and the causal primacy of the heart over all behavior.
4.24: 'Remove from you crookedness of mouth' (hāsēr mimmẹkā 'iqqeš peh); 'iqqēš (crooked/perverse) describes the twisted mouth — speech that distorts reality. The imperative to remove crooked speech flows directly from the previous verse: what flows from a guarded heart is straight speech.
4.25: 'Let your eyes look directly ahead' (êneyḵā lĕnōkaḥ yabiṭû) — the father links right vision with right walking. Nōkaḥ = directly/straight before you; le'umat = right in front. The disciplined gaze that does not wander is the complement to the guarded heart.
4.26: Peles (balance/level) is the root of the call to 'ponder/level' the path of your feet. The MKT 'ponder' captures both the reflective and the smoothing/leveling senses. The disciplined life is not impulsive but measured — paths are consciously assessed before being taken.
4.27: 'Do not swerve right or left' (al-tiṭ yāmîn ûśmō'l) — the final verse of ch4 frames the entire discourse with the two-way imagery: the righteous path is straight, and departures in either direction lead away from it. Hāsēr raglĕḵā mērā' = 'turn your foot away from evil.'

5.1: The singular address resumes ('my son'). Ḥokmātî = 'my wisdom'; lĕtôratî = 'to my instruction.' The opening call to listen (as in 4:1, 4:10, 4:20) frames the entire adultery-warning as a disciplinary address, not a moralistic lecture.
5.2: Mĕzimmôt (discretion/prudent plans) and da'at (knowledge) are the positive qualities that the father's instruction preserves. The threat from the 'strange woman' is precisely to these: she offers apparent intimacy while destroying genuine wisdom.
5.3: Zārāh (strange/foreign woman) from the verb zûr (to turn aside, be strange/foreign) — the term may denote a foreign woman, a prostitute, or simply a woman who is 'other' (someone else's wife). Nōpet tiṭṭōpenāh śiptê zārāh = 'honeycomb drips the lips of the strange woman.' The seductive speech is figured as the sweetest food before revealing its bitterness in v4.
5.4: Mar (bitter) and la'anāh (wormwood/gall) describe the aftertaste of the strange woman's speech — poison masked as honey. Pîpîyôt = double-edged (sword), from the word for 'mouth.' The weapon that kills with both sides of its blade mirrors the speech that seduces and destroys.
5.5: 'Her feet go down to death' (raggleyā yōrĕdôt māwet) — Sheol and death are the destination of the path she leads to. The downward movement (death is below) contrasts with the upward trajectory of wisdom in v6's 'path of life.' This is the literal end of the way that 'seems right' to a man (14:12).
5.6: Peles ḥayyîm (the balance/scale of life) — she does not ponder the path of life. Her paths wander (nā'û) and cannot be known (lô' tēdā'). The same peles (balance/level) that the son is to apply to his path (4:26) is conspicuously absent from hers — she does not measure consequences.
5.7: The father returns to the second-person address ('sons'/'my children') for emphasis. The resumptive call 'hear me now' signals the practical application of the warning about to follow in vv8-14.
5.8: 'Keep your way far from her, and do not go near the door of her house' (harḥēq mê'ālêhā darkẹkā) — distance, not dialogue, is the wisdom response. The father does not say 'resist her when she speaks to you' but 'stay away from her house entirely.' Avoiding the temptation is superior to resisting it.
5.9: Hôdeḵā (your honor/splendor) and šĕnôteḵā (your years) are what is lost: the substance of a life given away. Le'akzārî = 'to the cruel one' — the identity of the 'others' who benefit from the fool's loss (the woman's husband? death personified?) is deliberately ambiguous.
5.10: 'Lest strangers be sated with your strength' (yiśbĕ'û zārîm kōḥẹkā) — the irony is that the 'strange woman' (zārāh, v3) causes the man's substance to go to 'strangers' (zārîm, same root). The energy and wealth that should build his own household are consumed by those who have no claim on them.
5.11: Wĕnāhamtā bĕ'aḥarîtẹkā = 'and you will groan at your end' — nāham is the word for an animal's growl or roar, used here for the anguish of regret. The deathbed realization of wasted life is more devastating than any external punishment.
5.12: The retrospective voice of the regret-speaker (vv12-14): sānē'tî mûsār = 'I hated discipline.' The failure was not ignorance of wisdom but rejection of it — the man knew better and chose otherwise. This is Proverbs' diagnosis of moral failure: not stupidity but willful rejection of correction.
5.13: Qôl môrāy (voice of my teachers) and limmûdāy (those who instructed me) — the regret is specifically about not heeding the community of wisdom-teachers. The plural suggests the wisdom school context: the instruction was available and offered; it was refused.
5.14: Bĕme'aṭ hāyîtî bĕkol-rā' = 'I was almost in all evil' — the path of folly leads to total public disgrace; bĕtôk qāhāl wĕ'ēdāh = 'in the midst of the assembly and congregation.' The public nature of the shame is part of the warning: adultery destroys social standing and communal belonging.
5.15: 'Drink water from your own cistern' (šĕtēh-māyim mibborẹkā) — bôr (hewn cistern) and be'ēr (dug well/spring) are distinct: a cistern collects rainwater and requires labor to maintain; a well accesses a living underground source. The marriage is figured as both a stored resource and a living spring — the MKT's 'cistern' and 'well' preserve this distinction.
5.16: 'Should your springs be scattered outward?' (yāpûṣû mayanôteyḵā ḥûṣāh) — the rhetorical question challenges promiscuity: should the water that belongs to your household flow to public streets (palge-māyim birĕḥōbôt)? The expected answer is no — the water/sexuality of marriage belongs within the covenant household.
5.17: 'Let them be for you alone, and not for strangers with you' (yihyû-lĕkā lĕbaddẹkā) — the exclusivity of marital sexuality is grounded in ownership: the springs are yours; they are not for strangers. This is the foundation of the positive call in v18.
5.18: 'Let your fountain be blessed' (yĕhî-mĕqôrĕkā bārûk) — mĕqôr (fountain/source) is the term used for the woman's body as the source of life (and see Prov 18:4). The blessing-prayer over the wife-fountain is one of Proverbs' most tender marriage images. Ēšet nĕ'ûreḵā = 'wife of your youth' — the lifelong covenant relationship is in view.
5.19: 'Ayyelet 'ahābîm (loving doe/gazelle) and ya'alat ḥēn (graceful deer/chamois) are terms of endearment with erotic resonance. Daddeyā yĕrāwwûkā bĕkol-'ēt = 'let her breasts satisfy you at all times' — the Hebrew is explicit and unabashed. Proverbs does not spiritualize the marriage relationship away from its physical dimension but grounds it there. MKT preserves 'breasts' rather than euphemizing.
5.20: The rhetorical question returns: 'why be intoxicated with a foreign woman' (nûkrîyyāh)? The word šāgāh (wander/be intoxicated/go astray) describes the disoriented movement of someone who has lost their bearings — used for erring morally and for literal wandering. The contrast with the wife-of-your-youth is sharp.
5.21: 'For a man's ways are before the eyes of the LORD' (kî neged 'ênê YHWH darkê-'îš) — the surveillance theme: the key deterrent against adultery is not fear of the husband but awareness of divine observation. Pōlēs kol-ma'ggālôtāw = 'and he weighs/examines all his paths.' MKT 'examines every path' captures the forensic precision of divine scrutiny.
5.22: 'His iniquities will ensnare the wicked' ('awōnôtāw yilkĕdûhû) — the sinner is caught not by external punishment but by his own sin as a trap. Ḥablê ḥaṭṭā'tô = 'the cords of his sin' — sin as a rope that binds. The passive-trap image (the sin catches him) and the active-cord image (the sin binds him) are combined.
5.23: Yāmût bĕ'ên mûsār = 'he will die for lack of discipline' — the final verse brings the entire ch5 warning to its conclusion: the rejector of wisdom dies of his rejection. Bĕrōb 'iwwaltô yiš'geh = 'in the greatness of his folly he will go astray/wander' — šāgāh echoes v20's intoxication/wandering, completing the portrait of one who can no longer find his way.

6.1: 'Ārabhttā lĕrê'eḵā = 'you have pledged surety for your neighbor' — 'ārab (surety/pledge) is the financial-legal term for cosigning someone else's debt. The father warns not against helping a neighbor but against the specific legal exposure of pledging surety — a common path to financial ruin in the ancient world.
6.2: 'Snared (nôqaštā) by the words of your mouth' — the trap is self-made; the words you spoke legally bind you. The same speech that creates relationship (promises, pledges) can destroy it through careless commitment.
6.3: 'Do this now, my son, and deliver yourself' — the urgency is notable: when you realize you are snared, act immediately. Hitra'pēś = 'be earnest/importune' in seeking release — literally 'stamp yourself' or 'abase yourself.' The pride that must be swallowed to seek release is part of the wisdom lesson.
6.4: 'Give no sleep to your eyes, no slumber to your eyelids' (al-titten šēnāh lĕ'êneyḵā) — the urgency of escaping the pledge is contrasted implicitly with the sluggard's love of sleep (vv9-10). When a real problem demands immediate action, sleep is the enemy.
6.5: 'Save yourself like a gazelle from the hunter's hand' — the animal-flight imagery emphasizes urgency and self-preservation. The gazelle (ṣĕbî) and the bird (ṣippôr) are creatures that survive by swiftness of escape; the wisdom student must imitate their speed when trapped.
6.6: 'Go to the ant, you sluggard' (lēk-'el-nĕmālāh 'āṣēl) — nĕmālāh (ant) appears only twice in the OT (here and 30:25). The ant is the unexpected teacher: a creature with no king or overseer that nevertheless stores food for winter. The sluggard ('āṣēl) is the foil throughout vv6-11.
6.7: The ant works without authority (qāṣîn = officer, šōṭēr = overseer, mōšēl = ruler) compelling her. The self-motivated industry of the ant is the implicit contrast with the sluggard who requires external pressure. Wisdom is self-directing; folly is reactive and passive.
6.8: 'She prepares her bread in summer and gathers her food at harvest' (tākîn baqqayiṣ laḥmāh) — the ant's wisdom is temporal: she works in the season of abundance for the season of scarcity. The harvesting vocabulary (qāṣîr) links the ant to the broader wisdom theme of providential preparation.
6.9: The rhetorical questions directed at the sluggard — 'how long will you lie, O sluggard?' (ad-mātay 'āṣēl tiŝkāb) — are not answered; the sluggard's response in v10 is only more sleep. The question is not a real inquiry but a rebuke disguised as curiosity.
6.10: 'A little sleep, a little slumber, a little folding of the hands to rest' (me'aṭ šēnôt me'aṭ tenûmôt) — three minimizing phrases in a row, each reducing the rest to 'a little.' The self-deception is the point: each 'just a little more' accumulates to a life of failure.
6.11: 'Poverty will come upon you like a vagabond/traveler' (ûbā' kemĕhallēk rē'šeḵā) — the image of poverty arriving like an unexpected traveler stresses its certainty and suddenness. Māgēn (shield/armed man) — the MKT 'armed man' captures the aggressive, irresistible nature of the approaching want.
6.12: 'Îš bĕliyya'al = 'man of worthlessness/belial' — bĕliyya'al is a compound term (beli + ya'al = 'without profit/worth') describing someone of absolute moral worthlessness. Hôlēk 'iqqĕšût peh = 'walking with a crooked mouth' — the troublemaker's crooked speech precedes him in his path.
6.13: The troublemaker communicates deceptively through body language: winking eyes (qōrēṣ bĕ'ênāw), shuffling feet (mōlēl bĕraglāw), pointing fingers (mōreh bĕ'eṣbe'ōtāw). All three are non-verbal signals used to communicate malice while maintaining verbal deniability.
6.14: 'Perversity is in his heart' (tahpuḵôt bĕlibbô) — tahpuḵôt (perversities/distortions) denotes a fundamental inversion of values. Ḥōrēš rā' = 'devising/plowing evil'; yĕšallēaḥ mĕdānîm = 'sending out discord/strife' — the troublemaker is an active agent of communal damage.
6.15: 'Therefore his calamity will come suddenly' ('al-kēn pittō'om yābô' 'êdô) — the sudden destruction of the troublemaker mirrors the sudden arrival of poverty for the sluggard (v11). Peta' (suddenly) is emphatic; wĕ'ên marpē' = 'and there is no healing/remedy' — this ruin is irreversible.
6.16: The numerical saying ('six things... seven') is a wisdom genre (cf. Amos 1-2; Prov 30:15,18,21,29) in which the 'n+1' structure creates suspense and emphasizes the final item. The list that follows (vv17-19) catalogs the behaviors YHWH finds detestable (tô'ēbâ).
6.17: 'Haughty eyes' ('ênayim rāmôt, lit. 'lifted eyes') head the list. The seven abominations: (1) haughty eyes, (2) lying tongue (lĕšôn šāqer), (3) hands that shed innocent blood (yādayim šōpĕkôt dām nāqî). The list moves from internal attitude (pride) to speech to action, showing the descent of wickedness from disposition to deed.
6.18: Continuing the list: (4) a heart that devises wicked plans (lēb ḥōrēš maḥšĕbôt 'āwen), (5) feet that run quickly to evil (raglayim mĕmahărôt lārûṣ lārā'āh). The heart, feet, and earlier the tongue and hands create a whole-body portrait of wickedness — no faculty is excluded from the service of evil.
6.19: Items (6) and (7): a false witness who breathes out lies (mēpîaḥ kĕzābîm 'ēd šāqer) and one who sows discord among brothers (ûmĕšallēaḥ mĕdānîm bên 'aḥîm). Item 7 — discord-sowing — is the culminating abomination, returning to the troublemaker portrait of vv12-14. Communal harmony is the supreme social good whose violation is most detested.
6.20: 'Keep the commandment of your father' (nĕṣōr miṣwat 'ābîḵā) — the family-wisdom frame resumes. Miṣwāh (commandment) and tôrāh (instruction/teaching) placed in parallel reflect the convergence of family wisdom and covenant instruction in Proverbs: the father's teaching is functionally Torah.
6.21: 'Bind them on your heart continually' (qāšrēm 'al-libbẹḵā tāmîd) and 'tie them around your neck' ('anddēm 'al-gargrōteḵā) — the physical-binding metaphor parallels the tefillin/mezuzah commands in Deut 6:8-9, suggesting that wisdom instruction has the same binding authority as covenant law. The MKT 'bind them on your heart' preserves this.
6.22: 'When you walk, it will lead you' (bĕhithalleḵḵā tanhēh 'ōtāḵ); 'when you lie down, it will watch over you' (bĕšāḵbĕkā tiŝmōr 'ālêḵā); 'when you wake, it will talk with you' (wăhăqiṣōtā hî' tĕśîḥeḵā) — the wisdom that is internalized (vv20-21) becomes a constant guide and companion throughout every state of daily life.
6.23: 'For the command is a lamp and the teaching is a light' (kî nēr miṣwāh wĕtôrāh 'ôr) — nēr (lamp) and 'ôr (light) are distinct: the command is a handheld lamp (immediate, portable guidance); the teaching is light itself (the broader illumination of understanding). Together they define 'the way of life' (derek ḥayyîm). Psalm 119:105 applies the same imagery to the word of God.
6.24: 'To guard you from the evil woman' (lĕšomrĕkā mê'ēšet rā') — the lamp/light of wisdom has a specific protective function: to guard against the evil woman/adulteress (nēkrîyyāh, foreign woman). Ḥĕlaqat lāšôn nākrîyyāh = 'the smooth tongue of the foreign woman' — the honeyed speech of ch5:3 returns.
6.25: 'Do not desire her beauty in your heart' (al-taḥmod yopyāh bĕlibbẹkā) — the inner prohibition precedes the outer: desire is the first temptation. The eyelid ('appappayim, unique term) reference describes seductive glancing. The command parallels the 10th commandment's prohibition on covetousness, grounding the adultery warning in covenant ethics.
6.26: The economic comparison: a prostitute's price is a loaf of bread, but the adulterous wife hunts for a precious life (nāpĕš yĕqārāh tāṣûd). The contrast is stark: the prostitute's transaction costs money; the adultery with a married woman costs everything — the 'precious life' is the adulterer's own.
6.27: 'Can a man carry fire in his bosom (ḥēqô) and his clothes not be burned?' — the rhetorical question makes the adultery-is-fire analogy obvious. Ḥēq (bosom/lap) is deliberately physical; the fire-in-the-bosom image describes an intimate destruction that cannot be contained.
6.28: 'Or can one walk on hot coals and his feet not be burned?' — the second rhetorical question extends the fire imagery. The point is not that fire is powerful but that fire is fire: its destructive effect is natural and inevitable, not a matter of luck or circumstance.
6.29: 'So is the man who goes in to his neighbor's wife' (kēn habbā' el-'ēšet rē'ēhû) — the explicit application of the two fire-rhetorical-questions. The adultery is not merely immoral but physically self-destructive; the 'none who touches her will go unpunished' (lō' yinnāqeh) is the same language used for YHWH 'not clearing the guilty' in Exodus 34:7.
6.30: 'People do not despise a thief if he steals to satisfy his hunger' — the comparison is surprising: the thief who steals for hunger has some degree of understandable motive, even though he will still pay the penalty. This introduces a comparison to highlight how much worse adultery is.
6.31: 'But if he is caught, he will pay sevenfold' (yĕšallēm šib'ātayim) — the sevenfold penalty exceeds Exodus 22:4's double-restitution requirement, suggesting that Proverbs is using a proverbial figure for 'complete/total restitution' rather than a specific legal quantity. The thief, though his motive is understandable, still bears full consequences.
6.32: 'He who commits adultery lacks sense' (nō'ēp 'iššāh ḥasar-lēb) — ḥasar-lēb (lacking heart/mind) is Proverbs' clinical diagnosis: not wickedness primarily but stupidity. The adulterer destroys his own life (mĕšaḥēt napšô hû' = 'he who destroys himself does it') — the act is self-harm in the most fundamental sense.
6.33: 'Wounds and dishonor he will get' (nega' wĕqālôn yimṣā') — the physical violence implied (nega' = wound/blow) anticipates the cuckolded husband's vengeance in v34. Wĕḥerpātô lō' timmāḥeh = 'and his reproach will not be wiped away' — unlike theft, the shame of adultery is permanent; there is no restitution that restores reputation.
6.34: 'For jealousy makes a man furious' (kî qin'āh ḥĕmat-gāber) — the husband's jealousy (qin'āh) is presented as natural and fierce: a force that cannot be reasoned with. Lō' yaḥmôl bĕyôm nāqām = 'he will not spare in the day of vengeance' — the day of reckoning is certain.
6.35: 'He will not accept any ransom, nor be appeased though you multiply gifts' — in contrast to the thief who can make full restitution (v31), there is no financial resolution for adultery's offense against a marriage. No kōper (ransom/compensation) satisfies. The honor offense is categorical and irredeemable — the ultimate demonstration of why adultery, unlike theft, destroys the adulterer completely.

7.1: imrei ('emer, H561) — the plural noun for ‘words, sayings’; distinct from dabar (the weightier spoken word or event). The father’s instruction is characterized as 'emer — the lighter communicative word — yet these words are to be “stored up” (tzaphan, to hide/treasure, used of hiding valuables). The contrast between the casual word-form and the precious storage-command creates the tone of the entire chapter.
7.2: ke-'ishon 'einecha — “as the 'ishon of your eye.” 'Ishon (H380) means ‘pupil of the eye’ but literally translates as ‘the little man’ — the tiny reflection of oneself visible in another’s pupil. The idiom of guarding like the pupil of the eye appears in Deut 32:10 and Ps 17:8 of YHWH’s care for Israel; here the father uses it of the son’s care for the father’s teaching.
