The most reflexively sensitive organ of the body.
7.3: qashor 'al-'etzbe'otecha — bind on your fingers. 'Etzba' (H676) is the finger, the instrument of pointing and precision. The binding of instruction on the fingers extends the Shema-metaphor (Deut 6:8: bind on your hand) to a new context: the father’s teaching is to be as constantly present as one’s own fingers. The physical, embodied quality of this metaphor reinforces the MKT’s anthropology of instruction as bodily formation.
7.4:
'achoti — “my sister.” 'Achot (H269) as a term of intimate address for wisdom is significant: in Song of Songs the beloved calls the bride “my sister” (4:9-10, 12; 5:1-2), suggesting an erotic register of intimacy that is deliberately activated here to counter the seductive address of the adulteress in vv16-18. The father invites the son to the most intimate possible relationship with wisdom as the competing attraction to the “foreign woman” ('ishah zarah).

moda' — “close companion, kinsman.” Moda' (cf. Ruth 2:1, 3:2) is a term for the closest family member; the parallelism with “sister” intensifies the intimacy of the wisdom-relationship the father recommends.
7.5: tishmereka me-'ishah zarah — “she [wisdom] will guard you from the foreign/strange woman.” 'Ishah zarah (strange/foreign woman) is the dominant characterization of the seductress throughout chs 1-9. Zarah (H2114) means ‘alien, strange, foreign’ — not necessarily a non-Israelite, but one outside the legitimate covenant bond. The nokhriyah (‘alien woman’) in the parallel is a near-synonym emphasizing her outsider status relative to the father’s household and instruction.
7.6: be-'ad halloni — “through my window.” Chalon (H2474) is the standard window term; here the father positions himself as observer rather than participant. The narrative frame of vv6-23 is the father’s eyewitness account (“I saw”) — the literary device of the reported observation lends the cautionary tale the authority of testimony. The 'eshnav (H822, v6b, ‘lattice/casement’) refers to a window with a screening lattice.
7.7: va-'er ba-peta'im — “I discerned among the simple ones.” Bein (H995) is the verb of discernment/understanding used extensively in wisdom literature; here it is the father’s act of distinguishing. Peta'im (the simple, the naïve) are the category of the morally unformed who have not yet chosen wisdom or folly — vulnerable in both directions. The object of the father’s discernment is a na'ar chaser-lev — “a young man lacking heart (= mind, moral sense).” Lev (heart) in Hebrew denotes the seat of thought, will, and moral judgment, not merely emotion.
7.8: qoreh le-fathe'ach beithah — “passing near the corner of her street, drawing near the entrance of her house.” The young man’s path description (“in the street”, “in the square”, “near the corner”, “the way to her house”) is the geography of increasingly deliberate proximity. He is not yet at her door, but he is approaching it step by step — a wisdom observation about the incremental nature of moral failure.
7.9: be-'ishon layelah ve-'afelah — “in the darkness of night and the thick darkness.” 'Ishon layelah reuses the 'ishon word from v2 (the pupil of the eye), creating an ironic wordplay: the “little man of night” (the pupil — the organ for guarding wisdom) is now the metaphor for the deepest darkness. The darkness setting establishes the moral coding: the events of vv10-23 are hidden from public accountability, the shame-preventing community oversight of Israelite life.
7.10: 'eshet qedeshah — the MKT follows the note that 'ishah here does not require the explicit translation “prostitute” (KJV) but rather emphasizes the guarded heart (netzurat lev). Natsar (to guard, watch, keep) applied to the heart is the wisdom vocabulary of deliberate concealment of intention — she knows what she is doing. The phrase “dressed as a harlot” (shith zonah) characterizes her appearance rather than her legal status; she may be an adulteress (cf. v19) rather than a professional prostitute.
7.11: homiyah hi' ve-soreref — “she is noisy and wayward.” Hamah (H1993, to be noisy, to clamor) is used of tumultuous crowds and boiling water; the seductress’s characteristic is her disruptive presence. Sorer (H5637) means ‘wayward, stubborn’ — the same root used of the “stubborn and rebellious son” in Deut 21:18-21. The contrast with Wisdom’s structured invitation (ch 8-9) is deliberate: Wisdom calls from the heights; Folly/the seductress lurks in the streets.
7.12: 'atah ba-chutz 'atah ba-rechovot — “now outside, now in the streets, lurking at every corner.” The omnipresence of the seductress in the public spaces of the city is part of her characterization: she is the antithesis of the domestic virtue of 31:10-31. The three-fold location description (outside, streets, corners) emphasizes her unstable and deliberately exposing presence.
7.13: ve-hecheziqah bo ve-nashah lo — “she seized him and kissed him.” The grammatical subject of the verbs is the woman, making her the agent of the pursuit: she grabs him, she kisses him. The reversal of the expected courtship dynamic (male pursuit of female) is a deliberate element of the characterization of the seductress as one who transgresses social roles. he'ezah paneyha — “with a brazen face”; az panim is used in Prov 21:29 of the wicked man — the same characterization is applied here to the seductress.
7.14: zevachim shelamim 'alai — “peace offerings are with me; today I have paid my vows.” The shelamim (peace/fellowship offering, H8002) is the category of sacrifice where the worshipper kept a portion of the meat for communal eating after the priestly portions were given. Lev 7:16-17 required this meat to be eaten within one or two days — so her statement “today I have paid my vows” means she has abundant fresh meat available. The seduction uses the sacrificial calendar opportunistically: religious observance becomes a pretext for immorality.
7.15: yatzati liqra'techa leshacher panecha — “I came out to meet you, to seek your face earnestly.” The verb shachar (H7836, to seek earnestly, to seek at dawn) is used in wisdom literature for the diligent pursuit of wisdom itself (8:17: “those who seek me earnestly will find me”). The seductress deliberately mimics the vocabulary of wisdom-seeking — the verbal echo is ironic and pedagogically intentional.
7.16: 'erez 'arashti mitati divedim 'atun mitzrayim — “I have spread my bed with colored blankets and fine linen from Egypt.” 'Erekh (to spread/arrange, used of setting out a banquet or preparing a battle array) and the Egyptian linen ('etun mitzrayim) invoke luxury and sophistication: these are imported goods from the dominant cultural power of the day. The material detail locates the seduction in the context of international trade and cosmopolitan wealth, making her offer appear as participation in the broader world of cultured life.
7.17: naddafti mishkavi mor 'ahalim ve-qinamon — “I have scented my bed with myrrh, aloes, and cinnamon.” All three spices are luxury imports used in the Song of Songs (Song 3:6; 4:6, 14) as erotic fragrances. The seductress deliberately invokes the vocabulary and sensory world of legitimate marital love (the Song) to package her adulterous invitation in the most attractive possible form. Mor (myrrh, H4753), 'ahalim (aloes, H174), and qinamon (cinnamon, H7076) together represent the full spectrum of imported aromatic luxury.
7.18: nerve'eh dodim 'ad-habo'ker — “let us drink our fill of love until morning.” Ravah (to drink one’s fill, to be saturated) is used of saturation with water; here it is applied to sexual satisfaction. Dodim (H1730) means ‘love-making, caresses’ — the term is used throughout Song of Songs for physical love. The time-frame “until morning” expresses the total availability of the offer while presupposing the husband’s extended absence (v19).
7.19: ki ein ha-'ish be-beito — “for the man is not in his house.” The detail of the husband’s absence is the structural hinge of the seduction: she has knowledge of his travel plans and has prepared accordingly. The Hebrew ha-'ish (lit. ‘the man’, using the definite article — ‘her husband’) makes the adulterous context explicit. She is not a widow or a single woman; she is using her husband’s absence as an opportunity.
7.20: tzror ha-kesef laqach be-yado — “he has taken the money bag with him.” The husband carries the family wealth, suggesting a prosperous household: the seductress lives in luxury (cf. v16-17) and her husband is a merchant of means. The detail be-yom ha-kese' yavo' beito — “at the full moon he will return” — uses kese' (H3677, full moon) to specify the extended absence. A full lunar cycle gives her a calendar-window for the affair.
7.21: hittatu be-rov liqchah — “she seduced him with her many speeches.” Natah (to turn, to incline) with the sense of seduction: she turned his path. Chelqat sefateyha — “by the smooth talk of her lips.” Cheleq (H2506) means ‘portion, smoothness, flattery’; the same term is used in v5 for the words the seductress speaks. The smooth lips that deceive are the inverse of Wisdom’s straight and righteous speech (8:8-9: “all the words of my mouth are righteous; there is nothing crooked or perverse in them”).
7.22: holech 'achareyha pith'om ke-shor el-tevach yavo' — “he followed her at once, like an ox going to slaughter.” Pith'om (suddenly, immediately) is significant: the seduction works on the young man without deliberation or resistance. The ox-to-slaughter and fool-to-punishment comparisons frame the seduced young man’s action as both his own culpability ('evil, H191 = ‘fool’) and his victimhood. The animal comparisons use the OT’s standard imagery of the thoughtless victim.
7.23: 'ad yefallach chetz kevedo — “until an arrow pierces his liver.” The liver (kaved, H3516) in Hebrew thought is the seat of deepest inward experience (cf. Lam 2:11: “my liver is poured out”). An arrow through the liver is a mortal wound; the metaphor completes the animal-slaughter imagery of v22: this is not merely social embarrassment but death. The transition from “pleasant-seeming” to “lethal” without warning is the wisdom point.
7.24: ushme'u-li banim — “and now, sons, listen to me.” The father returns from narrative to direct address; the observation of vv6-23 is now applied to the sons as direct warning. The plural “sons” (also in 5:7, 8:32) suggests the discourse is not merely private fatherly instruction but a formative teaching document for a wider group of students in the wisdom tradition.
7.25: 'al-yeshte 'el-derakheyha libecha — “do not let your heart be drawn to her ways.” The danger is not primarily physical proximity but internal orientation: natah (to turn, incline) the heart is the first movement toward disaster. The heart (lev) as the decision-making center means that the internal posture precedes and determines the external action. Wisdom addresses the root, not only the symptom.
7.26: ki-rabbim challelim hipilah — “for many are those she has brought low.” The plural and the quantifier “many” (rabbim) extend the seductress beyond one dangerous woman to a pattern: she represents a type of death-inviting path that has claimed many victims. Challal (H2491) means ‘pierced, slain’ — using the vocabulary of battlefield death for the fate of adultery’s victims. The “mighty ones” ('atzumim, H6099) who are among her slain emphasizes that strength is no defense against this form of death.
7.27: darkhei sheol beithah — “her house is the roads of Sheol.” The plural darkei sheol (roads/ways of Sheol) is distinctive: the adulteress’s house is not a single path downward but a network of paths all leading to Sheol. The final image of “the inner chambers of death” (chadrei mavet) develops the domestic metaphor of the house: she has invited him into her house (vv11-18), but her house is a house of death. The structural inversion of wisdom’s house (9:1: seven pillars, a prepared feast) is complete: the adulteress’s house leads to Sheol, wisdom’s house leads to life (9:6).

9.1: chokmah banta beitah — “Wisdom has built her house.” The shift to perfective aspect (baneta, perfect) grounds the narrative: Wisdom’s house already exists, complete, ready to receive guests. Chatzevah 'ammudeyha shiv'ah — “she has hewn her seven pillars.” Chatzav (H2672, to hew, cut stone) suggests deliberate architectural labor; the seven pillars ('amudim, H5982) are the structural foundation of the building. Seven in Hebrew numerology signifies completeness. The Wisdom-house is the designed-and-built environment for the wisdom-life; its seven pillars have been interpreted as the seven books of the Torah, the seven days of creation, or simply completeness of preparation.
9.2: tavchah tivchah — “she has slaughtered her slaughter.” The intensive form (tavchah tivchah, the cognate object construction) emphasizes the abundance and completeness of the preparation: the feast is not incidental but central. Maskhah yeinah — “she has mixed her wine”: wine mixed with spices or water was the standard form for a feast. The parallel between Wisdom’s feast (slaughtered meat, mixed wine, prepared table) and the shelamim-feast of the adulteress in 7:14 is deliberately inverted: both offer a banquet, but wisdom’s leads to life and the seductress’s leads to Sheol.
9.3: shalachah na'aroteyha — “she has sent out her servant girls.” The plural na'arot (servant girls, attendants) makes wisdom’s household a substantial establishment. The servants call from the gappei meromei qaret — the “heights of the city” (literally ‘the wings/highest points of the city’). The elevated calling-point ensures maximum public visibility and reach — wisdom’s invitation is issued from the most prominent position available.
9.4: mi-peti yasur hennah — “whoever is simple, let him turn in here.” The address to the peti (simple, naive, H6612) is the same as in 8:5; wisdom’s invitation is first directed to the most morally unformed. Chasar-lev — “lacking heart/mind” — the phrase from 7:7 recurs: the naïve young man who was vulnerable to the adulteress is now the target of wisdom’s specific invitation. The parallel between the two invitations (ch 9:4 = ch 9:16) is structural: both Wisdom and Folly use the same opening invitation-formula.
9.5: lechemu be-lachmiy — “come, eat of my bread.” The invitation to eat wisdom’s bread and drink her wine is the fullest expression of the feast metaphor in Proverbs. Sheitu be-yayin masakhti — “drink of the wine I have mixed.” The bread and wine of wisdom’s banquet are the material elements of the feast that lead to life; the later NT eucharistic resonances are not the text’s intention but arise from this shared symbolic vocabulary of the feast that transforms and sustains life.
9.6: 'izvu peta'im vi-chiyu — “leave your simple ways and live.” The imperative 'izvu (H5800, forsake, leave behind) applied to the peta'im (simple ways, the path of the naïve) frames wisdom’s invitation as requiring the abandonment of a prior way of life. Vi-chiyu — “and live” — the life-promise is the direct consequence of leaving simpleness behind. Ve-'ishru be-derekh binah — “and walk in the way of understanding”: 'ashar (H833, to go straight, to walk happily, to call blessed) is the same root as 'ashrei (blessed); to walk in understanding’s way is itself the blessed life.
9.7: yoser letz laqach lo qalon — “whoever corrects a mocker gets dishonor for himself.” Meyasser (H3256, to discipline, instruct, correct) directed at letz (H3887, the mocker/scoffer — the one who has made mockery a settled disposition) produces qalon (H7036, dishonor, shame) for the one who corrects. The wisdom tradition has a sober realism about the limits of instruction: it does not work on those who have chosen contempt as a lifestyle. Umochiah le-rasha' mumo — the one who reproves the wicked gets his mum (blemish/fault) thrown back at him.
9.8: 'al-tokach letz pen-yisne'eka — “do not rebuke a mocker or he will only hate you.” The prohibition is wisdom’s recognition that the receptivity of the addressee is a precondition of effective instruction. Hokach le-chakham ve-ye'ahavekha — “rebuke a wise person and he will love you.” The contrast is stark: the same act (rebuke) produces hatred in the mocker and love in the wise person. The wise person’s love-response to correction is the mark of genuine wisdom: he receives the rebuke as a gift rather than an insult.
9.9: ten le-chakham ve-yecham 'od — “give to a wise person and he will be wiser still.” The self-compounding nature of wisdom: chakham (wise) receiving instruction becomes yacham 'od (wiser still). Hoda' le-tzaddiq ve-yoseph leqach — “instruct a righteous person and he will increase in learning.” Tzaddiq (righteous, H6662) as a near-synonym of chakham (wise) in this parallel confirms the wisdom tradition’s equation of moral rectitude and cognitive wisdom: they are the same quality from different angles.
9.10: techillat chokmah yir'at YHWH — “the fear of the LORD is the beginning of wisdom.” This is the thematic summary of Proverbs (cf. 1:7: “the fear of the LORD is the beginning of knowledge”). Techillat (H8462, beginning, first principle — used of the beginning of a journey or the principal element of something) implies both temporal priority and logical foundationality: wisdom begins with and is grounded in yir'at YHWH. Ve-da'at qedoshim binah — “and knowledge of the Holy One is understanding.” Qedoshim (the Holy One/holy ones, H6918 plural) is rare as a divine title for YHWH alone; the plural may be a plural of majesty or an echo of the divine council.
9.11: bi yirbu yamecha — “through me your days will be multiplied.” Wisdom speaks in first person again, linking the long-life promise directly to wisdom’s presence rather than merely to wise behavior. Ve-yosifu lecha shenot chayyim — “and years of life will be added to you”: the promise is quantitative (more years) and qualitative (years of chayyim, true/full life). The life-promise is the consistent refrain of Proverbs’ invitation; here it is stated in the most direct form.
9.12: 'im-chakhamta chakhamta lakh — “if you are wise, you are wise for yourself.” The reflexive consequence of wisdom — it primarily benefits the one who acquires it — is a wisdom tradition motif (cf. Job 35:6-7; Prov 19:8). Ve-latzta levadecha tissa' — “and if you mock, you alone will bear it.” The isolation of the mocker is his own doing: he bears the consequences alone because he has rejected the community-forming function of wisdom. The binary of v12 (wise = benefit yourself; mock = bear it alone) expresses wisdom’s deeply personal stakes.
9.13: 'eshet kesilut homiyah — “Dame Folly is loud.” The personification of Folly as a woman ('eshet kesilot, ‘woman of foolishness/fools’ — a genitive of quality) creates the structural parallel to Wisdom-woman of vv1-6. Both are women, both call from elevated positions (vv3,14), both address the simple (vv4,16) with the same opening formula. Homiyah (H1993, clamorous, noisy — the same term used of the adulteress in 7:11) links Dame Folly to the seductress. Peta'yut (H6612, simplemindedness) describes her character: she has the innocence-vocabulary but weaponizes it.
9.14: ve-yashevah le-fetach beitah — “she sits at the entrance to her house.” The contrast with Wisdom is spatial as well as moral: Wisdom’s servants go out actively to invite (v3); Folly sits (yashevah) passively at her own door. 'Al-kisse' meromei qaret — “on a seat at the heights of the city” — the same elevated location (meromei qaret) that Wisdom’s servants use (v3). The spatial parallel is an ironic mirror: Folly occupies wisdom’s calling-height but does so passively and deceptively.
9.15: liqro' le-'ovrei-derekh — “calling to those who pass by.” Ha-mesharim 'orchotehem — “those who are going straight on their way.” Yashir (H3474, straight, upright) used of the travelers’ paths indicates that Folly’s specific target is those who are already on a straight path — people not looking for alternative invitations. The seduction of those already walking the right path is more dangerous than the temptation of those already wandering.
9.16: mi-peti yasur hennah — “whoever is simple, let him turn in here.” The verbatim repetition of v4’s opening invitation (mi-peti yasur hennah and chasar-lev) is the structural climax of the two-invitation structure of ch 9: both Wisdom and Folly use identical language to extend their invitations to the same audience. The difference is in what they offer: Wisdom offers bread and wine that lead to life (vv5-6); Folly offers stolen water and secret bread that lead to Sheol (vv17-18).
9.17: mayim genuvim yimtaqu — “stolen water is sweet.” Ganav (H1589, to steal) applied to water is the idiom of the forbidden secret; matok (H4966, sweet) captures the experiential appeal of transgression. Lechem setarim yin'am — “and food eaten in secret is delicious.” Sathar (H5641, hidden, secret — the verb for concealment) applied to food: the appeal of Folly is not that she offers better food than Wisdom but that the hiddenness and illicitness add a false sweetness. The wisdom tradition’s realism: transgression feels sweet in the moment.
9.18: ve-lo' yada' ki refa'im sham — “but he does not know that the Rephaim are there.” Refa'im (H7496, the shades/ghosts of the dead, those in Sheol) are the final revelation about Folly’s house: it is populated by the dead. Be-'imqei she'ol qeru'eyha — “that those she has invited are in the depths of Sheol.” The house of Folly is not a feast-hall but Sheol itself; her invitation is an invitation to death wearing the language of delight. This final verse closes the structural envelope of chs 7-9: the adulteress’s house leads to Sheol (7:27), and Folly’s house is Sheol. Wisdom’s house leads to life (9:6, 11); folly’s house leads to death (9:18).

10.1: Proverbs of Solomon (10:1) — a new collection heading; distinguishes this antithetical-couplet corpus from the discourses of chs 1–9. The bēn ḥākām / bēn kĕsîl (wise son / fool son) contrast sets the collection's dominant register: wisdom is relational and has social consequences, beginning in the home.
10.2: ṣĕdāqāh tassîl mimmāwet — “righteousness delivers from death.” Ṣĕdāqāh (H6666) has a semantic range from judicial rectitude to generosity/almsgiving; in rabbinic Hebrew it narrowed to charity. The MKT renders “righteousness” to preserve the forensic and moral range. “Delivers from death” (tassîl mimmāwet) is the Proverbs life/death binary: wisdom-aligned conduct leads away from the trajectory of Sheol.
10.3: lōʾ-yaʿăzōb YHWH nepeš ṣaddîq — “the LORD does not let the righteous go hungry.” Literally, “YHWH will not let the soul of the righteous go hungry (rāʿāb).” Nepeš is frequently rendered “soul” but here functions as the vitalizing appetite of the whole person. Hāwat rĕšāʿîm (the craving of the wicked) uses hawwāh — desire bordering on greed — which YHWH actively frustrates.
10.4: rāš ʿōśê kap rĕmiyyāh — a slack (rĕmiyyāh) hand brings poverty. Rĕmiyyāh can mean both “slack/lazy” and “deceitful/treacherous” — the same word used of the false balance in 11:1. The pun is live: slackness is a form of deception, pretending to work.
10.5: ʿōśê māśkîl — “a wise son gathers in summer” (lit. “acts wisely”). The agricultural image (qāṣîr, harvest) grounds wisdom in observable seasonal reality — there is a right time for action. Mēbîš (causes shame) is the consistent antonym of wisdom in Proverbs: shame is the social consequence of the fool’s failure to read time and circumstances.
10.6: bĕrākôt lĕrōʾš ṣaddîq — “blessings crown the head of the righteous.” Bĕrākāh (blessing) flows naturally to the righteous person; the chiastic second half — “the mouth of the wicked conceals violence (ḥāmās)” — contrasts blessings on the head with violence hidden in the mouth. Words both reveal and conceal character.
10.7: zēker ṣaddîq liḇrākāh — “the memory of the righteous is a blessing.” The verse affirms that moral reputation outlasts life itself: the righteous leave a name that blesses posterity, while the wicked’s name rots (yirqāb). This is not immortality but šēm-honor — the blessing of being remembered well.
10.8: ḥakam-lēb yiqqaḥ miṣwôt — “the wise in heart (lēb) accepts commands.” Lēb (heart/mind) is the Proverbs seat of will and understanding together; wisdom is not abstract intelligence but integrated character. Yiqqaḥ miṣwôt (receives commands) contrasts with the babbling fool (ʾĕwîl śĕpātayim) who talks rather than heeds.
10.9: hôlēk battōm yēlēk beṭaḥ — “whoever walks in integrity (tōm) walks securely (beṭaḥ).” Tōm (integrity/completeness) is used of complete fidelity in covenant contexts. Beṭaḥ (security, trust) is the condition of the one whose path is not crooked; the double-walk (hôlēk... yēlēk) emphasizes that integrity and security travel together.
10.10: qōrēṣ ʿāyin — “the one who winks with malice.” The eye-wink (qāraṣ ʿayin) signals deceptive intent (cf. 6:13); it communicates what is not said aloud, making the winking person a disruptor of communal trust. The fool who babbles (repeated from v8) disrupts trust differently — with too many words rather than too few.
10.11: maqqôr ḥayyîm — “fountain of life.” The mouth-as-fountain image is central to Proverbs’ speech theology: the righteous person’s words are life-giving like a spring. The image recurs at 13:14; 14:27; 16:22 (each time as maqqôr ḥayyîm). The wicked’s mouth, by contrast, “conceals violence (ḥāmās)” — the same word used for pre-flood human wickedness (Gen 6:11).
10.12: ḥesed tĕkassē kol-pĕšāʿîm — “love covers all offenses.” Ḥesed (H2617; dispute_level=4) is one of the most theologically loaded Hebrew words: covenant loyalty, steadfast love, mercy. The MKT renders “love” here to capture both the relational and affective dimensions. The verse is quoted directly in 1 Pet 4:8, applied to fervent love among believers. The covering of offenses is not blind indifference but actively absorbing conflict rather than inflaming it.
10.13: ḥokmāh... śĕbĕṭ lĕgēw ḥăsar-lēb — wisdom on the lips of the discerning; a rod for the back of those who lack lēb (heart/understanding). The rod is not punitive cruelty but the corrective consequence designed to produce the understanding the fool refuses to acquire through instruction. Wisdom is the default state of the discerning; corporal correction is the fallback.
10.14: ḥăkāmîm yiṣpĕnû dāʿat — “wise people store up knowledge.” Ṣāpan (store, hide) — the same verb used for Moses’ mother hiding him (Exod 2:2) — suggests that knowledge is a strategic reserve, not for display. The fool’s mouth, by contrast, is mĕḥittāh qĕrōbāh (destruction near): voluble ignorance brings harm down quickly.
10.15: hôn ʿāšîr qiryat ʿuzzô — “a wealthy person’s riches are like a fortified city (qiryat ʿuzzô).” The verse observes social reality without endorsing it: wealth functions as protection, while poverty is the poor man’s ruin. The observation is later complicated by v22 (the blessing of YHWH makes truly rich) and 11:4 (wealth is useless on the day of wrath).
10.16: pĕʿullat ṣaddîq lĕḥayyîm — “the earnings of the righteous lead to life (ḥayyîm).” The ṣaddîq / rāšāʿ binary extends into economics: the righteous person’s labor produces life, while the wicked’s income (tĕbûʾat rāšāʿ) leads to sin. The point is not that poverty is sinful but that money is morally directional.
10.17: ʾōraḥ lĕḥayyîm šômēr mûsār — “whoever keeps instruction (mûsār) is on the path of life (ʾōraḥ ḥayyîm).” Mûsār is disciplinary instruction (from yāsar, to chastise/correct) — formation through correction. The path-of-life formula (also 6:23; Ps 16:11) is a major organizing metaphor of wisdom: life is a road, and attending to correction keeps the traveler on the right one.
10.18: mĕkassē śinaʾāh śiprê šāqer — “whoever conceals hatred has lying lips.” The verse probes the relationship between speech and inner reality: the person who hides hatred through polished speech is a liar by definition. Śinaʾāh (hatred) beneath smooth words is the precise dynamics of hypocrisy; the second line compounds it with slander (môṣîʾ dibbāh — spreading an evil report).
10.19: bĕrōb dĕbārîm lōʾ-yĕḥdal pāšaʿ — “when words are many, sin is not absent.” One of Proverbs’ most concise speech-ethics axioms. Ḥōśēk śĕpātāyw maskîl — “whoever restrains his lips is prudent.” Ḥāśak (restrain, hold back) is active verbal discipline. The proverb captures a consistent wisdom insight: more words = more opportunity for sin; restraint = wisdom.
10.20: kesep niḇḥār lĕšôn ṣaddîq — “the tongue of the righteous is like choice silver.” The tongue-as-precious-metal image measures the value of righteous speech: rare, refined, and valuable. The heart of the wicked, by contrast, has kimʿāṭ (little value). The contrast is between refined utterance and worthless interior disposition.
10.21: śiptê ṣaddîq yirʿû rabbîm — “the lips of the righteous feed many.” Rāʿāh (to feed/shepherd) extends the agricultural/pastoral register: righteous speech is pastoral, sustaining a community. Fools, by contrast, die (yāmûtû) for lack of heart (lēb). The inversion is sharp: the righteous nourish others while fools cannot even sustain themselves.
10.22: bĕrakat YHWH hîʾ taʿăšîr — “the blessing of YHWH is what truly makes rich.” The adverb hîʾ (it is this that) places the emphasis: not diligence alone, not strategy, but YHWH’s blessing. Wĕlōʾ-yôsip ʿeṣeb ʿimmāh (he adds no sorrow to it) — the blessing of YHWH comes without the grief that accompanies wealth gained by other means (cf. v15, where riches without YHWH’s blessing are a fragile fortress).
10.23: kimśĕḥōq lĕkĕsîl ʿăśôt zimmāh — “doing wrong is like a game to a fool.” Zimmāh (wicked scheme/lewdness) is treated as entertainment (śĕḥōq, laughter) by the fool. The serious moral weight of evil is invisible to those without wisdom. By contrast, wisdom (ḥokmāh) is the śimḥāh (delight) of the person of understanding — their genuine pleasure.
10.24: megôrat rāšāʿ hîʾ tĕbôʾennû — “what the wicked fears will come upon him.” The self-fulfilling nature of the wicked person’s trajectory: their megôrāh (dread, the thing dreaded) comes to them. The contrast with the righteous whose longing (taʾăwat ṣaddîqîm) is granted positions desire and dread as directional: you receive what you are oriented toward.
10.25: kĕʿābōr sûpāh wĕʾên rāšāʿ wĕṣaddîq yĕsôd ʿôlam — “when the storm passes, the wicked are gone; the righteous stand on an everlasting foundation (yĕsôd ʿôlam).” The storm metaphor is eschatological in Proverbs: ultimate stability belongs to the righteous because their foundation is ʿôlam (eternal/lasting). The wicked have no such anchor; they are swept away.
10.26: kĕḥōmeṣ lĕšinnayim ûkĕʿāšān lĕʿênāyim — “like vinegar to the teeth and smoke to the eyes.” The uncomfortable sensory images (acid on teeth, irritating smoke) capture the frustration of relying on a lazy person. The proverb uses physical discomfort as the experiential equivalent of the social cost of laziness.
10.27: yirʾat YHWH tôsîp yāmîm — “the fear of YHWH (yirʾat YHWH) adds length to life.” The fear-of-YHWH formula is Proverbs’ epistemological anchor (1:7; 9:10); here it has a directly temporal consequence: added days. The wicked’s years are yiqṣōrû (cut short) — the life/death binary expressed in longevity terms.
10.28: tôḥelet ṣaddîqîm śimḥāh — “the hope of the righteous brings joy.” Tôḥelet (hope, expectation) in Proverbs is not wishful thinking but confident anticipation rooted in YHWH’s ordering of the world. The wicked’s expectation (tiqwat rĕšāʿîm) comes to nothing (tōʾbad) — not because hope is wrong but because their hope is misplaced.
10.29: māʿôz lattōm derek YHWH — “the LORD’s way (derek YHWH) is a stronghold (māʿôz) for the blameless.” The divine way — YHWH’s path of governance — is simultaneously shelter for those who walk it and ruin (mĕḥittāh) for those who work evil. The same divine order that protects the righteous destroys the wicked.
10.30: ṣaddîq lĕʿôlam bal-yimmôṭ — “the righteous will never be dislodged (yimmôṭ).” Mōṭ (to totter, slip, be dislodged) is used of the unstable; the righteous have secure footing (lĕʿôlam, forever). The wicked will not inhabit the land (ereṣ) — reversing the covenant promise; evil disrupts the conditions of the covenantal land.
10.31: pî ṣaddîq yānûb ḥokmāh — “the mouth of the righteous produces wisdom.” Nûb (to bear fruit) is an agricultural verb applied to speech — the mouth of the righteous is a fruit-bearing plant. The perverse tongue (tahpûkôt) will be cut off (tikārēt). The speech-plant metaphor that ends ch10 anticipates 11:30’s “fruit of the righteous is a tree of life.”
10.32: śiptê ṣaddîq yēdĕʿûn rāṣôn — “the lips of the righteous know what is pleasing (rāṣôn).” Rāṣôn (pleasure, favor, delight) implies social and theological attunement: the righteous person’s speech is pleasing to YHWH and to people because it is rightly calibrated. The wicked’s mouth speaks only tahpûkôt (perversities, turnings-upside-down).

11.1: môʾznê mirmāh tôʿăbat YHWH — “dishonest scales are detestable (tôʿăbāh) to YHWH.” Môʾznê mirmāh (deceitful balances) is a marketplace-ethics formula recurring across the ANE; the OT uniquely grounds market honesty in YHWH’s character rather than social convention (cf. Lev 19:36; Deut 25:13-15; Mic 6:11). ʾeben šĕlēmāh (accurate/complete weight, lit. “whole stone”) — completeness (šālēm) in measurement reflects divine wholeness.
11.2: bāʾ zādôn wayyābōʾ qālôn — “when pride (zādôn) arrives, disgrace follows.” Zādôn (presumptuous pride, arrogance) is the pride of a person who oversteps their creaturely limits. Ṣānûʿîm ḥokmāh (with the humble is wisdom) — ṣānûaʿ (humble, modest) occurs only here and Mic 6:8 (“walk humbly with your God”). Wisdom accompanies this specific form of lowliness — not servile but rightly measured.
11.3: tummâ yĕšārîm tanḥēm — “the integrity (tummāh) of the upright guides them.” Tummāh (completeness, wholeness, integrity) is the noun form of tōm (10:9); it functions as an internal guide — the integrated person is self-directing in the right path. The treacherous (bōgedîm) are destroyed by their own treachery’s perversion of their path.
11.4: lōʾ-yôʿîl hôn bĕyôm ʿebrāh — “wealth is of no use on the day of wrath (yôm ʿebrāh).” Yôm ʿebrāh (day of wrath/overflow) appears in Zeph 1:15-18 as the eschatological day of judgment; Proverbs uses the phrase for the moment when divine governance reasserts itself decisively against accumulated wickedness. On that day, ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness) alone delivers from death — echoing 10:2.
11.5: ṣidqat tāmîm tĕyaššēr darkô — “the righteousness of the blameless (tāmîm) makes his way straight (tĕyaššēr).” Tāmîm (blameless, complete, whole) is the cultic-moral ideal for the person in covenant with YHWH (Gen 17:1; Job 1:1). The straight path is not self-achieved but is the consequence of integrated righteousness; the wicked fall by their own wickedness — a self-undoing.
11.6: ṣidqat yĕšārîm taṣṣîlēm — the righteousness of the upright rescues them. The verse parallels 10:2 with taṣṣîlēm (rescues them) where the object is plural — righteousness is communal rescue as well as individual. The treacherous are caught by their own evil desire (ḥawwat — the same greedy-craving from 10:3), trapped by what they reached for.
11.7: bĕmôt ʾādām rāšāʿ tōʾbad tiqwāh — “when a wicked man dies, his expectation (tiqwāh) perishes.” Tiqwāh (hope, a thing hoped for; lit. the string of hope) dissolves at death for the wicked because their hope was grounded in the present order. Wĕtôḥelet ʾônim ʾābādāh (the hope of wrongdoers comes to nothing) — both the wicked’s expectation and the results they counted on evaporate.
11.8: ṣaddîq miṣṣārāh neḥĕlāṣ — “the righteous are rescued (neḥĕlāṣ) from trouble.” Ḥālaṣ (to draw out, rescue) is a rescue vocabulary word used of divine deliverance. The wicked take the righteous’s place in the trouble — a substitutionary exchange in reverse of what is expected; the moral order eventually redirects suffering toward its true source.
11.9: bĕpeh ḥānēp yašḥit rēʿēhû — “through his words the godless person (ḥānēp) destroys his neighbor.” Ḥānēp (godless, profane, one who has corrupted their relation to the sacred) is a stronger term than mere wickedness — it implies deliberate sacrilege. Their speech is destructive; the righteous are saved bĕdaʿat (through knowledge/discernment) — knowledge as shield.
11.10: bĕṭûb ṣaddîqîm taʿălōz qiryāh — “when the righteous prosper, the city (qiryāh) rejoices.” The proverb locates moral character within communal space: the righteous person’s wellbeing (ṭûb) is civic property that generates urban joy. This is the social-capital dimension of wisdom: the righteous bless the city, the wicked degrade it.
11.11: bĕbirkat yĕšārîm tārûm qāret — “by the blessing of the upright the city is lifted up (tārûm).” Rûm (to be exalted, lifted up) in this civic context suggests moral elevation — the city rises through the quality of its people’s speech and character. The wicked’s mouth tears it down — the city’s fate is determined by its speech environment.
11.12: bāz lĕrēʿēhû ḥăsar-lēb — “whoever despises his neighbor lacks good sense (ḥăsar-lēb).” Bāzāh (to despise, treat as worthless) applied to a neighbor is the mark of heart-deficiency. ʾîš tĕbûnôt yaḥărîš (a person of understanding stays silent) — restraint is the mark of tĕbûnāh (discernment); silence in the face of a neighbor’s faults is more sophisticated than contempt.
11.13: hôlēk rākîl mĕgalleh-sôd — “whoever goes about gossiping (rākîl) reveals secrets.” Rākîl (a slanderer/gossip) is from a root meaning “to go about as a trader” — the gossip traffics in secrets as a merchant traffics in goods. Neʾĕman-rûaḥ (faithful of spirit) keeps confidence (mĕkassēh sôd) — covers/hides the secret rather than trading in it.
11.14: bĕʾên taḥbûlôt yippol ʿām — “without guidance (taḥbûlôt), a people falls.” Taḥbûlôt (pl. of taḥbûlāh) is the nautical term for the ropes used to steer a ship — plural because effective governance requires multiple steering mechanisms. Tĕšûʿāh (safety/deliverance — from the root yāšaʿ, same as yĕšûʿāh/salvation) comes with many counselors.
11.15: rāʿ yērōaʿ kî ʿārab zār — “whoever gives surety for a stranger will suffer harm.” The surety-for-a-stranger warning recurs in Proverbs (6:1-5; 17:18; 20:16; 22:26-27) — financial entanglement with unknown parties is consistently presented as a wisdom failure, not just financial risk. Śōnēʾ tĕqîʿôt bōṭēaḥ (whoever refuses such pledges is safe) — the safe path is refusal of the obligation.
11.16: ʾēšet ḥēn titmlĕk kābôd — “a gracious (ḥēn) woman gains honor.” Ḥēn is unearned favor; the gracious woman attracts honor (kābôd) through her relational quality rather than force. Ruthless men (ʿārîṣîm) gain only wealth (ʿōšer) — the contrast of honor versus money tracks a deeper wisdom distinction between what is intrinsically valuable and what is merely economically useful.
11.17: gōmēl napšô ʾîš ḥāsed — “a kind man benefits himself (napšô).” Nepeš here functions reflexively (himself, his own person) rather than as soul or appetite. Ḥāsed (lovingkindness) enacted toward others returns on the practitioner. The cruel person (ʾakzārî) disturbs his own flesh (šĕʾērô) — his cruelty circles back to harm him at a visceral level.
11.18: rāšāʿ ʿōśē pĕʿullat šeqer — “the wicked earns deceptive wages.” Šeqer (falsehood, deceptiveness) as a modifier for wages: what the wicked earns is inherently unreliable. By contrast, zōrēaʿ ṣĕdāqāh śekar ʾĕmet — “whoever sows righteousness gains a sure (ʾĕmet) reward.” Śekar ʾĕmet (wage of truth/reliability) — the harvest metaphor for moral consequence.
11.19: kēn ṣĕdāqāh lĕḥayyîm — “genuine righteousness leads to life.” The adverb kēn (so, truly, genuinely) stresses the authentic quality of the righteousness that produces life — not performance but substance. Ûmĕradef rāʿāh lĕmôtô — “whoever pursues evil runs toward his own death.” The active pursuit (rādap, to chase/hunt) of evil is self-directed death-seeking.
11.20: tôʿăbat YHWH ʿiqqĕšê-lēb — “those with crooked hearts (ʿiqqĕšê-lēb) are detestable to YHWH.” ʿIqqēš (crooked, twisted, perverse) denotes structural distortion of the lēb (heart/will). YHWH’s delight (rĕṣônô) is in those whose way (derek) is blameless (tĕmîmê-derek) — the perfect path arising from an integrated character.
11.21: yād lĕyād lōʾ-yinnāqeh rāʿ — “hand to hand, the wicked will not go unpunished.” The phrase yād lĕyād (hand to hand) may describe conspiring (joining hands to plot evil) or simply emphasizes certainty (you can count on it). Wĕzera ṣaddîqîm nimlāṭ (the offspring of the righteous will escape) — the righteous person’s moral legacy extends to their children.
11.22: nēzem zāhāb bĕʾap ḥăzîr — “like a gold ring in a pig’s snout.” The deliberately jarring image — expensive jewelry in an unclean animal — captures the waste of beauty without discretion (ṭaʿam, taste/judgment). Physical beauty (yāpāh) is valuable but misplaced without the wisdom to use it well. The proverb is not misogynistic but is making a point about the relationship between surface qualities and character.
11.23: taʾăwat ṣaddîqîm ʾak-ṭôb — “the desire (taʾăwāh) of the righteous ends in good (ṭôb).” The righteous person’s desires are themselves righteous — the integration of character means that what they want is what is good. The wicked’s hope (tiqwat rĕšāʿîm) brings wrath — the word ʿebrāh (wrath/overflow) connects to 11:4’s day of wrath.
11.24: yēš mĕpazzēr wĕnôsāp ʿôd — “one person gives freely and grows richer.” One of Proverbs’ most counter-intuitive economic observations: liberality (pāzar, scatter/disperse) increases, while withholding (ḥāśak from the proper amount) leads to want. The paradox of generous economics reflects a wisdom understanding of bĕrākāh (blessing) as replenishing rather than depleting.
11.25: nepeš bĕrākāh tĕduššān — “a generous person (nepeš bĕrākāh, a soul of blessing) will be enriched.” The phrase nepeš bĕrākāh (soul/person of blessing) is unusual — the person has become so identified with generosity that blessing is their very nature. Ûmarweh gam-hûʾ yôreh — “whoever waters others will himself be watered.” The agricultural metaphor of irrigation applied to social generosity.
11.26: mônēaʿ bār yiqbûhû lĕʾôm — “the people curse whoever hoards grain.” Qābab (to curse) reflects the social contract around food distribution in an agrarian community: grain-hoarding during scarcity violates communal covenant and brings collective cursing. Bĕrākāh lĕrōʾš maśbîr (blessings on the head of the one who sells) — generosity in the market is a communal blessing.
11.27: šōḥēr ṭôb yĕbaqqēš rāṣôn — “whoever earnestly seeks good (ṭôb) will gain favor (rāṣôn).” Šāḥar (to seek eagerly, seek at dawn — same root as šaḥar, dawn) implies an early and earnest seeking. Seeking rāʿāh (evil) brings the same evil on the seeker — the moral reciprocity principle that structures chs 10-12.
11.28: bôṭēaḥ bĕʿošrô hûʾ yippōl — “whoever trusts (bāṭaḥ) in his wealth will fall.” Bāṭaḥ (to trust, rely on) is the key covenantal-trust verb usually directed toward YHWH (Ps 22:5; 37:3); trusting in wealth redirects it from YHWH to mammon. Kĕʿāleh yipraḥ ṣaddîqîm (the righteous will flourish like a green leaf/branch) — ʿāleh (fresh leaf) suggests vibrant, life-connected growth.
11.29: ʿōkēr bêtô yinḥal rûaḥ — “whoever brings trouble on his own household will inherit the wind.” ʿākar (to trouble, disturb — used of Achan in Josh 7:25-26) and rûaḥ (wind, breath) — nothing to show, nothing to hold. Wĕʿebed ḥākām liḇnê-bôšet (the fool ends up serving the wise) — the social reversal: the household-destroyer becomes a servant.
11.30: pĕrî-ṣaddîq ʿēṣ ḥayyîm — “the fruit of the righteous is a tree of life (ʿēṣ ḥayyîm).” The ʿēṣ ḥayyîm (tree of life) links back to Gen 2-3 and forward to Rev 22:2; Proverbs uses it in 3:18 (wisdom is a tree of life) and 15:4 (a healing tongue). Here the righteous person’s pĕrî (fruit, produce — their deeds and influence) is itself life-giving to others. Lōqēaḥ nĕpāšôt ḥākām (the wise wins souls) — lāqaḥ as capture/take; winning people to wisdom.
11.31: hēn ṣaddîq bāʾāreṣ yĕšullām — “if the righteous receive their due on earth (bāʾāreṣ).” The qal wāḥōmer (light-to-heavy argument): if even the righteous receive consequences on earth, how much more the wicked and the sinner. The verse does not say the righteous deserve punishment but that the earthly order renders judgment on all; the wicked’s reckoning is therefore assured.

12.1: ʾōhēb mûsār ʾōhēb dāʿat — “whoever loves discipline (mûsār) loves knowledge (dāʿat).” The love of mûsār (corrective instruction) is the pragmatic test of whether someone genuinely wants knowledge. Śōnēʾ tôkaḥat baʿar (whoever hates correction is without understanding — lit. “is stupid/brutish”) — baʿar (H1197, brutish, like a dumb animal) is the strongest negative term in Proverbs’ vocabulary for intellectual deficiency.
12.2: ṭôb yāpîq rāṣôn mēʾet YHWH — “a good man obtains favor (rāṣôn) from YHWH.” Pûq rāṣôn (to bring forth favor) — pûq is to bring out a supply, suggesting favor is available and the good person draws on it. Wĕʾîš mĕzimmôt yarśîaʿ (a man who schemes wickedly he condemns) — YHWH’s condemnation is the decisive reversal of the schemer’s plans.
12.3: lōʾ-yikkôn ʾādām bĕrešaʿ — “no one is made secure by wickedness.” Kûn (to be established, secured, firm) in the niphal is the word for a stable foundation; wickedness cannot produce it. Wĕšōreš ṣaddîqîm bal-yimmôṭ (the root of the righteous will not be shaken) — the root (šōreš) image is organic and deep; the righteous person is grounded where wickedness cannot go.
12.4: ʾēšet ḥayil ʿăteret baʿlāh — “an excellent wife (ʾēšet ḥayil) is a crown to her husband.” Ḥayil (valor, strength, ability) in ʾēšet ḥayil is the same phrase used for the Woman of Valor in 31:10 — the ideal of full competence and character. Ûkĕrāqāb bĕʿaṣmôtāyw (shame is like decay in his bones) — the contrast of crown-honor and bone-rot is deliberately visceral.
12.5: maḥšĕbôt ṣaddîqîm mišpāṭ — “the plans of the righteous are just (mišpāṭ).” Maḥšābet (plan, thought) as the interior level of moral action: the righteous person’s thinking itself is justice-shaped. The schemes (taḥbûlôt) of the wicked are deceitful (mirmāh) — the same word used for dishonest scales in 11:1.
12.6: diḇrê rĕšāʿîm ʾĕrob-dām — “the words of the wicked set an ambush for blood (ʾĕrob-dām).” The wicked person’s speech is predatory — an ambush laid with words. Pî yĕšārîm yaṣṣîlēm (the mouth of the upright delivers people) — the same mouth-as-agent that ambushes in the wicked rescues in the upright.
12.7: hāpok rĕšāʿîm wĕʾênām — “the wicked are overthrown and are gone.” Hāpak (to overturn, be overthrown) is the verb of Sodom’s destruction (Gen 19:29; Isa 13:19) — the wicked person’s end is a catastrophic overturning with no recovery. Bêt ṣaddîqîm yaʿămōd (the house of the righteous stands firm) — the household as the durable unit of righteous life.
12.8: lĕpî-śiklô yĕhullal ʾîš — “a man is praised according to his wisdom (śekel).” Śekel (prudence, good sense) is the practical-intelligence dimension of wisdom. Naʿăwē-lēb (twisted of mind/heart) is the antonym — structural deformation of the lēb that produces contempt (bāzāh) from others. Reputation tracks inner character.
12.9: ṭôb niqləh wĕʿebed lô — “it is better to be of humble standing and have a servant than to put on airs (mitkabbēd) and lack bread.” The proverb punctures pretension: self-aggrandizement (kābad in the hitpael — presenting oneself as honored) that is materially empty is worse than genuine but modest sufficiency. Reality outranks performance.
12.10: yôdēaʿ ṣaddîq nepeš bĕhemtô — “a righteous man cares for the wellbeing (nepeš) of his animal.” Nepeš applied to the animal — the righteous person attends to the animal’s vitality and appetite. Wĕraḥămê rĕšāʿîm ʾakzārî (even the compassion of the wicked is cruel) — the wicked person’s apparent mercy contains cruelty; character shapes every act.
12.11: ʿōbēd ʾadmātô yiśbaʿ lāḥem — “whoever works his land will have plenty of bread.” The agricultural work-ethic proverb: tilling the land (ʿābad ʾadāmāh) produces bread (śābaʿ lāḥem). Mĕraḥēp rêqîm ḥăsar-lēb (whoever chases empty pursuits lacks heart/sense) — rêqîm (empty things, vain pursuits) is the pursuit of what cannot sustain.
12.12: ḥāmad rāšāʿ mĕṣôd rāʿîm — “the wicked craves what evil people catch.” Ḥāmad (to desire covetously — used in the Decalogue, Exod 20:17) applied to the wicked person’s desire for the prey of other evil people — coveting even the fruits of evil. Šōreš ṣaddîqîm yittēn (the root of the righteous produces its own fruit) — the righteous person generates their own fruitfulness, not dependent on predatory acquisition.
12.13: bĕpeša śĕpātayim môqēš rāʿ — “an evil man is trapped (môqēš, a snare) by the sins of his lips.” Speech as self-entrapment: the evil person’s own words form the snare. Wĕyēṣē miṣṣārāh ṣaddîq (the righteous escapes from trouble) — the juxtaposition of the wicked trapped by speech and the righteous escaping trouble highlights the moral asymmetry in crisis.
12.14: miprî pî-ʾîš yiśbaʿ-ṭôb — “from the fruit of his words a man is satisfied with good.” The mouth-as-fruit-tree image of 11:30 extended to the satisfaction (śābaʿ) of the speaker himself. Ûpĕʿullat yĕdê ʾādām tāšûb lô (the work of his hands comes back to him) — the reciprocity principle: both verbal and manual labor return their quality to the agent.
12.15: derek ʾĕwîl yāšār bĕʿênāyw — “the path of a fool seems right (yāšār) in his own eyes.” Self-perception as epistemological trap: the fool’s path is subjectively right because he lacks the external correction that exposes its error. Wĕšômēaʿ lāʿēṣāh ḥākām (a wise person listens to counsel) — receptivity to external correction is the distinguishing mark of wisdom over folly.
12.16: ʾĕwîl bayyôm yiwwādaʿ kaʿasô — “a fool’s annoyance (kaʿas) is known at once.” Kaʿas (vexation, anger, irritation) is immediately public when it belongs to the fool — no filter between inner state and outer expression. Wĕkōsē qālôn ʿārûm (a prudent person overlooks an insult) — kāsāh (to cover) is the same verb used of love covering offenses in 10:12; the prudent person applies the same covering to minor social slights.
12.17: yāpîaḥ ʾĕmûnāh yaggîd ṣedeq — “a truthful witness (ʿēd ʾĕmet) gives honest testimony (ṣedeq).” ʾĂmûnāh (faithfulness, reliability) is the character quality behind truthful testimony; the true witness’s words are ṣedeq (right, just). Wĕʿēd šĕqārîm mirmāh (a false witness deceitfulness) — the false witness’s core is mirmāh (treachery, the dishonest-scales word of 11:1).
12.18: yēš bôṭē dōbĕr kĕmadqĕrôt ḥāreb — “reckless words (bôṭē) pierce like sword-thrusts (madqĕrôt ḥāreb).” Bāṭāh (to speak rashly/thoughtlessly) creates word-weapons: the image of being stabbed by speech captures the visceral damage of careless talk. Wĕlāšôn ḥăkāmîm marpēʾ (the tongue of the wise brings healing — marpēʾ) — the same tongue-instrument that pierces in the reckless heals in the wise.
12.19: śĕpat ʾĕmet tikkôn lāʿad — “truthful lips stand firm (tikkôn) forever (lāʿad).” Kûn in the niphal (to be established, firm) applied to truthful lips: truth has ontological staying power — it is established in the order of things. Wĕʿad-ʾargeʿāh lĕšôn šāqer (a lying tongue lasts only a moment) — ʾargeʿāh (a moment, a wink of time) is the lifespan of the lie.
12.20: mirmāh bĕlēb ḥōrĕšê rāʿ — “deceit fills the heart of those who plan evil.” Ḥāraš (to plow, to devise) — the agricultural metaphor of plowing applied to devising evil; the heart-field is plowed with deceit. Wĕliôʿăṣê šālôm śimḥāh (those who counsel peace find joy) — šālôm as the goal of speech acts and its reward being śimḥāh (genuine joy).
12.21: lōʾ-yĕʾunneh laṣṣaddîq kol-ʾāwen — “no harm (ʾāwen) happens to the righteous.” ʾĀwen (trouble, wickedness, iniquity) in the sense of harm that befalls one. The proverb is experiential wisdom, not a metaphysical guarantee; its counterpart is that the wicked are filled with trouble (rāʿāh). The righteous person’s moral alignment creates a form of protection.
12.22: tôʿăbat YHWH śiprê šāqer — “lying lips are detestable (tôʿăbāh) to YHWH.” The tôʿăbāh-formula (applied to dishonest scales in 11:1; to lying lips here) marks the most serious moral violations — those that are not merely unwise but abhorrent to divine character. Wĕʿōśê ʾĕmûnāh rĕṣônô (those who act faithfully are his delight) — ʾĕmûnāh as faithful reliability is the positive pole of YHWH’s pleasure.
12.23: ʾādām ʿārûm kōsē dāʿat — “a prudent man (ʿārûm) conceals what he knows.” ʿĀrûm (shrewd, prudent) in Proverbs is consistently positive (unlike Gen 3:1 where the serpent is ʿārûm as cunning); the positive ʿārûm conceals knowledge strategically — not deception but disciplined restraint. Wĕlēb kĕsîlîm yiqrāʾ ʾiwwelet (the heart of a fool broadcasts its folly) — folly calls out, wisdom is silent.
12.24: yad ḥārûṣîm timšôl — “the diligent (ḥārûṣ, lit. sharp/incised, hence eager) hand will rule.” Diligence leads to authority; the lazy (rĕmiyyāh) will be put to forced labor (mas) — the reversal of the expected social position. Mas is tribute-labor, typically imposed on the conquered; the lazy person’s fate is the lowest social tier.
12.25: dĕʾāgāh bĕlēb-ʾîš yašḥennāh — “anxiety (dĕʾāgāh) in a man’s heart weighs him down.” Dĕʾāgāh (anxious care, sorrow) bends (šāḥāh) the heart; the remedy is a ṭôb-dābār (a good word) — a spoken encouragement that makes glad (yĕśamĕḥennāh). The proverb captures the therapeutic power of speech as direct pastoral medicine.
12.26: yātēr mērēʿēhû ṣaddîq — “the righteous man guides his neighbor well.” Yātēr (to lead, guide) from tûr (to explore, search out a path) — the righteous person scouts the path for others. Wĕderek rĕšāʿîm taṭʿēm (the way of the wicked leads them astray) — tāʿāh (to wander, go astray) as the destination of the wicked person’s path: leading followers into error.
12.27: lōʾ-yaḥărōk rĕmiyyāh ṣêdô — “the lazy man does not even bother to prepare his game.” Ḥārak (to roast/singe) — the lazy person captures prey but does not bother to cook it: slackness at every stage, not only in the initial effort. Wĕhôn-ʾādām yāqār ḥārûṣ (the wealth of a diligent man is a precious thing) — diligence makes ordinary wealth precious.
12.28: bĕʾōraḥ ṣĕdāqāh ḥayyîm — “in the path of righteousness is life.” The climactic statement of ch12 and a summary of the two-ways theme across chs 10-12: righteousness, wisdom, and the path of life are the same road. Wĕderek nĕtîbāh ʾal-māwet — “and along that road there is no death.” ʾAl-māwet (no-death) is the fullest expression of the life promised to the wise; the road of righteousness is defined by the absence of the death-trajectory.

13.1: מוּסָר (mûsār) is the dominant wisdom term for disciplinary instruction — not merely the transfer of information but formative correction that shapes character through consequence. The ben ḥākām (wise son) heeds it; the lēṣ (mocker/scorner) does not even respond to gəʿārâ (rebuke, a sharp verbal correction). The antithesis is absolute: wisdom begins with teachability.
13.2: נֶפֶשׁ (nephesh) appears twice — the appetite-soul of a person satisfied by the fruit of words (13:2a) and the appetite-soul of the treacherous that craves ḥāmās (violence). The word covers both the deepest self and its hungers; what the inner person feeds on shapes what it becomes. The MKT renders both occurrences as "soul," preserving this continuity.
13.3: שֹׁמֵר פִּיו (guard/watch his mouth) uses the watchman root šāmar — active vigilance, not passive restraint. The contrast is pōśēq śəpātāyw (one who opens wide his lips): the participle pōśēq implies a permanent disposition, a mouth habitually open. The consequence — mehittâ (ruin/terror) — intensifies this: careless speech is not just socially costly but destructive.
13.4: נֶפֶשׁ עָצֵל (soul of the lazy) אִוָּתָה (craves, Hithpael of ʾāwâ) — the reflexive stem intensifies the desire: the lazy person craves intensely, but wəʾayin (and nothing). The nephesh ḥārûṣîm (soul of the diligent) is richly satisfied (dušenâ, fat/prosperous). The diligent and the lazy both have deep desire; only one of them acts on it.
13.5: שֶׁקֶר (šeqer, falsehood/lie) is what the righteous person śānēʾ (hates) — hate is the correct moral response to deception. The wicked person yaḇîš (causes shame/stench) and yaḥpîr (brings disgrace) — both verbs have roots in shame and putrefaction; the MKT "acts disgracefully" captures the sense.
13.6: צְדָקָה (ṣedāqâ) and רִשְׁעָה (rišʿâ) stand as polar abstractions — righteousness and wickedness as active forces that either guard or overthrow. Both are personified agents in the verse, not merely descriptions. The tammîm dārēk (one of blameless way) is protected by righteousness as though by a sentinel.
13.7: The verse is deliberately compressed: one person mitrōšēš (Hithpolel of rāšaš, acts rich) yet has nothing; another mitrôšēš (Hithpael of rûš, acts poor) yet has great wealth. The Hithpael stems signal self-presentation — the verse is about performance and appearance versus reality. Both verbs are rare; the contrast is ironic and the form deliberate.
13.8: כֹּפֶר (kōpher) is the ransom or redemption price — the same word used for the atonement payment in Exod 30:12 and for the price that averts a penalty. Here it is the rich person's option: wealth can buy off threats. The poor person, paradoxically, does not hear gəʿārâ (rebuke/threatening) at all — they have nothing to be taken, so they are not threatened. Wealth creates vulnerability as well as security.
13.9: אוֹר צַדִּיקִים (light of the righteous) יִשְׂמָח (rejoices/shines brightly) — the verb is the same as "rejoice"; the MKT "shines with joy" bridges the metaphor. The lamp of the wicked is yidʿak (snuffed out, extinguished) — a sudden, complete ending, not gradual dimming.
13.10: זָדוֹן (zādôn, presumptuous pride/arrogance) produces only maṣṣâ (contention/strife). The word translated "pride" carries the sense of deliberate, willful presumption — acting as though one does not need God or others. Wisdom (ḥokmâ) is found with those who niwwāʿăṣîm (take counsel together).
13.11: הוֹן מֵהֶבֶל (wealth from vanity/breath/nothing) — hebel is the same word Ecclesiastes uses for transience and futility; wealth gained that way will similarly vanish (yimʿāṭ, diminish). By contrast, qōbēṣ עַל-יָד (one who gathers hand-by-hand, gradually) will yarbeh (multiply/increase). The contrast is not merely honest vs. dishonest but patient accumulation vs. windfall seeking.
13.12: תּוֹחֶלֶת מְמֻשָּׁכָה (hope drawn out/prolonged) makes the heart ḥolâ (sick) — the verb root is illness, genuine malaise. The contrast is עֵץ חַיִּים (ʿēṣ ḥayyîm, tree of life) — the Genesis phrase (2:9; 3:22, 24) for the source of immortal life. A desire fulfilled carries the weight of restored access to life itself; the delay of hope inverts that promise.
13.13: בָּז לְדָבָר (despises the word/matter) — dābār can be word, commandment, or matter; the deliberate ambiguity covers both the spoken word and the commandment. יֵחָבֶל לוֹ (will be destroyed for it) — the Niphal passive: destruction comes to the person. יְרֵא מִצְוָה (fears/reverences the commandment) will be yəšullam (rewarded/made complete/paid in full).
13.14: תּוֹרַת חָכָם (tôrat ḥākām, teaching/torah of the wise) — the word tôrâ carries its full weight: not a suggestion but an authoritative instruction. It is məqôr ḥayyîm (fountain of life), the same phrase used in Ps 36:9 and Prov 14:27 and 16:22 for God himself as the source of life. The sage's instruction participates in that divine vitality.
13.15: שֵׂכֶל טוֹב (good understanding/insight) wins ḥēn (favor/grace/charm) — the same word used for unmerited divine favor in Gen 6:8 and Exod 33:12. The way of the treacherous (בּוֹגְדִים) is ʾêtān (enduring/constant) — the MKT "leads to their ruin" is an interpretation; the Hebrew says their way is "permanent/hard" (suggesting inescapability).
13.16: כָּל-עָרוּם (every prudent/crafty person) acts from dāʿat (knowledge) — the same root as the knowledge of good and evil; here used positively for deliberate, informed action. The fool "broadcasts his foolishness" — the verb yiphrōś (spreads out, makes known) implies involuntary display: the fool cannot help revealing what he is.
13.17: מַלְאָךְ רָשָׁע (messenger of wickedness) — the same word malʾāk used for divine angels; the context determines whether the messenger is holy or corrupt. A faithful envoy (ṣîr ʾĕmûnîm, ambassador of faithfulness) brings marpēʾ (healing/health) — the word covers both physical and relational restoration.
13.18: רֵישׁ וְקָלוֹן (poverty and shame/dishonor) — both are social states, not merely inner ones. The shame (qālôn) that comes with refusing mûsār is public degradation. By contrast, kābôd (honor/weight/glory) comes to the one who accepts tôkaḥat (correction/reproof) — the same word used for God's reproof in the Psalms.
13.19: תַּאֲוָה נִהְיָה (longing become/fulfilled) is sweet (ʿārēb) to the nephesh — something satisfied at the depth of the self. The second half: it is tôʿăbat kəsîlîm (abomination/detestable) to fools to turn from evil (sûr mērāʿ). The word tôʿăbâ (abomination) is usually what God finds detestable; here fools find the good itself detestable — the inversion is striking.
13.20: הוֹלֵךְ אֶת-חֲכָמִים יֶחְכָּם (walking with the wise, one becomes wise) — the Qal of ḥākam is not instantaneous; the participle walking suggests an ongoing process of formation. Companionship with fools (rōʿeh kəsîlîm) will be "shattered/broken" — the verb yērôaʿ does not mean merely "suffer harm" but implies the kind of rupture that cannot easily be repaired.
13.21: חַטָּאִים (sinners) are pursued (təraddēp) by calamity — the verb is the same used for military pursuit; sin is stalked by its consequences. The righteous will be yəšallēm-ṭôb (repaid with good) — šālem (wholeness/completion) as the root: the repayment brings them to completeness.
13.22: חֵיל חוֹטֵא (wealth of the sinner) is ṣāpûn laṣṣaddîq (stored up for the righteous) — the passive participle ṣāpûn suggests it has been laid away in secret, reserved. Providence steers the flow of accumulated wealth across generations. The word ṣaddîq occurs in both halves — the good man leaves an inheritance, and the righteous ultimately receive the sinner's hoarded wealth.
13.23: נִיר רָאשִׁים (fallow ground / tillage of the poor/heads) is uncertain — the word rāʾšîm can mean either "poor people" (from rûš) or "heads/chiefs." The MKT follows "poor" as more contextually consistent: their land can yield abundant food (ʾōkel) but it is swept away by lōʾ mišpāṭ (injustice, without justice) — the system, not the soil, is the problem.
13.24: שֵׁבֶט (šēbeṭ) is the rod/staff — the same word for a tribe's scepter and a shepherd's staff. Discipline and authority converge in the image. חוֹשֵׂךְ שִׁבְטוֹ (the one who withholds his rod) שׂוֹנֵא בְנוֹ (hates his son) — the equation is deliberately shocking. Love (ʾōhăbô, Qal participle, continuous love) disciplines bəšaḥar (diligently/from early on, while there is time).
13.25: נֶפֶשׁ צַדִּיק (soul of the righteous) תֹּאכַל לְשָׂבַע (eats to satisfaction) — the imperfect verb is customary: this is habitually true, not just once. The belly (beṭen) of the wicked lacks (teḥsar, is deficient) — the same word for the poverty of hunger. Material prosperity follows the contours of character in the wisdom worldview; the connection is structural, not superstitious.

14.1: חָכְמוֹת (ḥokmôt) is a plural form — often understood as a plural of majesty or intensity, "Lady Wisdom" writ large, the same plural used in 1:20 and 9:1 where Wisdom is personified. The wise woman (ʾēšet ḥāyil implied) builds (bānātâ, perfect: accomplished action) her house; the fool tears it down with her own hands (bəyādehā) — the instrument of destruction is herself.
14.2: יְרֵא יהוה (fearer of the LORD) — the fear-of-YHWH formula; yārēʾ as a participle indicates a settled character, not a momentary fright. The contrast is bôzeh dərākāyw (one who despises his [the LORD's] ways) — the suffix "his ways" refers to the LORD's ways, not the person's own; contempt for God's path is contempt for God himself.
14.3: בְּפִי-אֱוִיל (in the mouth of a fool) is a חֹטֶר גַּאֲוָה (branch/shoot of pride/arrogance) — ḥōṭer is a twig or rod; the fool's speech is itself a weapon of arrogance that strikes back against him. The lips of the wise (śiptê ḥăkāmîm) tišmôrēm (protect them, Qal imperfect) — the lips are both the instrument and the means of protection.
14.4: אֵין אֲלָפִים (where there are no oxen) the אֵבוּס בָּר (manger is empty/clean) — the word bār can mean both "clean" and "grain/food." The deliberate ambiguity: clean because empty, or empty of the grain that oxen produce. Much increase (tebûʾat ʿāz, produce of strength) comes from ox power. Productivity requires accepting the mess of living things.
14.5: עֵד אֱמוּנִים (faithful/trustworthy witness) — ʾĕmûnîm carries the covenant-faithfulness register of ʾemet (truth, reliability); the witness is truthful because they are fundamentally oriented toward reality. The false witness (ʾēd šeqer, a witness of šeqer, falsehood/deceit) breathes out (yāpîaḥ) lies — the breathing metaphor suggests effortless, habitual production of deception.
14.6: The mocker (lēṣ) seeks (biqqēš, Piel: deliberate seeking) wisdom but does not find it. Knowledge comes easily (nāqāl, is light/swift) to the person of understanding (nābôn). The lēṣ's problem is not intellectual incapacity but the prior moral stance — contempt for correction (see 13:1) that closes the mind to wisdom before the seeking begins.
14.7: לֵךְ מִנֶּגֶד (go from before) is a strong spatial imperative: physically remove yourself from the fool's presence. The reason: בַּל-יָדַעְתָּ שִׂפְתֵי-דָעַת — there are no lips of knowledge on him; knowledge is not present to his mouth. The negative bal (never/certainly not) is stronger than simple lōʾ.
14.8: עָרְמַת הַפִּקֵחַ (cleverness/prudence of the discerning) is to understand his way — the same root ʿāram used for the serpent's cleverness in Gen 3:1; context determines whether shrewdness is virtue or vice. The folly of fools is mirmâ (deception/deceit) — the MKT "self-deception" draws out the direction: fools deceive themselves first.
14.9: אֱוִילִים יָלִיץ אָשָׁם — fools mock or scoff at the ʾāšām (guilt/guilt-offering). The word ʾāšām carries its full Levitical weight: the guilt offering was the mechanism for repairing a damaged relationship with God or others. Fools treat the concept of restitution/guilt as something to ridicule. Among the upright (yəšārîm), rāṣôn (favor/goodwill) is present.
14.10: מַרַּת נַפְשׁוֹ (bitterness of his soul) — mar is the taste of gall, the physical sensation of grief. Nephesh here is the inner experiential self, inaccessible to outsiders. No stranger (zār) shares in its joy either — the same inaccessibility applies to gladness. The verse establishes the fundamental privacy of the inner life as an anthropological fact.
14.11: בֵּית רְשָׁעִים (house of the wicked) will be destroyed (yiššāmēd, Niphal: will be caused to perish) — the house as a material symbol of the family line. The tent (ʾōhel) of the upright will flourish (yiphrāḥ). The pairing of "house" with "tent" is deliberate: even the more temporary structure of the upright outlasts the supposedly permanent house of the wicked.
14.12: דֶּרֶךְ יָשָׁר לִפְנֵי-אִישׁ (a way straight before a man) — the initial assessment is positive, "straight/upright" by appearance. Its end (ʾaḥărîteyhā) is ways of death (darkê-māvet, plural: there are many such paths). The verse is a philosophical statement about the limits of self-evaluation and the danger of trust in one's own moral sense (cf. Prov 3:5–7).
14.13: גַּם בִּשְׂחוֹק (even in laughter) the heart may be painful (yikʾāb, is in pain/hurt) — the imperfect here is present-tense observation: laughter does not guarantee inner peace. וְאַחֲרִיתָהּ שִׂמְחָה תוּגָה (and its end, joy is grief) — the feminine suffix on "its end" refers back to laughter; the grammatical trace is subtle. Joy contains the seed of its own reversal.
14.14: מִדְּרָכָיו יִשְׂבַּע (from his own ways he will be satisfied/full) — the backslider (sûg lēb, one who turns away of heart) is sated with his own choices: the consequences he chose. The good man will be satisfied mimmennû (from himself, from within) — the prep min makes the source internal, suggesting genuine inner resource rather than external circumstance.
14.15: פֶּתִי (petî, simple/naive one) — the naivety of inexperience, not malice; the term is used for the educable youth throughout Proverbs. Yaʾamin (believes) every word — the imperfect indicates habitual credulity. The prudent (ʿārûm) considers carefully (yābîn ʾăšurô, understands/discerns his step) — the verb bîn implies ongoing deliberation.
14.16: חָכָם יָרֵא (a wise person fears) — the object is implied; the fear is of God and consequence. He turns from evil (sār mērāʿ). The fool (kəsîl) is mitrabbēr (overconfident, puffed up) and bôṭēaḥ (trusts/is secure) — the same root used for godly trust in God; the fool redirects that trust toward himself.
14.17: קְצַר-אַפַּיִם (short of nostrils/anger, quick-tempered) — the Hebrew idiom for anger runs through the nostrils; ʾapayyim literally means nostrils, and a flaring nose signals fury. Verse 29 below pairs this with the positive: ʾerek ʾapayyim (long of nostrils, slow to anger). The anatomical metaphor is precise and visceral.
14.18: נָחֲלוּ פְתָאיִם (the naive have inherited) folly (ʾiwwelet) — the verb nāḥal is the inheritance-word; folly becomes their estate, their portion. The prudent (ʿărûmîm) are crowned (yaktiru, Hiphil: will crown/encircle) with knowledge. The social and metaphorical resonance of keter (crown) elevates knowledge to royalty.
14.19: שַׁחוּ (they bowed down, Qal perfect) — the prophetic perfect: the moral order demands this outcome so certainly that it is spoken as accomplished fact. Evil people bow before the good; the wicked kneel at the gates of the righteous. The gate (šaʿar) is the place of judgment and community life; the reversal happens precisely where honor is publicly distributed.
14.20: גַּם לְרֵעֵהוּ (even by his neighbor) is hated (yiśśānēʾ) — the social isolation of poverty. The rich man (ʿāšîr) has many friends (ʾōhăbîm rabbîm, many lovers) — the word ʾōhăbîm (from ʾāhab, love) in a social context means those who are drawn to him; the same root used for genuine love here denotes transactional attachment to wealth.
14.21: בָּז לְרֵעֵהוּ (despises his neighbor) חוֹטֵא (sins, is a sinner) — the participial form makes contempt for the neighbor a defining sin, not a minor failing. The one who shows grace (mēḥônnēn, Piel participle: continually shows grace) to the poor (ʿănāyîm) is called ʾašrāyw (blessed, happy) — the same beatitude form as Ps 1:1.
14.22: חֹרְשֵׁי רָע (those who plow evil) — the agricultural metaphor: deliberate, sustained evil-cultivation. They yitʿû (go astray, wander) — the same verb used for straying sheep. Those who plan good (ḥōrəšê ṭôb) find ḥesed wĕʾemet (loyal love and faithfulness) — the covenant-pair that characterizes God's own character (Exod 34:6).
14.23: בְּכָל-עֶצֶב (in all labor/painful toil) there is profit (môtār, something left over, surplus) — ʿeṣeb is the word used for the painful labor of Gen 3:17 (the curse); here it is the path to gain. Debar śəpātayim (word of lips) — mere talking — leads only to poverty. The contrast is between embodied action and verbal substitutes for it.
14.24: עֲטֶרֶת חֲכָמִים עָשְׁרָם (the crown of the wise is their wealth) — ʿōšer (wealth) as the crowning achievement; the word carries both material and honorific weight. The folly of fools (ʾiwwelet kəsîlîm) is ʾiwwelet (folly) — the tautology is deliberate and damning: fools' crown is simply their own foolishness, which they display unwittingly.
14.25: עֵד אֱמֶת (witness of truth) מַצִּיל נְפָשׁוֹת (saves lives/souls) — māṣal in the Hiphil is a rescue/deliverance verb, used for God's deliverance in the Psalms. The false witness (ʾēd šeqer) breathes out (yāpîaḥ) lies (cf. 14:5) — the same breathing metaphor, the habitual effortlessness of deception.
14.26: בְּיִרְאַת יהוה (in the fear of the LORD) is strong confidence (mibtaḥ ʿōz, a strong refuge/trust). The suffix ûbānāyw (and for his children) — God's covenant with the God-fearer extends to the next generation; the fear of the LORD as an inherited orientation and social structure.
14.27: This verse is nearly identical to 13:14: יְרֵאַת יהוה מְקוֹר חַיִּים (fear of the LORD is a fountain of life). The repetition is deliberate and structural; the wisdom collection reinforces its core affirmations by recurrence. The fountain of life is not an abstraction but a relational orientation toward God that produces vitality.
14.28: בְּרָב-עָם (in the abundance/multitude of people) is a king's glory (hadar) — the same word used for God's splendor in the Psalms. The prince's ruin (mahattâ, downfall) is the lack of people (bĕʾepes leʾōm, in the absence of a nation) — power is measured in persons, not territory. Leadership is relational.
14.29: אֶרֶךְ אַפַּיִם (ʾerek ʾapayyim) — literally "long of nostrils," the anatomical idiom for patience/slowness to anger, contrasting with qəṣar-ʾapayyim (short of nostrils, v. 17). The contrast shows great insight (rab-tebûnâ, abundant understanding) — tebûnâ is not raw intelligence but the discernment that comes from understanding relationships and consequences. A quick temper (qəṣar-rûaḥ, short of spirit) elevates foolishness: makes it prominent, lifts it up.
14.30: לֵב מַרְפֵּא (a healing heart) is ḥayyê bāśār (life of flesh/the body) — the inner emotional state of the heart (lēb) generates the physical vitality of the whole body. Qinʾâ (envy/jealousy) rots the bones (raqqab-ʿăṣāmôt) — the same verb used for decay and putrefaction; the emotion produces biological deterioration. The psychosomatic connection is explicit.
14.31: עֹשֵׁק דָּל (oppressor of the poor) insults (ḥērēp) his Maker — the same word used for the Philistine's taunting of Israel (Goliath). To oppress the poor is to mock God as Creator. The one who shows grace (mēḥannēn) to the needy honors him (məkabbədhô) — the Piel of kābad, to give weight, to honor.
14.32: בְּרָעָתוֹ יִדָּחֶה רָשָׁע (in his evil the wicked is thrust down/driven away) — the passive Niphal: his own evil is the agent. חֹסֶה בְמוֹתוֹ צַדִּיק (the righteous has refuge in his death) — ḥāsâ (take refuge) is the verb of shelter and trust used throughout the Psalms. Bəmôtô (in his death) is striking: the righteous person's hope in the moment of death itself.
14.33: בְּלֶב נָבוֹן תָּנוּחַ חָכְמָה (in the heart of the discerning wisdom rests quietly) — tānûaḥ (rests, settles) is the verb of the Sabbath rest; wisdom finds a home in the discerning heart. Among fools (bəqereb kəsîlîm), wisdom "makes itself known" (tiggāḏaʿ, Niphal: is made known, reveals itself) — even unintentional disclosure; wisdom cannot be entirely suppressed even in fool company.
14.34: צְדָקָה תְּרוֹמֵם-גּוֹי (righteousness lifts up a nation) — the verb rûm in the Polel is a stately elevation; ṣedāqâ as the national character creates social height. Ḥesed (in many translations "reproach"; others "loyal love/steadfast love") — here ḥaṭṭāʾt (sin/sin-offering) is the subject: "sin is the shame of peoples." The MKT "sin brings disgrace" follows the more common reading.
14.35: רְצוֹן-מֶלֶךְ (favor of the king) — rāṣôn is the same word as divine acceptance or favor; royal and divine approval run in parallel in wisdom literature. The servant who acts wisely (meskîl, Hiphil participle: causes insight/acts intelligently) receives this. The king's wrath (ʾapô, his nose/anger) falls on the shameful (mēbîš, one who causes shame).

15.1: מַעֲנֶה רַּךְ (maʿăneh rak, a soft/gentle answer) — maʿăneh is the response that meets a question or situation; rak is soft, tender. The verb yāšîb ḥēmâ (turns away wrath) uses the return/restore verb: the soft answer sends wrath back the way it came. A grievous word (debar-ʿeṣeb, a word of pain/toil) stirs up (yaʿăleh, causes to rise) wrath — the uprising image of anger inflamed.
15.2: לְשׁוֹן חֲכָמִים (tongue of the wise) תֵּיטִיב דָּעַת (presents knowledge rightly/makes knowledge good) — the Hiphil of yāṭab: causes knowledge to be good, presents it well. The mouth of fools (pî כְּסִילִים) yabbîaʿ (gushes/pours out) foolishness — the same word used for springs and fountains overflowing; uncontrolled output.
15.3: עֵינֵי יהוה (eyes of the LORD) are in every place (bəkol-māqôm) — omnipresence stated plainly. They watch (ṣōpôt, Qal participle: constantly surveying) both the evil and the good. The theological import: there is no hidden space. The verse functions as the epistemological foundation for the entire Proverbs moral system — behavior is always observed.
15.4: מַרְפֵּא לָשׁוֹן (a healing tongue) is a ʿēṣ ḥayyîm (tree of life) — the Genesis phrase again (cf. 13:12). The tongue as a tree of life: speech as a life-giving or life-denying force. Selep (crookedness/perverseness) in it is a breaking of the spirit (šeber bərûaḥ) — the same šābar used for the breaking of bones and the fracture of nations; twisted speech destroys at that depth.
15.5: אֱוִיל (ʾĕwîl) — one of several Hebrew words for fool; this one emphasizes moral stubbornness rather than mere ignorance. He despises (yinʾaṣ, Qal: holds in contempt) his father's mûsār (discipline). The one who guards (šōmēr, keeps watch over) reproof (tôkaḥat) shows good sense (yaʿrim, acts prudently/craftily in the good sense).
15.6: בֵּית צַדִּיק חֹסֶן רָב (house of the righteous is great treasure) — ḥōsen is the word for stored wealth or treasury; the household is a wealth-store not just materially but structurally. The income of the wicked (tebûʾat rāšāʿ) brings ʿăkārâ (trouble/disturbance) — the word used for Achan's troubling of Israel (Josh 7:25).
15.7: שִׂפְתֵי חֲכָמִים (lips of the wise) יְזָרוּ דָעַת (spread/scatter knowledge) — the verb zārâ is used for winnowing grain; wisdom spread by speech is like grain scattered in the wind, broadcast generously. The heart (lēb) of fools — not their mouth — is not so (right/established) — the problem begins deeper than speech.
15.8: זֶבַח רְשָׁעִים תּוֹעֲבַת יהוה (sacrifice of the wicked is an abomination of the LORD) — tôʿăbâ is the cultic revulsion-word: what is fundamentally incompatible with God's holiness, specifically what violates covenant relationship. Sacrifice without inner righteousness is not merely ineffective; it is actively offensive. Tephilat yəšārîm (prayer of the upright) is his raṣôn (delight/pleasure/favor).
15.9: The double use of תּוֹעֲבַת יהוה in vv. 8–9 (sacrifice, then the way/lifestyle of the wicked) intensifies: not just cultic performance but life-orientation provokes divine revulsion. God yəʾahab (loves, Piel: deeply loves) whoever earnestly pursues (məraddēp, Piel participle: continuously, hotly pursues) righteousness — the verb suggests relentless chase.
15.10: מוּסָר רָע (evil/harsh discipline) for the one who ʿōzēb ʾōraḥ (forsakes the path/way) — the abandonment of the way earns painful discipline. Śōnēʾ tôkaḥat (hater of reproof) will die — the imperfect is stark: this is the trajectory. The connection is not mechanical punishment but the internal logic of rejecting the corrective that sustains life.
15.11: שְׁאוֹל וַאֲבַדּוֹן (Šəʾôl wəʾăbaddôn) — the paired names for the realm of death; Abaddon means destruction/perdition (from ʾābad, to perish). Both "lie open (nəgêd, before/visible) to the LORD" — the underworld is not hidden from divine sight.
