The rhetorical ʾap kî (how much more): if even death's domain is transparent to God, how much more are human hearts.
15.12: לֵץ לֹא-יֶאֱהַב (the mocker does not love) being reproved (hōkêaḥ lô) — the same ʾāhab (love) as genuine attachment; the mocker's aversion to correction is visceral and defining. He will not go (wəʾel-ḥăkāmîm lōʾ yēlēk) to the company of the wise — self-isolation from the corrective community is the mocker's chosen condition.
15.13: לֵב שָׂמֵחַ (a joyful/glad heart) makes the face (pānîm) good (yêṭib, Hiphil: causes the face to be good) — the inward state shows outwardly. A crushed spirit (bəʿaṣbat-rûaḥ) — ʿaṣab is pain/grief/toil — breaks/bows (nəkēʾâ rûaḥ) the spirit. The psychosomatic connection is explicit in both directions: inner peace radiates outward; inner grief collapses inward.
15.14: לֵב נָבוֹן (a discerning heart) is always seeking (yəbaqquēš, Piel: continuously, intensively seeks) knowledge (dāʿat). The mouth of fools (pî kəsîlîm) feeds (yirʿeh, literally grazes/shepherds) on folly — the pastoral metaphor is precise: fools graze on foolishness as naturally as sheep on grass. The contrast is active seeking vs. passive consumption of one's default diet.
15.15: כָּל-יְמֵי עָנִי רָעִים (all the days of the afflicted are evil) — ʿānî is the poor/afflicted/oppressed person; their subjective experience of daily life is consistently bad. But a good heart (ṭôb-lēb) is a continual feast (mišteh tāmîd) — mišteh is a banquet/drinking feast; the contrast is between the bleakness of external poverty and the inner abundance of the joyful heart. The inner life can override external conditions.
15.16: בְּיִרְאַת יהוה (with the fear of the LORD) accompanying little (məʿaṭ) is better (ṭôb) than great treasure (ʾôṣār rāb) with turmoil (ûmehûmâ bô) — mehûmâ is the panic and tumult of battle or disaster; wealth without divine orientation generates its own chaos. The ṭôb (better-than) formula is a wisdom rhetorical device for relative evaluation.
15.17: אֲרֻחַת יָרָק (a portion of herbs/vegetables) with love (ʾahăbâ šām, love being there) is better than šôr ʾăbûs (a fattened ox) with hatred (śinʾâ bô). The ʾăbûs (stall-fed) ox is the luxury meal. The relational quality of the meal determines its nourishment more than the food itself.
15.18: אִישׁ חֵמָה (a man of wrath/heat) stirs up (yəgāreh, Piel: provokes, picks a fight with) strife (māḏôn). The one slow to anger (ʾerek ʾapayyim, long of nostrils — the same idiom as 14:29) quiets (yəšakkēn, Piel: causes to dwell/settle) strife. The Piel in both cases intensifies: the provocation is deliberate, and the quieting is active work.
15.19: דֶּרֶךְ עָצֵל (way of the lazy) is like a hedge of thorns (kəמְשׂוּכַת קוֹצִים) — məśûkâ is a barrier of thorns, an impenetrable wall. The road of the upright (ʾōraḥ yəšārîm) is a level highway (səlullâ, a built-up road, the kind paved for royal procession). The contrast is not just easy vs. hard but organic obstacle vs. constructed path.
15.20: בֵּן חָכָם (a wise son) makes his father glad (yəśammēaḥ-ʾāb, Piel: causes joy to his father). A foolish man (ûkəsîl אָדָם) despises his mother (bôzeh ʾimmô) — the contempt word. The shift from "son" to "man" in the second half is deliberate: the grown fool, who should have matured, still acts like the scorner of ch. 13:1.
15.21: אִוֶּלֶת שִׂמְחָה לַחֲסַר-לֵב (folly is joy/entertainment to the one lacking heart/understanding) — ḥăsar-lēb (lacking heart) is the Proverbs phrase for the person without moral judgment; folly actually brings them pleasure. The person of understanding (ʾîš tebûnâ) walks straight (yəyaššer-lāket) — the causative of yāšar: makes his walking straight, corrects his own course.
15.22: הֲפֵר מַחֲשָׁבוֹת (plans come to nothing/are frustrated) where there is no counsel (bəʾên sôd) — sôd is the intimate counsel of a close group, the divine council language used in Amos 3:7 and Jer 23:18. With the multitude of advisors (bərōb yôʿăṣîm) plans are established (tāqûm, will stand/arise). The wisdom of collaborative discernment echoes the divine council pattern.
15.23: שִׂמְחָה לָאִישׁ בְּמַעֲנֵה-פִיו (joy belongs to a person in the answer of his mouth) — the joy of giving the right word; maʿăneh (answer/response) is the same root as 15:1. The rhetorical question ûדָבָר בְּעִתּוֹ מַה-טּוֹב (a word in its time, how good it is!) — ʿittô (its time, the right moment): timing is as important as content.
15.24: אֹרַח חַיִּים לְמַעְלָה (the path of life leads upward) — ləmaʿlâ is a spatial-moral indicator: upward, heavenward. The purpose clause: לְמַעַן סוּר מִשְּׁאוֹל מָטָּה (in order to turn from Sheol below) — the spatial antithesis is complete. The wise person's life-trajectory is upward, away from the downward pull toward the realm of death.
15.25: בֵּית גֵּאִים (house of the proud) will be torn down (yissaḥ, Qal: uprooted/pulled apart) by the LORD — direct divine action. The widow's property line (גְּבוּל אַלְמָנָה) he maintains (yaṣṣēb, Hiphil: causes to stand, maintains firmly). The widow as the paradigmatic vulnerable person in Israelite law; the LORD as her boundary-keeper against the encroachment of the powerful.
15.26: The third use of תּוֹעֲבַת יהוה in chapter 15 (vv. 8, 9, 26) — now applied to the plans (maḥšəbôt) of the wicked. Pure words (ʾimrê-ṭōhar, words of purity) are pleasing (təhôrîm nōʿam) to him — the same nāʿam (pleasantness) used for Wisdom's paths in Prov 3:17. The cultic and ethical are fused: purity of speech is liturgically acceptable.
15.27: בּוֹצֵעַ בָּצַע (one who cuts/gains unjust gain) troubles (ʿōkēr) his household — the same verb used for Achan's troubling of Israel in Josh 7:25. Śōnēʾ mattānôt (hater of bribes) will live — not merely "survives" but yiḥyeh (will have life), the full life that wisdom promises. The ethical and the vital are inseparably linked.
15.28: לֵב צַדִּיק (heart of the righteous) יֶהְגֶּה (meditates/weighs carefully) before answering — hāgâ is the meditation verb of Ps 1:2 (meditating on Torah). The speech of the righteous is the outcome of inner deliberation. The mouth of the wicked (pî rəšāʿîm) yabbîaʿ (gushes, same as 15:2) evil things — unfiltered overflow without prior thought.
15.29: רָחוֹק יהוה מֵרְשָׁעִים (the LORD is far from the wicked) — rāḥôq (distant) as a spatial metaphor for relational absence. This is not merely metaphor: in wisdom theology, the distance is the consequence of choosing the path that diverges from God. He hears (yišmaʿ) the prayer of the righteous — the covenant hearing-response formula; prayer reaches God because proximity exists.
15.30: מְאוֹר-עֵינַיִם (light of eyes, bright eyes) makes the heart glad (yəśammah-lēb) — a psychosomatic chain running from the visual sense to the emotional center. Good news (šəmûʿâ ṭôbâ) refreshes (tədaššen, causes to be fat/prosperous) bone — dāšan means to become fat or well-nourished; the idiom suggests deep physical restoration from within.
15.31: אֹזֶן שֹׁמַעַת תּוֹכַחַת חַיִּים (ear that listens to life-giving correction) — tôkaḥat ḥayyîm (reproof of life, life-giving reproof): the correction that leads to life is so described. The one who heeds it bəqereb ḥăkāmîm tālin (lodges/dwells among the wise) — lîn is an overnight lodging verb; the correction-hearer makes a permanent home in the company of wisdom.
15.32: פּוֹרֵעַ מוּסָר (one who neglects/throws off discipline) מוֹאֵס נַפְשׁוֹ (despises his own nephesh) — the person who refuses correction injures the very self they meant to protect. By contrast, šōmēaʿ tôkaḥat qōneh lēb (whoever listens to reproof gains/acquires heart/understanding) — qānâ is the acquisition verb used for buying wisdom (4:5, 7); correction is a purchase that yields understanding.
15.33: יִרְאַת יהוה מוּסַר חָכְמָה (fear of the LORD is training/discipline for wisdom) — mûsar (discipline/instruction) is the same word that opens ch. 1:2; it closes ch. 15 to form a structural bracket. Wisdom is not innate but acquired through the fear-shaped formative process. Liphney kābôd ʿănāwâ (before honor is humility) — ʿănāwâ (humility, meekness) precedes honor; the sequence matters.

16.1: 'The plans (ma'arĕkê) of the heart belong to man, but the answer (ma'anēh) of the tongue is from the LORD' — the verse turns on a contrast between human intention and divine determination of what actually comes out in speech. Ma'arĕkê = arrangements/orderings; ma'anēh = the actual answer/response. YHWH controls not just big history but the specific words that leave the mouth, which Proverbs regards as the most revealing indicator of a person's inner state.
16.2: Kol-darkê-'îš zak bĕ'ênāyw = 'All the ways of a man are pure in his own eyes' — radical self-assessment blindness. Tōkēn rûḥôt YHWH = 'but the LORD weighs/measures spirits.' Tākan (weigh/measure) is the precision-metrological verb used for calibrating scales — YHWH applies exact measurement to what humans cannot objectively assess in themselves.
16.3: Gōl 'el-YHWH ma'ăśeḵā = 'Roll your works to the LORD' — gāll (roll) is used for rolling away a stone or off-loading a burden; the visual image is of handing the whole weight of one's enterprises to YHWH. Wĕyikkōnû maḥšĕbōteḵā = 'and your plans will be established' — the MKT 'Commit your work' captures the intent but loses the rolling/burden-transfer image.
16.4: Kōl pā'al YHWH lamma'anēhû = 'The LORD has made everything for its purpose/answer' — ma'anēhû (its answer/purpose) echoes ma'anēh in v1: everything has its fitting response in the divine order. Wĕgam-rāšā' lĕyôm rā'āh = 'even the wicked for the day of calamity' — divine sovereignty extends over human wickedness itself, which serves its appointed function in YHWH's purposes without YHWH being the author of the wickedness.
16.5: Tô'ăbat YHWH kol-gĕbah-lēb = 'An abomination to the LORD is every proud-of-heart person' — gābah (be high/exalted) applied to the heart describes arrogance. Yād lĕyād = 'hand to hand' = a certain/sure oath gesture; the proud will certainly not go unpunished.
16.6: Bĕḥesed we'ĕmet yĕkuppar 'āwōn = 'By steadfast love and faithfulness (ḥesed we'emet) iniquity is atoned for/covered' — the covenant-pair terms here function as the moral rather than cultic means of atonement: the person of covenant-loyalty finds their sin covered. Yĕsūr mērā' = 'turns away from evil' — the fear of YHWH is the practical instrument of moral departure from evil.
16.7: 'When a man's ways please the LORD, he makes even his enemies be at peace with him' — yašlîm (make peace) — even hostile third parties become instruments of divine blessing when a person's life is aligned with YHWH's ways. This is the positive application of divine sovereignty: YHWH controls the dispositions of enemies.
16.8: A better-than saying: 'Better a little with righteousness than great revenues with injustice' (bĕṣĕdāqāh). The principle of qualitative over quantitative measurement runs through Proverbs; ṣĕdāqāh (righteousness/right-relationship) modifies the value of what is possessed.
16.9: Lēb 'ādām yĕḥaššēb darkô = 'The heart of a man plans his way' — the verb ḥāšab is the word for careful calculation/devising; wĕYHWH yākîn ṣa'ădô = 'but the LORD establishes his step.' Ṣa'ăd (step/stride) is the individual footfall — YHWH's providential control is not merely over large trajectories but over each specific step. The contrast of human planning with divine directing is the governing theme of ch16.
16.10: Qesem 'al-śiptê-melek = 'Divination/oracle is on the lips of the king' — qesem normally refers to prohibited divination (Deut 18:10), but here it is used positively for the quasi-oracular authority of the king's judicial pronouncements. The royal mouth speaks with binding authority; bĕmišpāṭ lō' yim'al pîw = 'his mouth should not err in judgment.'
16.11: Peles ûmō'zĕnê mišpāṭ lYHWH = 'A just balance and scales belong to the LORD' — YHWH is the ultimate standard of all measurement. The divine ownership of measuring instruments means that commercial dishonesty is not merely a social wrong but a theological offense. Kol-'abnê-kis = 'all the stones of the bag' = all the calibration weights.
16.12: 'An abomination to kings to do evil' — kî-bĕṣĕdāqāh yikkôn kissē' = 'for by righteousness the throne is established.' The throne's stability is a function of the righteousness of its occupant; this is the standard prophetic critique (Amos, Isaiah, Jeremiah) applied to the wisdom context: power without righteousness is self-undermining.
16.13: Śiptê-ṣedeq reṣôn mĕlākîm = 'Lips of righteousness are the delight of kings' — reṣôn (delight/favor) describes what the wise king values in counsel. Wĕ'ōhēb dibēr yĕšārîm = 'and one who speaks straight/upright things he loves.' The king who values honest counsel is functionally wise; the king who does not (see 14:35; 19:12) is a danger.
16.14: Za'ap-melek mal'ăkê māwet = 'The wrath of the king is messengers of death' — the plural mal'ăkê (messengers) figures the king's anger as an agency that sends out death-agents. Wĕ'îš ḥākām yĕkappĕrennāh = 'but a wise man will appease/atone for it.' Kāpar (atone/cover) applied to pacifying the king's anger — wisdom is the instrument that deflects lethal power.
16.15: 'ôr-pĕnê-melek ḥayyîm = 'In the light of the king's face is life' — the king's approving look gives life; ûrĕṣônô kĕ'āb malkôš = 'and his favor is like a cloud of spring rain.' The malkôš (spring rain, late/latter rain) is the rain that causes the grain to mature in the final weeks before harvest — the most life-giving of rains. Royal favor is that precious and that productive.
16.16: 'How much better to get wisdom than gold' (qĕnôh ḥokmāh mah-ṭôb mizzāhāb) — the comparative wisdom saying uses the commercial vocabulary of v8 (qānāh = acquire/buy). The parallel 'and to get understanding is to be chosen rather than silver' reinforces the value-comparison: wisdom beats both metals.
16.17: Mĕsillat yĕšārîm sûr mērā' = 'The highway of the upright is turning away from evil' — mĕsillāh is the paved royal road, not a footpath. The deliberate road-avoidance of evil is the upright person's characteristic movement. Šōmēr napšô nōṣēr darkô = 'the one who guards his soul keeps his way.'
16.18: Lifnê-šeber gā'ôn = 'Before destruction is pride' — the famous verse. Gā'ôn (pride/majesty) can be positive (divine majesty) or negative (human arrogance); here clearly the latter. Gōbah rûaḥ lifnê kāšel = 'and a haughty spirit before stumbling/falling.' The MKT 'Pride goes before destruction, and a haughty spirit before a fall' reproduces the standard English idiom derived from this verse; the Hebrew gā'ôn is more intense than 'pride' — it denotes presumptuous self-exaltation.
16.19: 'Better to be lowly (šĕpal-rûaḥ, low of spirit) with the poor than to divide spoil with the proud' — the antithesis between true humility (voluntarily taken low position with those of low status) and triumphalist gain-sharing with the arrogant. The socioeconomic dimension is deliberate: wisdom chooses the social bottom over the prideful top.
16.20: Maśkîl 'al-dābār = 'The one who attends wisely to a matter' (or 'who handles a word skillfully') yimṣā' ṭôb = 'will find good.' Ûbôṭēaḥ baYHWH = 'and blessed is the one who trusts in the LORD.' The two lines create an implied equation: skillful attention to affairs and trust in YHWH belong together; neither is separable from the other in Proverbs' wisdom theology.
16.21: Ḥăkam-lēb yiqqārē' nābôn = 'The wise of heart will be called discerning' — nābôn (discerning) is the social recognition of inner wisdom. Ûmeteq śĕpātayim yôsîp leqaḥ = 'and sweetness of lips increases learning/reception' — the communicative dimension of wisdom: not only possessing understanding but conveying it with meteq (sweetness/pleasantness) that makes it receivable.
16.22: Mĕqôr ḥayyîm śĕkel bĕ'ālāyw = 'A fountain of life is insight to its possessor' — the same fountain-of-life image as 13:14; 14:27 — wisdom as the source of life. Ûmûsar 'ĕwîlîm 'iwwelet = 'but the discipline of fools is folly' — discipline applied to the fool only produces more folly; the fool turns even correction into an expression of his nature.
16.23: Lēb ḥākām yaśkîl pîhû = 'The heart of the wise makes his mouth wise/teach skillfully' — the internal-external connection: a wise heart produces wise speech. Wĕ'al-śĕpātāyw yōsîp leqaḥ = 'and on his lips it adds learning/persuasive power.' The wise person's words carry more weight because they arise from genuine understanding rather than mere rhetorical skill.
16.24: Nōpet ṣûpîm 'imrê nō'am = 'Pleasant words are like honeycomb' — nōpet ṣûpîm is dripping/flowing honeycomb (the purest, finest honey). Marpe' lā'etsem = 'healing to the bones' — the physical metaphor for the restorative power of well-chosen words. The bones as the deepest bodily structure suggest that good speech heals at the most fundamental level.
16.25: Verbatim repetition of 14:12: 'There is a way that seems right to a man, but its end is the way to death' (derek yāšār lipnê-'îš wĕ'aḥărîtāh darkê-māwet). The repetition of the saying is intentional — it belongs to the center of ch16's meditation on divine sovereignty (vv1,9,33 frame the chapter) and human self-deception. The way that 'seems right' is the alternative to YHWH-ordered steps (v9).
16.26: Nepeš 'āmēl 'āmĕlāh-lô = 'The soul/appetite of the laborer labors for him' — the worker is driven by his own nepeš (appetite/self). Kî-'āpap 'ālāyw pîhû = 'for his mouth urges/presses him' — hunger is the motor of labor; the bodily need drives the work. This is neutral observation, not critique: physical need is part of the created order that motivates human industry.
16.27: 'Îš bĕliyya'al kōreh rā'āh = 'A man of worthlessness digs evil' — kārar (dig) suggests deliberate labor to produce wickedness; evil doesn't happen accidentally for such a person, it is excavated. Wĕ'al-śĕpātāyw kĕ'ēš ṣōrābet = 'and on his lips is like scorching fire' — the speech of the worthless person burns those it touches.
16.28: 'Îš tahpukôt yĕšallēaḥ mādon = 'A man of perversities/distortions sends out strife' — tahpukôt (perversities) describes fundamental distortion of the moral order. Wĕnirgan maphrid allûp = 'and a whisperer separates close friends' — nirgan (whisperer/gossip) does maximal damage in the most intimate relationships; allûp = a close friend or intimate companion.
16.29: 'Îš ḥāmās yĕpatteh rē'ēhû = 'A violent man entices/seduces his neighbor' — pātāh (entice/seduce) is the word used for seduction in sexual contexts; the violent person uses persuasion before force. Wĕhôlîkô bĕderek lō'-ṭôb = 'and leads him in a way that is not good' — Proverbs' characteristic understatement for spiritual ruin.
16.30: 'One who winks with his eyes ('ōṣeh 'ênāyw) to devise perverse things, one who purses his lips (qōrēṣ śĕpātāyw)' — the non-verbal communication of malicious intent. These are the same body-language indicators as 6:13-14: crooked dealings are signaled through eyes and lips before they are enacted in action. Kille' rā'āh = 'finishes/completes evil.'
16.31: Wĕśêbāh 'ăṭeret tipʾāret = 'Grey/white hair is a crown of glory' — śêbāh (grey hair/old age) as a crown is unusual; typically crowns are given by wisdom (4:9). Here old age itself is the crown, but only bĕderek ṣĕdāqāh timāṣē' = 'when found in the way of righteousness.' Long life that has been righteous is the crown; long wicked life is not crowned.
16.32: 'Erek 'appayim miggibôr = 'Slow to anger is better than a warrior' — 'erek 'appayim (long of nose/anger = patient/slow to anger) is the divine attribute of Exod 34:6 applied to the human sphere. Ûmōšēl bĕrûḥô milōkēd 'îr = 'and the one who rules his spirit is better than one who conquers a city' — the most demanding conquest is internal. The military vocabulary applied to self-mastery is deliberately ironic.
16.33: Haggôrāl yûṭal bĕḥêq = 'The lot is cast into the lap' — the mechanism of lot-casting (Urim/Thummim; casting lots for decisions) is described; ûmēYHWH kol-mišpāṭô = 'but from the LORD is its every decision/judgment.' What appears to be chance is actually divine determination. This closes the divine-sovereignty inclusio of ch16 (vv1,9,33): YHWH governs the tongue (v1), the step (v9), and even the lot (v33).

17.1: Ṭôb pat ḥărēbāh wĕšalwāh-bāh = 'Better a dry morsel with quietness/peace in it' — pat ḥărēbāh = a dry/crumbled morsel (the opposite of fat, moist food). Mibayit mālē' zibeḥê-rîb = 'than a house full of sacrificial feasts with strife' — sacrificial feasts (zibeḥê) represent the height of abundance and religious occasion; the contrast is between austere peace and sumptuous conflict.
17.2: 'Ebed maśkîl = 'A wise servant' who rules over a disgraceful son — social mobility through wisdom: the slave who exercises wisdom surpasses the birth-right of the fool-son. Ûbĕtôk 'aḥîm yḥaliq naḥalāh = 'and among brothers he will share in the inheritance.' Wisdom overturns normal social hierarchy.
17.3: Maṣrēp lakkesep wĕkûr lazzāhāb = 'A crucible/refining pot for silver and a furnace for gold' — maṣrēp (refining pot) and kûr (furnace/smelting furnace) are the metallurgical vessels; ûbōḥēn libbôt YHWH = 'but the tester of hearts is the LORD.' The divine smelting-test applies to the heart what the crucible applies to metal: YHWH's scrutiny purges and reveals what is genuinely valuable.
17.4: Mērēa' maqšîb 'al-śepat-'āwen = 'An evildoer listens to wicked lips' — the audience shapes the speaker: those who listen to wickedness are themselves wicked. Šeqer maqšîb 'al-lĕšôn hawwôt = 'a liar pays attention to a destructive tongue' — the mutual reinforcement of wickedness in speech-listening: you listen to what you are.
17.5: Lō'ēg lārāš ḥērēp 'ōśēhû = 'Whoever mocks the poor insults his Maker' ('ōśēhû = the one who made him) — the same creation-theology as 14:31. Śāmēaḥ lĕ'ēd lō' yinnāqeh = 'the one who rejoices at calamity will not go unpunished' — the prohibition on gloating extends to all suffering; malice in the face of others' disasters is an active moral failing.
17.6: 'ăṭeret zĕqênîm bĕnê bānîm = 'The crown of the elderly is grandchildren' — the multigenerational structure of wisdom-transmission: grandchildren are the elderly person's crown because they represent the continuation of the legacy. Wĕtipʾeret bānîm 'ăbôtām = 'and the glory of children is their fathers' — the bidirectional nature of generational honor: children bring honor to fathers; fathers are the crown of children.
17.7: Lō' nā'wāh lĕnābāl śepat-yeter = 'Not fitting/appropriate for a fool is excellent speech' — nābāl (fool/knave) is the morally deficient person. The irony: fine speech in a fool's mouth is as incongruous as lying lips in a nobleman's. The implication is that speech quality must match character to be authentic; eloquent lies are more dangerous than clumsy truths.
17.8: 'Eben-ḥēn haššōḥad bĕ'ênê bĕ'ālāyw = 'A charm-stone/magical stone (ḥēn) is the bribe in the eyes of its owner' — the bribe is described as a jewel or lucky stone that seems to work like magic. Bĕkol-'ăšer yipneh yaśkîl = 'wherever it turns it prospers.' This is social observation without explicit moral comment — the effectiveness of bribery is noted; the condemnation is implicit in the broader context.
17.9: Mĕkasseh-peša' mĕbaqqēš 'ahăbāh = 'Covering/overlooking a transgression seeks love' — kāsāh (cover) is a positive act here: not sweeping sin under the rug but choosing not to expose or repeat an offense, in service of relationship. The contrast: šôneh bĕdābār maphrid allûp = 'but repeating a matter separates close friends' — allûp again (v28); the intimate friendship most vulnerable to gossip.
17.10: Tērad gĕ'ārāh bĕmêbîn = 'A rebuke sinks deeper into a discerning person' than a hundred blows into a fool. The receptivity to correction is the diagnostic of wisdom — not how much punishment is required, but how much truth is received. The discerning person is changed by a single word; the fool is unaffected by violence.
17.11: Ak-merî yĕbaqqēš-rā' = 'Only/nothing but rebellion does the evil man seek' — merî (rebellion/contumacy) as the single definitive characteristic. Ûmal'āk 'akzārî yĕšullaḥ-bô = 'and a cruel messenger will be sent against him' — divine retribution in the form of a harsh emissary; what you cultivate is what you receive.
17.12: Pāgoš dōb šakkûl bĕ'îš = 'Meet a bereaved bear rather than a fool in his folly' — the bereaved bear (dōb šakkûl) robbed of her cubs is one of the ancient world's most dangerous animals; the fool's danger exceeds even this. The comparison calibrates the lethality of the fool: the bear's danger is situational; the fool's is chronic and incorrigible.
17.13: Mēšîb rā'āh taḥat ṭôbāh = 'One who returns evil for good' — the treachery of repaying good with evil is the deepest form of relational betrayal. Lō'-tāmûš rā'āh mibbêtô = 'evil will not depart from his house' — the principle of sowing and reaping (Gal 6:7 echoes this) applies to treachery: the household of the betrayer becomes the dwelling-place of the evil he returned.
17.14: Pōṭēr mayim rēšît mādon = 'The beginning of strife is like letting out water' — pōṭēr (let out, release) of water describes the first crack in a dam: once the water is released, the whole structure collapses. The MKT 'start of conflict is like a crack letting out water' captures this vividly. Ûlibnê hithraqqēaḥ hārîb = 'so abandon the quarrel before it breaks open' — wisdom acts at the first sign of fracture, not after the flood.
17.15: Maṣdîq rāšā' ûmarší'a ṣaddîq = 'Acquitting the wicked and condemning the righteous' — both are abominations to YHWH. The judicial perversion is double: falsely clearing the guilty and falsely condemning the innocent. This is the central prophetic concern (Isa 5:20,23; Amos 5:7; Mic 3:11) placed in the wisdom frame: miscarriage of justice is categorically intolerable to YHWH.
17.16: Lāmmāh-zeh mĕḥîr bĕyad-kĕsîl liqnôt ḥokmāh = 'Why is there a price in the hand of a fool to buy wisdom, when he has no heart for it?' — the irony is economic: the fool might have the purchasing power but lacks the capacity to receive what wisdom offers. Wĕlēb 'āyin = 'no heart' — not absence of desire but absence of the necessary cognitive-moral organ for wisdom acquisition.
17.17: 'Āhēb 'ohēb bĕkol-'ēt = 'A friend loves at all times' — the 'at all times' (bĕkol-'ēt) is the mark of genuine friendship: not situational but constant. Wĕ'āḥ lĕṣārāh yiwwālēd = 'and a brother is born for times of adversity.' The friend as 'all-times' lover and the brother as 'adversity-born' companion: friendship transcends circumstance while brotherhood is revealed by it.
17.18: Ādam ḥăsar-lēb tôqēa' kāp = 'A man lacking sense gives a pledge/strikes hands in surety' — echoing 6:1-5; the surety-pledge is the sign of deficient practical wisdom. 'ōrēb 'ărubāh lipnê rē'ēhû = 'gives surety in the presence of his neighbor' — the public nature of the commitment makes it even more binding and more reckless.
17.19: 'Ōhēb peša' = 'One who loves transgression loves strife' — the love of sin produces communal damage as a natural output. Magbîah petaḥô mĕbaqqēš-šāber = 'one who raises his gate high seeks destruction' — the 'high gate' is probably an image of arrogance/ostentation that provokes conflict; 'seeking destruction' describes the inevitable result of proud self-assertion.
17.20: 'Iqēš-lēb lō'-yimṣā'-ṭôb = 'The crooked-of-heart does not find good' — 'iqēš (crooked) is the same term as 16:28 (tahpukôt); fundamental distortion produces a life where good is inaccessible. Wĕnehpāk bilšônô yippôl bĕrā'āh = 'and the one perverted in his tongue falls into evil' — speech and character are inseparable in Proverbs; crooked speech is both symptom and cause of moral downfall.
17.21: 'To have a fool for a son brings grief' (lĕtûgāh hôledô) — the parental perspective: the father's grief at a foolish son is deep precisely because it is personal, not abstract. Wĕlō' yiśmaḥ 'ăbî nābāl = 'and the father of a fool does not rejoice' — the double statement intensifies: not just sadness but absence of joy. Compare the prodigal son's father's joy at the son's return (Luke 15:24) as the inversion of this portrait.
17.22: Lēb śāmēaḥ yêṭib gēhāh = 'A joyful heart (śāmēaḥ) makes good/is a good healing/medicine' — gēhāh (healing/cure) is the medical term; the joyful heart is therapeutic. Wĕrûaḥ nĕkē'āh tĕyaббēš-gārem = 'but a crushed/stricken spirit dries the bones' — the psychosomatic connection: inner spirit directly affects bodily health. Modern medical understanding of stress physiology resonates with this.
17.23: Šōḥad miḥêq rāšā' yiqqāḥ = 'The wicked takes a bribe from the bosom' — ḥêq (bosom/fold) is where valuables were hidden in ancient clothing; the bribe is taken from its hiding place. Lĕhaṭṭôt 'orḥôt mišpāṭ = 'to pervert the paths of justice' — the specific damage of bribery: not merely corruption but the structural distortion of the justice system itself.
17.24: Pĕnê-mêbîn ḥokmāh = 'Wisdom is before/in the face of the discerning' — the discerning person is always looking at wisdom, always attending to it. Wĕ'ênê kĕsîl biqṣēh-'āreṣ = 'but the eyes of a fool go to the ends of the earth' — the fool's gaze is scattered and unfocused; wisdom and folly are distinguished by where the eyes habitually rest.
17.25: 'A foolish son is grief to his father and bitterness to her who bore him' (kā'as lĕ'ābîw) — the maternal-paternal grief is distinct: kā'as (vexation/anger) for the father; memer (bitterness) for the mother. The deeply personal nature of parental grief at a foolish child is not abstract moralism but the real relational cost of folly.
17.26: Gam 'ănôš laṣṣaddîq lō'-ṭôb = 'Even to punish the righteous is not good' — the fundamental injustice of punishing the innocent. Lehakkôt nĕdîbîm 'al-yōšer = 'to strike nobles for what is right' — a secondary injustice: punishing the honorable for their integrity, which is a form of persecution of virtue.
17.27: Ḥōśēk 'ămārāyw dô'ēa' da'at = 'The one who restrains his words knows knowledge' — ḥāśak (restrain/hold back) is the virtue of verbal restraint; the knowledgeable person recognizes that not every thought needs expression. Wĕqar-rûaḥ 'îš tĕbûnāh = 'and a cool spirit (qar-rûaḥ) is a person of understanding.' Cool-spirited is the antithesis of the hot-tempered person of 14:29; 16:32.
17.28: Gam 'ĕwîl maḥăriš ḥākām yēḥāšēb = 'Even a fool who keeps silent is counted wise' — the paradox: silence can simulate wisdom even in the foolish. 'Ōṭēm śĕpātāyw nābôn = 'the one who keeps his lips shut is discerning.' The implication is not that silence is always wisdom but that wisdom's primary visible marker — restrained speech — can be faked; genuine wisdom requires both the restraint and the inner reality.

18.1: Lĕtā'ăwāh yĕbaqqēš niprad = 'For his desire/appetite the one who separates himself seeks' — the enigmatic opening verse: the person who isolates himself (niprad) does so to pursue his own desire (tā'ăwāh). Bĕkol-tûšîyyāh yitgallā' = 'against all sound wisdom he breaks out' — the self-isolating person is described as acting against communal wisdom; the verse critiques antisocial individualism as desire-driven.
18.2: Lō' yāḥpoṣ kĕsîl bitbûnāh = 'A fool has no delight in understanding (tĕbûnāh)' — not stupidity but deliberate disinterest. Kî 'im-bĕhitgallôt libbô = 'but only in revealing/exposing his heart' — the fool's goal in speech is self-expression, not learning or communication. The diagnostic: what is your speech for?
18.3: Bĕbô'-rāšā' bā' gam-bûz = 'When the wicked comes, comes also contempt' — the wicked person brings an atmosphere of contempt with him; wickedness and contempt are package-deal companions. Wĕ'im-qālôn ḥerpāh = 'and with dishonor comes reproach' — a descending sequence: wickedness → contempt → dishonor → reproach.
18.4: Mayim 'ămuqqîm dibrê pî-'îš = 'Deep waters are the words of a man's mouth' — the depth of speech is the depth of the person; naḥal nōbēa' mĕqôr ḥokmāh = 'a flowing/gushing stream is the fountain of wisdom.' Two water-images in parallel: deep waters suggest the unfathomable resources of the wise person's speech; the flowing brook suggests accessibility and generosity. See also 20:5.
18.5: Śe'ēt pĕnê rāšā' lō'-ṭôb = 'To show partiality/lift the face of the wicked is not good' — nāśā' pānîm (lift the face = show favor/partiality) in legal contexts is the act of letting the guilty off. Lĕhaṭṭôt ṣaddîq bammišpāṭ = 'to thrust aside the righteous in judgment' — the double injustice of the corrupt court echoes 17:15.
18.6: Śiptê ḵĕsîl yābō'û bĕrîb = 'The lips of a fool come into strife/contention' — the fool's speech generates conflict automatically. Ûpîw lĕmahălummôt yiqrā' = 'and his mouth calls for blows' — the fool's mouth invites physical punishment as the natural response to the offense his words generate.
18.7: Pî-ḵĕsîl mĕḥittāh-lô = 'The fool's mouth is his ruin/destruction' (mĕḥittāh = destruction/terror/ruin) — the auto-destruction of the fool through speech. Wĕśĕpātāyw môqēš napšô = 'and his lips are a trap for his soul' — the lips that set the trap in which the soul is caught; speech as self-entrapment.
18.8: Dibrê nirgan kĕmitlahamîm = 'The words of a gossip/whisperer are like delicacies' (mithlahamîm = dainties/sweet morsels) — the appetizing quality of gossip: it is consumed greedily. Wĕhēm yārdû ḥadrê-bāṭen = 'and they go down into the inner chambers of the belly' — gossip enters the deep interior of the listener and cannot easily be expelled; once absorbed, it becomes part of the hearer's inner life.
18.9: Gam mitrappeh bĕmĕla'ktô = 'Also the one who is slack/negligent in his work' — the slothful worker is placed in explicit parallel with the destroyer: 'āḥ hû' lĕba'al mašḥît = 'he is a brother to the one who destroys.' The apparently mild sin of laziness in one's work is categorized as destroying: negligence and active destruction belong in the same moral family.
18.10: Migdal-'ōz šēm YHWH = 'A strong/mighty tower is the name of the LORD' — migdal 'ōz (tower of strength) is a military fortress metaphor applied to YHWH's name. Bô-yārûṣ ṣaddîq wĕniśgāb = 'the righteous runs into it and is set on high/protected' — śāgab (be set on high, inaccessible to attack) describes the military safety of the elevated fortification. The righteous person's refuge is not a place but a name — YHWH's personal identity and character.
18.11: Hôn 'āšîr qiryat 'uzzô = 'The wealth of the rich is his strong city (qiryat 'uzzô)' — the rich person treats wealth as a fortress; ûkĕḥômāh niśgābāh bĕmaśkîtô = 'and like a high wall in his imagination/conceit.' The word maśkît (imagination/mental picture) reveals that the wealthy person's fortress is ultimately psychological — the protection exists in how he perceives his wealth, not in its actual power. Contrast with v10's genuine fortress.
18.12: Lifnê-šeber yigbah lēb-'îš = 'Before destruction the heart of a man is proud' — echoing 16:18 but varying the verb: gābah (be high/proud). Wĕlifnê kābôd 'ănāwāh = 'and before honor is humility' — the reversal pattern of the kingdom: what precedes honor is the opposite of what human nature expects. See Luke 14:11; James 4:10.
18.13: Mēšîb dābār beṭerem yišmā' = 'The one who gives an answer before he hears' — premature response before adequate listening. 'Iwwelet hî'-lô ûkĕlimmāh = 'it is folly and shame to him' — the fool exposes himself by speaking before understanding; the shame is both cognitive (he's wrong) and social (he's seen to be wrong).
18.14: Rûaḥ-'îš yĕkalkēl maḥălēhû = 'The spirit of a man sustains/bears his illness' — resilient inner spirit enables endurance of physical suffering; wĕrûaḥ nĕkē'āh mî yiśśā'ennāh = 'but a crushed spirit, who can bear it?' The crushed spirit (nĕkē'āh, stricken/smitten) is the one load the human frame cannot carry; external suffering is more bearable than internal collapse.
18.15: Lēb nābôn yiqneh-da'at = 'The discerning/understanding heart acquires knowledge' — active pursuit: qānāh (acquire) as in 4:5,7. Wĕ'ōzen ḥăkāmîm tĕbaqqēš-da'at = 'and the ear of the wise seeks knowledge' — the complementary image: the discerning heart acquires and the wise ear seeks; both instruments of reception are active.
18.16: Mattān 'ādām yarḥîb lô = 'A gift of a man makes room for him' (rāḥab = make wide/spacious) — the gift opens doors and creates social access. Wĕlifnê gĕdōlîm yanḥennû = 'and it leads him before great men' — gifts as social currency enabling access to power. The observation is pragmatic, not endorsement of bribery (cf. 17:23).
18.17: Ṣaddîq hārī'šôn bĕrîbô = 'The first to state his case seems righteous' — the rhetorical advantage of being first: before cross-examination, any case appears strong. Ûbā' rē'ēhû ûbĕqārô = 'but his neighbor comes and searches/examines him' — judicial process requires adversarial scrutiny; the truth of any claim is tested by challenge. This is the wisdom basis for adversarial legal systems.
18.18: Midyānîm yašbît haggôrāl = 'Lots put an end to contentions' — the lot as a conflict-resolution mechanism removes the human element of partisanship; YHWH controls the lot (16:33). Ûbên 'aṣûmîm yiphrîd = 'and it separates between the powerful' — when human negotiation between strong parties fails, divine arbitration through the lot provides a result that both must accept.
18.19: 'Āḥ nipšā' miqiryat-'āz = 'A brother offended is harder than a strong city' — the most difficult reconciliation is with one you were closest to; the betrayed intimate becomes impenetrable. Ûmdānîm kimbrîaḥ 'armon = 'and contentions are like the bars of a fortress' — the relational barricade that follows betrayal between brothers is structurally as resistant as castle-bars.
18.20: Mippĕrî pî-'îš tiśba' biṭnô = 'From the fruit of his mouth a man's belly is satisfied' — the harvest-metaphor for speech-consequences: what comes from the mouth returns to nourish or harm the speaker. Tĕbû'at śĕpātāyw yiśbā' = 'the harvest of his lips fills him' — the speaker reaps what he sows with his words.
18.21: Māwet wĕḥayyîm bĕyad-lāšôn = 'Death and life are in the hand/power (yād) of the tongue' — the most sweeping claim in Proverbs about the power of speech. Yād (hand) is the hand of power/agency; the tongue holds life and death in its power as a hand holds a tool. Wĕ'ōhăbêhā yō'kal piryāh = 'and those who love it will eat its fruit' — the tongue-lover reaps exactly what speech produces: life or death.
18.22: Māṣā' 'iššāh māṣā' ṭôb = 'Whoever finds a wife finds a good thing' — māṣā' (find) with ṭôb (good thing/goodness) frames marriage as a discovered gift, not a made arrangement. Wayyāpeq rāṣôn mēYHWH = 'and receives favor (rāṣôn) from the LORD' — the good wife is a form of YHWH's favor; marriage is framed as theologically significant, not merely socially convenient.
18.23: Taḥănûnîm yĕdabbēr-rāš = 'With supplications/entreaties the poor speaks' — the asymmetry of social speech: the poor must beg; wĕ'āšîr ya'aneh 'azzôt = 'but the rich answers harshly/rudely' ('azzôt = harsh/insolent words). Proverbs observes the economic sociology of speech: your social position determines both how you speak and how you are heard.
18.24: 'Îš rē'îm lĕhitrō'ēa' = 'A man of many companions may come to ruin' (or 'must show himself friendly') — the opening phrase is disputed: lĕhitrō'ēa' = 'to show friendship/associate' or 'to his own ruin.' Wĕyēš 'ōhēb dābēq mē'āḥ = 'but there is a friend who sticks closer than a brother' — the quality-vs-quantity contrast: a single faithful friend who dābaq (cleaves/clings) surpasses the shallow social network of many companions.

19.1: “Better is a poor man who walks in his integrity (tōm).” The ṭôb min (better than) comparison formula is a characteristic wisdom device used to challenge conventional valuations. Tōm (integrity, wholeness, completeness — from tāmam, to be whole) describes the undivided person who is the same inside and outside (cf. 28:6 for the near-identical doublet). “Crooked in speech (ʿiqqēš śᵉpātāyw)” — the twisted-lipped person, morally deformed speech as the indicator of inner corruption. Integrity and poverty outrank wealth and deception in Proverbs’ hierarchy of values.
19.2: “Desire (nepeš) without knowledge is not good.” Nepeš here is appetite, craving, the drive of the self — used as the seat of desire (cf. 16:26). Without daʿat (knowledge, discernment) to guide it, strong desire becomes reckless. “The one who moves too fast (ʾāṣ bᵉraglāyw = hurries with his feet) misses the way (ḥōṭēʾ).” Ḥāṭāʾ (to miss, to sin — the basic sin-word: to miss the target or the road) — haste causes a person to miss the path. The antidote is deliberate, knowledge-guided movement.
19.3: “A man’s own foolishness (iwwelet) leads his way astray (tᵉsallēp darkô), yet his heart rages (yizʿap) against the LORD.” Sālap (hiphil: to subvert, twist, overturn — cf. 13:6; 21:12) describes how folly inverts the normal order. Zāʿap (to rage, to be vexed — the same word for Jonah’s anger, Jonah 4:1) — the fool blames God for the consequences of his own choices. The psychological insight is sharp: self-caused disaster generates God-directed resentment rather than self-examination.
19.4: “Wealth (hôn) attracts many friends (yaôsip rēʿîm rabbîm), but the poor man (dāl) is separated (yippārēd) from his neighbor.” A social observation rather than a moral prescription — Proverbs regularly notes how wealth and poverty function in society without endorsing the outcome. Pārad (to divide, separate — cf. Gen 13:9; Prov 17:9) — poverty creates social distance. The contrast with 17:17 (a friend loves at all times) is pointed: wealth attracts fair-weather friends; true friendship is tested by poverty.
19.5: “A false witness (ʿēd šᵉqārim) will not go unpunished (lōʾ-yinnāqeh).” The declaration uses the niphal of nāqāh (to be clean, innocent, acquitted — Exod 34:7; Num 14:18). False witness is an assault on justice at its root; the declaratory formula states divine certainty of consequence (cf. v9 for a near-identical pair). The ninth commandment (Exod 20:16) is the legal background for this repeated warning in Proverbs (6:19; 12:17; 14:5, 25).
19.6: “Many seek (yᵉḥallû) the favor (pᵉnê) of a generous man (nādîb); everyone is a friend (rēaʿ) to the one who gives gifts (mattan).” Ḥālāh (piel: to entreat, seek the face of — cf. Ps 45:12; Mal 1:9) is a courtly gesture of seeking favor. Nādîb (noble, generous person — one who gives freely, from nādab, to volunteer, give willingly) is contrasted with the king of v12. The verse observes without condemning: generosity creates social bonds, but their quality depends on whether the giver notices who is only there for the gifts.
19.7: “All the brothers of a poor man hate him (śānᵉʾûhû) — how much more do his friends keep their distance (rᵉḥēqû mimenû).” The argument qal wāḥōmer (light to heavy): if brothers, with kinship bonds, hate the poor man, how much more mere friends. The final colon — “he may call after them (mᵉraddep ʾᵃmārîm), but they are gone (lōʾ-hēmmāh)” — is difficult and perhaps textually awkward; the sense is the poor man calling after departed friends who no longer exist for him. Poverty’s social isolation is its bitterest dimension.
19.8: “Whoever acquires wisdom (qānāh-lēb = acquires a heart/mind) loves himself (ʾōhēb napšô).” Qānāh (to acquire, get — cf. 4:5, 7; 8:22 where Wisdom is acquired/created by God) — wisdom is something you can buy and own. “Loves himself (napšô)” — in a striking formulation, self-love is here the positive motivation for wisdom: to pursue wisdom is to care properly for oneself. “Whoever holds to (šōmēr) understanding (tᵉbûnāh) will find what is good (limsōʾ-ṭôb)” — keeping wisdom is keeping oneself in the way of flourishing.
19.9: “Whoever breathes out lies (mᵉpiaḥ kᵉzābîm) will perish (yōʾbēd).” The variant ending of v5 — there, “will not escape” (lōʾ-yimmālet); here, “will perish” (yōʾbēd, the destruction-verb) — intensifies the consequence. Pûaḥ (to breathe out, puff) suggests effortless, habitual lying. The doubling of the false-witness warning in vv5 and 9 gives it the emphasis of a refrain — one of the most condemned behaviors in Proverbs.
19.10: “Luxury (taʿᵃnûg) does not suit (lōʾ-nāʾweh) a fool.” Nāweh (fitting, becoming — used of a beautiful pasture in Ps 93:5; Song 1:5) — luxury on a fool is aesthetically and morally wrong, a mismatch. “Even worse for a slave to rule over princes (mišneh kî-ʿebed yimšōl bᵉśārîm)” — mišneh (double, much more) escalates to the social order: a slave ruling over princes is a sign of social inversion (cf. 30:21–23; Eccl 10:7). The verse addresses the category of fitness: things must match their proper level.
19.11: “A man’s wisdom (śekel) makes him slow to anger (heʾĕrîk ʾappô).” Śekel (insight, prudent wisdom) slows the anger response — the wise person delays reaction in order to perceive correctly. “It is his glory (tipʾartô) to overlook (laʿᵃbōr ʿal) an offense (pešaʿ).” Pāšaʿ (rebellion, transgression — cf. Amos 1:3; Ps 51:1) is a strong word for offense; to pass over it (ʿābar) is an act of sovereign restraint. The wisdom that can absorb an affront without retaliation is described as glory (tipʾeret — cf. Exod 28:2 for priestly glory; Prov 4:9), not weakness.
19.12: “A king’s wrath (zaʿap-melek) is like the roaring (kᵉnaham) of a lion (kᵉpîr), but his favor (rᵉṣônô) is like dew (ṭal) upon the grass.” The lion and dew comparison for royal power appears also in 20:2 and 16:15. Kᵉpîr (young lion — the most dangerous and aggressive) for royal wrath; ṭal (dew — the gentle moisture that falls overnight, source of life in Palestine’s dry season) for royal favor. The king’s power to destroy or to nourish is absolute.
19.13: “A foolish son (bēn kᵉsîl) is ruin (hawwōt) to his father, and a wife’s quarreling (midyanê ʾišāh) is like a constant drip (ṭēret dôlep).” Hawwāh (ruin, calamity, disaster — from hāwāh, to fall, come to nothing; cf. Job 6:2; Ps 38:12) is the word for utter destruction. The dripping roof metaphor (ṭārap = dripping; dōlep = rain, dripping) recurs in 27:15 — the irritation of unceasing minor agitation wearing down the one who endures it.
19.14: “A house and wealth (hôn) can be inherited from one’s father, but a sensible wife (ʾišāh maśkîlet) comes from (mēʾēt) the LORD.” Two categories of good: the transferable (property, money) and the non-transferable (a wise wife). Maśkîlet (prudent, insightful — the hiphil participle of śākal) designates a woman of practical wisdom. That she “comes from the LORD” places her in the category of divine gift, like wisdom itself (2:6) — she cannot be earned or inherited, only received.
19.15: “Laziness (ʿaṣlāh) brings on a deep sleep (tardēmāh), and the idle person (nepeš rᵉmîyyāh) will go hungry.” Tardēmāh (deep sleep — the sleep of stupor rather than rest; the same word for the sleep God placed on Adam in Gen 2:21 and the deep sleep of the seers) conveys a stupefied paralysis. Nepeš rᵉmîyyāh (a slack soul — the soul without tension or drive) will find its fridge empty. The connection between sloth and hunger is one of Proverbs’ most repeated practical teachings.
19.16: “Whoever keeps (šōmēr) the commandment (miṣwāh) keeps his life (šōmēr napšô); whoever is careless (bôzēh) about his conduct (dᵉrākāyw) will die.” The link between commandment-keeping and life is Deuteronomic at its core (Deut 4:40; 6:2; 30:16). Bāzāh (to despise, treat with contempt) — carelessness toward one’s own conduct is an act of contempt toward the very structure of life. The symmetry is tight: the verb šāmar (to keep, guard) governs both commandment and life — keep the commandment and you keep yourself.
19.17: “Whoever is kind to the poor (ḥônēn dāl) lends to (malweh) the LORD, and he will repay him for his deed (gᵉmûlô yᵉšallem-lô).” The metaphor is audacious: a loan to God. Māwāh (to lend — cf. Exod 22:25) transposes the act of charitable giving into a commercial transaction with divine security. The poor are so identified with God that helping them is extending credit to the Almighty. Gāmal (to repay, deal bountifully — cf. Ps 13:6; 116:7; 119:17) guarantees the return.
19.18: “Discipline (yassēr) your son while there is still hope (kî-yēš tiqwāh); do not set your heart on his destruction (ʾal-tittᵉ napšᵉkā lahᵃmîtô).” The hope-window is temporal: there is a period when discipline can form character; after that period closes, it cannot. Tiqwāh (hope, expectation — from qāwāh, to wait, hope; also “cord” in Josh 2:18) names the window of formability. “Do not set your heart on his destruction” — the negative caution: undisciplined indulgence (“letting him have his way”) actually leads toward the child’s death, not his flourishing.
19.19: “A man of great wrath (gᵉdal-ḥēmāh) will bear the penalty (yiśśāʾ-ʿōneš); if you rescue him (ṣiltāh), you will only have to do it again (yôsip).” Ḥēmāh (wrath, heat, poison — cf. 6:34; 27:4) — the hot-tempered man creates consequences. The second clause is a practical warning to would-be helpers: rescuing someone from the natural consequences of their hot temper does not change the temper; it enables the next episode. Proverbs here is psychologically astute about the limits of rescue.
19.20: “Listen to counsel (šᵉmaʿ ʿēṣāh) and accept instruction (qabbēl mûsār), that you may gain wisdom (tᵉḥkam) in the end (bᵉʾaḥᵃrîtekā).” The temporal horizon is key: wisdom is not always immediately gratifying; its fruit appears “in the end” (ʾaḥᵃrît — the latter end, the future outcome; cf. 5:11; 14:12; 23:18; 24:14). The willingness to receive counsel and correction now is the investment that pays wisdom later.
19.21: “Many are the plans (maḥᵃšābôt) in a man’s heart, but the LORD’s purpose (ʿᵃṣat-YHWH) is what stands (hîʾ tāqûm).” Maḥᵃšābāh (plan, thought, design — from ḥāšab, to think, reckon, plan) versus ʿēṣāh (counsel, purpose, deliberate plan — the counsel of God that no human scheme can overturn). The sovereignty of divine purpose over human planning echoes 16:1, 9; 21:30–31. The human plans are numerous; the divine purpose is single and invincible.
19.22: “What is desired (taʾᵃwat) in a person is faithful love (ḥasdô), and a poor man is better than a liar.” The first clause is difficult: taʾᵃwāh (desire, what is longed for) combined with ḥesed (faithful love) may mean “the desirable quality in a person is his ḥesed” or “the desire a person inspires depends on his ḥesed.” Either way, ḥesed (covenant loyalty, steadfast love — here in the human virtue dimension) is the most desirable trait. The comparison “better a poor man than a liar” repeats 19:1’s poverty-integrity comparison with a different pairing.
19.23: “The fear of the LORD (yirʾat-YHWH) leads to life (lᵉḥayyîm); whoever has it will rest satisfied (śābēaʿ yālîn) and will not be touched by evil (lōʾ-yippāqqēd rāʿāh).” The programmatic thesis of 1:7 is here unfolded into its consequences: life, satisfaction, immunity from evil. Lîn (to lodge overnight, to stay — cf. Ps 91:1) suggests the security of one who rests undisturbed. Pāqad (to visit, attend to, appoint — the same word used of God visiting Israel with judgment or blessing) — evil will not visit the one who fears the LORD.
19.24: “The sluggard (ʿāṣēl) buries (ṭāman) his hand in the dish and will not even bring it to his mouth again.” The most absurdist image in Proverbs — a man too lazy to complete the motion of eating. Ṭāman (to bury, hide — cf. 2:7; Gen 35:4) — the hand is “buried” in the dish, inert. The verse is satirical, approaching caricature, but the point is precise: the extreme of sloth is self-starvation while food is available. The same image appears in 26:15.
19.25: “Strike (hak) a mocker (lēṣ) and the naive (petaʾî) will gain caution (yaʿrum); correct (hôkîaḥ) a man of understanding (nābôn) and he gains knowledge.” A ladder of responses to correction: the naive learns vicariously (watching punishment produces caution); the man of understanding learns directly from reproof. The mocker is not improved by his own punishment — its benefit falls on bystanders. Different levels of wisdom require different pedagogical approaches.
19.26: “A son who mistreats (mᵉšaddēd) his father and drives away (mᵉbîaḥ) his mother brings shame (bôš) and disgrace (ḥerpāh).” Šādad (to devastate, to rob by violence — cf. Isa 21:2; Prov 11:3) — the son who robs or crushes the father violates the foundation of the family order (Exod 21:17; Prov 20:20). Ḥerpāh (reproach, public shame — the worst social outcome) falls on the perpetrator, not the victim. The inversion of the fifth commandment brings maximum social disgrace.
19.27: “Stop listening (ḥadal) to instruction, my son, and you will drift (lišgôt) from words of knowledge.” A deliberately paradoxical address: the teacher tells the student to stop listening — as a warning about what results if they do. Šāgāh (to wander, go astray — the same root as drunken staggering in Isa 28:7; Prov 5:19–20) — ceasing to receive instruction produces moral wandering from knowledge. The verse makes audible the consequence of closing one’s ears.
19.28: “A corrupt witness (ʿēd bᵉliyyaʾal) mocks justice (yālîṣ mišpāṭ), and the mouth of the wicked gulps down (yᵉballaʾ) iniquity (ʾāwen).” Bᵉliyyaʾal (worthlessness, the thing that profits nothing — the epithet of the person with no redeeming value; cf. 6:12; 1 Sam 2:12) — the “worthless witness” corrupts the entire judicial system. Bālaʿ (to swallow, gulp down — cf. 1:12 where Sheol swallows) — the wicked devour iniquity as food, consuming it greedily rather than being polluted by it accidentally.
19.29: “Penalties (šᵉpāṭîm) are set in store for mockers (lēṣîm), and beatings (maḥᵃlumôt) for the backs of fools (ḥᵉsîlîm).” The judicial term šᵉpāṭ (judgment, penalty) is normally used of divine or royal verdicts; here it is applied to the mocker’s inevitable fate. “Beatings for the backs of fools” — the corporal punishment designed for those who refuse verbal correction (cf. 17:10; 26:3). The chapter ends with the certainty of consequence for those who mock both wisdom and justice.

20.1: “Wine (yayin) is a mocker (lēṣ) and strong drink (šēkār) a brawler (hōmeh); whoever is led astray (šōgeh) by it is not wise.” The personification of wine as a lēṣ (scoffer/mocker — the most dangerous human type in Proverbs) gives the substance the characteristics of the most destructive human character. Hāmāh (to roar, to be boisterous — used of the crowds, the sea, the city; cf. 1:21; 9:13; Ps 46:3) — strong drink makes men riotous. Šāgāh (to stagger, go astray — as in 19:27) — the drunkard wanders from the path of wisdom.
20.2: “The terror (ʾêmat) of a king is like the roaring (kᵉnaham) of a lion (kᵉpîr); whoever provokes him to anger (mitʿabbᵉrô) puts his own life (napšô) at risk (ḥôṭēʾ).” Nearly identical to 19:12, but here the focus falls on the danger of provoking royal wrath — ʿābar in the hithpael (to make oneself pass over, to become enraged) describes the king going beyond normal limits in anger. Ḥāṭāʾ (to sin, to miss — used in 19:2 for missing the path) — provoking the king is to sin against one’s own life.
20.3: “It is an honor (kābôd) for a man to stay out of conflict (šebet mêrîb), but every fool (ʾewîl) will be drawn into quarreling (yitgallāʿ).” Šebet (sitting, staying — the quiet of non-engagement) in contrast to rîb (strife, lawsuit, quarrel — the legal word for dispute). Gālaʿ (to quarrel, to break out — hithpael: to involve oneself in a brawl) — the fool cannot resist the pull of a fight. Honor (kābôd) in Proverbs is consistently given to restraint and wisdom, not to aggressive self-assertion.
20.4: “The sluggard does not plow (lōʾ-yaḥᵃrōš) in the cold season (ḥōrep); at harvest (qāṣîr) he will look for food and find nothing.” Ḥōrep (autumn, the cold-wet season — the plowing season in Palestine) — the window for sowing is also the uncomfortable season. The sluggard’s avoidance of seasonal labor forfeits the harvest. The agricultural calendar is moralized: refusal to work in the hard season produces hunger in the good season. The verse is one of the most concrete images of the consequences of sloth.
20.5: “The counsel (ʿēṣāh) in a man’s heart (bᵉleb-ʾîš) is like deep water (mayim ʿᵃmûqqîm), but a man of understanding (ʾîš tᵉbûnāh) draws it out (yidlennāh).” Deep water requires a drawing-vessel to access; wisdom in the heart requires another wise person to draw it out. Dālāh (to draw water — the word used by the daughters of Reuel in Exod 2:16) applied to drawing out a person’s inner wisdom. The image commends the kind of deep listening that extracts wisdom others cannot reach themselves.
20.6: “Many people proclaim (yiqrāʾ) their own loyalty (ḥasdô), but a truly faithful person — who can find (mî yimṣāʾ)?” The rhetorical question exposes the gap between claimed and actual ḥesed. Ḥesed (steadfast love, faithful loyalty) is the highest covenant virtue — many claim it, few embody it. The question “who can find?” echoes the search for wisdom herself (8:17) and the capable wife (31:10), placing faithful love in the category of rare and precious discoveries.
20.7: “A righteous man (ṣaddîq) who walks in his integrity (bᵉtummô) — blessed are his children (ʾašrê bānāyw) after him.” The ʾašrê (beatitude) formula applies not to the righteous man himself but to his children — they inherit the blessedness of their father’s integrity. Tōm (integrity — cf. 19:1) is the quality of wholeness that generates generational blessing (cf. Ps 112:1–2). The father’s righteousness radiates outward into his posterity.
20.8: “A king who sits (yōšēb) on the throne of judgment (kissēʾ-dîn) scatters (mᵉzāreh) all evil with his eyes (bᵉʿênāyw).” Zārāh (to scatter, winnow — the act of throwing grain into the air to separate wheat from chaff; cf. Jer 15:7; 31:10) applied to the king’s judicial discernment. The eyes that “scatter evil” are the penetrating gaze of the just judge who exposes and disperses wickedness by the mere act of righteous seeing. The king as winnower is a significant image for divine judgment (Matt 3:12).
20.9: “Who can say, ‘I have made my heart pure (zikkîtî libbî); I am clean (ṭāhartî) from my sin’?” The rhetorical question expects the answer: no one. Zākāh (to be pure, clean — the root of zāk, pure; cf. Job 15:14–16; 25:4) and ṭāhēr (to be ritually/morally clean) — both purity terms. The verse is the closest thing in Proverbs to a doctrine of universal sinfulness (cf. Ps 51:5; Eccl 7:20). No one can truthfully claim self-achieved moral purity; the honest person knows this about themselves.
20.10: “Unequal weights (ʾeben wāʾāben) and unequal measures (ʾêpāh wᵉʾêpāh) — both are detestable (tôʿᵃbat) to the LORD.” Two stones (weights) and two ephahs (dry measures) — the rigged scale. Tôʿēbāh (abomination — the strongest negative assessment term; cf. 3:32; 11:1; 12:22) places commercial fraud in the category of the most offensive sins against God. The Mosaic law is explicit (Lev 19:35–36; Deut 25:13–16; Amos 8:5); Proverbs repeats the condemnation in 11:1; 20:23.
20.11: “Even a child (naʿar) is known (yitnakkēr) by his actions (bᵉmaʿᵃlālāyw), whether his conduct is pure (zāk) and right (yāšār).” Nākar (hithpael: to make oneself known, to be recognized — cf. 26:24; Gen 42:7) — even at the child level, behavior reveals character. Maʿᵃlāl (deed, action — usually of significant or evil acts; cf. Isa 1:16; Jer 4:4; Ps 77:11) — a person’s deeds are their introduction. The verse commends early character formation: the child already has a moral track record.
20.12: “The hearing ear (ʾōzen šōmaʿat) and the seeing eye (ʿayin rōʾāh) — the LORD made them both.” A creation-theology observation: the faculties by which wisdom enters the person — hearing and sight — are divine gifts. They carry an implicit obligation: organs made by God for wisdom-reception should be used for that purpose (cf. 17:24; Ps 94:9; Isa 6:9–10). The verse also grounds epistemology in creation: our capacity to perceive truly is a gift to be honored.
20.13: “Do not love sleep (ʾal-tʾehab šēnāh), or you will come to poverty (tiwwārēš); keep your eyes open (pᵉqaḥ ʿênekā) and you will have plenty of food (tiśbaʿ-lāḥem).” The sleep-love of the sluggard (cf. 6:9–11; 24:33–34; 19:15) produces poverty. Pāqaḥ (to open the eyes — the same word as when Hagar’s eyes were opened in Gen 21:19; when blind eyes are opened in Isa 35:5) — alertness is the opposite of sloth, and it produces abundance (śābēaʿ, fullness).
20.14: “Bad, bad (raʿ raʿ), says the buyer, but when he goes away, he boasts (yithallel) of his deal.” A scene from marketplace psychology — the buyer deprecates the merchandise to drive down the price, then brags about the bargain when safely away. No moral condemnation is explicit; the verse is an observation about commercial human nature and the strategic use of speech. Hāllal (hithpael: to boast — cf. 27:1–2; Ps 34:2) — self-congratulation in private after deceptive speech in public.
20.15: “There is gold (zāhāb) and plenty of rubies (pᵉnînîm), but the lips that speak knowledge (śiptê-daʿat) are the truly precious thing (kᵉlî yāqār).” The three-level comparison: gold, rubies (already at the top of material value), and speech-that-gives-knowledge. Kᵉlî yāqār (a precious vessel, a rare implement) — knowledge-bearing speech is rarer and more valuable than the rarest physical commodity. The image carries the same rhetorical weight as 3:14–15 and 8:10–11.
20.16: “Take the garment (beged) of one who puts up security (ʿārab) for a stranger; hold it in pledge (baḥᵃbōlēhû) for those who are surety for foreigners.” The practical wisdom of collateral: surety for strangers is high-risk lending (cf. 6:1–5; 11:15; 17:18). ʿĀrab (to mix oneself in, to become surety — cf. Gen 43:9) — pledging your own property for another’s debt. The advice is essentially: insist on security from reckless pledgers. The verse is repeated nearly verbatim in 27:13.
20.17: “Bread (leḥem) gained by deceit (šeqer) is sweet (yiʿᵃrab) to a man, but afterward (ʾaḥᵃrîtô) his mouth will be full of gravel (ḥāṣāṣ).” The temporal structure — sweet now, grit later — is the standard Proverbs pattern for folly’s attractive lies (cf. 5:3–4 for the adulteress). ʿĀreb (pleasant to the taste — cf. 3:24; 13:19) versus ḥāṣāṣ (gravel, small stones — what results when food turns to grit). Deceit masquerades as sweetness; its exposure fills the mouth with the inedible.
20.18: “Plans (maḥᵃšābôt) are established by seeking advice (bᵉʿēṣāh), and with wise guidance (taḥbulôt) you can wage war (taʿᵃśeh milḥāmāh).” Maḥᵃšābāh (plan — cf. 19:21) requires communal wisdom to be sound. Taḥbulôt (guidance, skillful steering — the nautical term from 1:5) now appears in a military context: strategic planning requires the wisdom of many counselors (cf. 11:14; 15:22; 24:6). The wisdom community, not the lone genius, is the source of reliable strategy.
20.19: “The gossip (hôlēk rākîl) reveals secrets (gôleh-sôd) wherever he goes; avoid anyone who talks too freely (ʿim-pōteh śᵉpātāyw lōʾ titʿārab).” Rākîl (slander, gossip — from rākal, to go about trading secrets; cf. Lev 19:16; Jer 6:28) — the gossip trades in other people’s private information. Pātāh śᵉpātāyw (one who opens his lips — talks freely, without restraint) — the advice is relational: avoid entering into confidences with those who cannot keep them (cf. 11:13; 18:8).
20.20: “Whoever curses (mᵉqallel) his father or his mother — his lamp (nērô) will be snuffed out (yidʿak) in deepest darkness (bᵉʾîšôn ḥōšek).” Qālal (piel: to curse, to treat as trivial and contemptible — the opposite of kābēd, to honor; Exod 21:17) — the fifth commandment violation inverted. Nēr (lamp — the life-symbol; cf. 13:9; Job 18:6; 21:17) extinguished “in the deepest darkness (ʾîšôn = the pupil of the eye, the darkest center)” — a particularly evocative phrase for total obliteration.
20.21: “An inheritance (naḥᵃlāh) quickly gained (mᵉbōhelet) at first will not be blessed (lōʾ tᵉbōrak) in the end.” Bāhal (pual: to be hastened, seized prematurely) — the inheritance obtained ahead of its proper time (by manipulation, impatience, or the death-wish toward parents) is cursed rather than blessed. The principle mirrors 13:11 (“wealth gotten hastily will dwindle”). Patient reception of what comes in due course is contrasted with greedy seizure of what is not yet yours.
20.22: “Do not say, ‘I will repay (ʾᵃšallem-rāʿāh) evil’; wait on (qawwēh) the LORD and he will deliver you (wᵉyōšaʿ-lāk).” Qāwāh (to wait, to hope — the same root as tiqwāh, hope, in 10:28; 11:7; 13:12) — the posture of patient trust rather than active vengeance. “He will deliver you (yōšaʿ)” — the salvation-verb, from the root of Yeshua/Joshua. The proverb anticipates the NT teaching on non-retaliation (Rom 12:17–19, which quotes Deut 32:35; cf. Matt 5:39).
20.23: “Unequal weights (ʾeben wāʾāben) are detestable (tôʿᵃbat) to the LORD, and dishonest scales (mōʾzᵉnê mirmāh) are no good (lōʾ-ṭôb).” A near-repetition of v10, using slightly different vocabulary. Mirmāh (deceit, fraud — cf. Ps 5:6; Zeph 1:9) combined with mōʾzᵉnayim (scales, balance) — the dishonest scale embodies deceptive commercial practice. The repetition reinforces the absolute nature of the condemnation: commercial fraud is not a minor infraction but a divine abomination.
20.24: “A man’s steps (miṣʿᵃdê-gāber) are directed by the LORD (mēYHWH); how then can anyone understand (yābîn) his own path (darkô)?” Gāber (a strong man, a warrior — cf. Job 3:3; 38:3) — even the strong man’s steps are divinely directed. The rhetorical question acknowledges the limits of human self-understanding: if God directs your steps, you cannot from your vantage point trace the full pattern of your own path. This does not produce fatalism but humility (cf. 16:9; 19:21).
20.25: “It is a snare (mōqēš) for a man to dedicate something rashly (yālaʿ qōdeš) and only afterward to reconsider (ʾaḥar nᵉdārîm lᵉbaqqēr).” yālaʿ (to swallow up, here in the sense of rash vowing — cf. Eccl 5:2–6; Num 30) — a hasty dedication to God becomes a trap when one later has second thoughts. Neder (vow — solemn promise to God; Num 30:2; Deut 23:21–23) cannot be undone casually. The wisdom is: think before you vow.
20.26: “A wise king (melek ḥākām) scatters (mᵉzāreh) the wicked and drives (wayyāšeb) the threshing wheel (ʾôpān) over them.” The winnowing image of v8 reappears: the just king’s justice is agricultural separation — wicked chaff from righteous grain. ʾôpān (wheel, threshing wheel) — the heavy wheel rolled over grain to separate it. The violence of the image (the wicked crushed by the threshing wheel) underscores that wise kingship cannot be purely passive benevolence; it must actively deal with wickedness.
20.27: “The human spirit (nišmat ʾādām) is the LORD’s lamp (nēr-YHWH), searching (ḥōpēś) all the innermost parts (ḥadrê-bāṭen) of a person.” Nišmāh (breath, spirit — cf. Gen 2:7 where God breathed nišmat ḥayyîm into Adam) — the divine life-breath in the human person becomes God’s lamp for illuminating the inner self. Ḥōpēś (searching, probing — cf. Jer 17:10; Ps 139:1) — the divine-origin spirit searches the deepest chambers of the person’s interior. Conscience is here understood as the divine light within the human frame.
20.28: “Steadfast love (ḥesed) and faithfulness (ʾĕmet) preserve (yiṣṣᵉrû) the king, and his throne is upheld (sāmûk) by faithful love (ḥesed).” The paired covenant-character terms of Exod 34:6–7 and Prov 3:3 now applied to royal governance: the king who embodies ḥesed-wĕʾĕmet secures his throne. ḥesed (steadfast love) appears twice — framing the verse — emphasizing that covenant loyalty is the foundation, not military power or political cunning (cf. 29:14; Ps 89:14).
20.29: “The glory (tipʾeret) of young men (bᵉḥûrîm) is their strength (kōḥām), but the honor (hadar) of old men (zᵉqēnîm) is their gray hair (śêbāh).” A complementarity observation: each stage of life has its appropriate glory. Tipʾeret (splendor, beauty, glory — cf. 4:9; Isa 28:1; Ezek 16:12) for the vigor of youth; hādār (dignity, majesty — cf. Ps 96:6; 111:3) for the experienced elder. Śêbāh (gray hair — cf. 16:31; Lev 19:32) is the badge of the zāqēn (elder/elders), who earned both the title and the dignity through years of lived wisdom.
20.30: “Painful blows (ḥabburvôt-pāreʿ) cleanse away (yitmᵉraq) evil (bᵉrāʿ); wounds (makkôt) reach the innermost parts (ḥadrê-bāṭen) of a person.” Ḥabbûrāh (welt, bruise — cf. Isa 1:6; 53:5) and makkāh (blow, wound) — physical correction has a purifying effect (yitmᵉraq = to be scoured clean, polished). “Reaches the innermost parts” (ḥadrê-bāṭen — the same phrase as v27) — the correction that scours the surface also reaches the deepest interior. Discipline penetrates where words cannot.

21.1: “The king’s heart (leb-melek) is in the hand of the LORD like channels of water (pelagê-māyim); he directs it (yaṭṭennû) wherever he pleases (ʾᵃšer yaḥpōṣ).” Peleg (irrigation channel, canal — cf. Ps 1:3; 65:9; Isa 30:25) — not the unpredictable flood but the directed stream. The agricultural image of irrigation channels emphasizes sovereign control: the king’s heart is not an autonomous power center but is channeled by God toward where he directs it. The most powerful human ruler is in the divine hand (cf. Isa 44:28; 45:1).
21.2: “Every man thinks his own way (darkô) is right (yāšār bᵉʿênāyw), but the LORD weighs (tōkēn) the heart (libbôt).” Yāšār bᵉʿênāyw (straight in his own eyes — cf. 14:12; 16:2; 21:2b) — subjective moral self-assessment is inherently unreliable. Tākan (to weigh, measure — the verb of the divine balance; cf. 16:2; 24:12; 1 Sam 2:3; Job 28:25) — the LORD’s weighing of hearts is the objective standard that corrects self-deception. The same thought as 16:2 with slightly different vocabulary.
21.3: “To do righteousness (ʿᵃśôt ṣᵉdāqāh) and justice (mišpāṭ) is more acceptable (bāḥar) to the LORD than sacrifice (mizbāḥ).” One of the clearest expressions in Proverbs of the prophetic critique of empty ritual (cf. 1 Sam 15:22; Hos 6:6; Amos 5:21–24; Isa 1:11–17; Mic 6:6–8). Bāḥar (to choose, prefer — the election verb: God prefers moral action to liturgical performance). The verse does not abolish sacrifice but insists that ethical action is its prerequisite and its meaning.
21.4: “Haughty eyes (rûm-ʿênayim) and a proud heart (rᵉḥab-lēb) — even the tillage (nîr) of the wicked — are sin (ḥaṭṭāʾt).” Rûm ʿênayim (high eyes — the lifted gaze of arrogance; cf. Ps 18:27; 131:1; Prov 6:17 where haughty eyes head the seven abominations). Nîr (freshly plowed field, fallow ground — cf. Jer 4:3; Hos 10:12) is difficult here: possibly “the lamp of the wicked” (an alternative reading) or “even the tillage of the wicked is sin” — even their productive work is morally contaminated. The second reading emphasizes that pride corrupts the whole person’s activity.
21.5: “The plans (maḥšᵉbôt) of the diligent (ḥārûṣ) lead to abundance (limôtār), but hasty action (kol-ʾāṣ) leads only to poverty (lᵉmaḥsôr).” Ḥārûṣ (diligent, decisive, incisive — from ḥāraṣ, to cut, decide sharply; cf. 10:4; 12:24) versus ʾūṣ (to hurry, to hasten — the anxiety-driven rush). Môtār (surplus, profit — what is left over after abundance) versus maḥsôr (lack, need — the state of deficiency; cf. 14:23; 28:19). Deliberate planning yields surplus; impulsive haste produces deficit.
21.6: “Treasures (ʾôṣārôt) gained by a lying tongue (bᵉlāšôn šāqer) are a fleeting vapor (hebel niddāp) — a deadly snare (mᵉbaqšê māwet).” Hebel (breath, vapor — Ecclesiastes’ signature word; cf. Eccl 1:2) applied to fraudulently-gained wealth: it evaporates as quickly as breath. The second image “deadly snare (mᵉbaqšê māwet = those who seek death)” — the pursuit of such treasures is not merely futile but lethal. Ill-gotten gains evaporate; their pursuit kills.
21.7: “The violence (šōd) of the wicked will drag (yᵉgōrem) them away, because they refuse (mēʾᵃnû) to do what is right (mišpāṭ).” Šōd (destruction, violence — from šādad, to devastate; cf. 24:2; Isa 13:6) — the violent act becomes the agent of the violent person’s own destruction. Gāram (to drag away, cause — an unusual verb; cf. Num 24:22) — the very violence the wicked commit sweeps them away. The refusal of justice (mišpāṭ) is the cause; being dragged away by one’s own violence is the effect.
21.8: “The way (derek) of the guilty person (ʾîš wāwāh) is crooked (ḥᵃpakpak) and strange (wāzār); the conduct (poʿᵃlô) of the pure (zak) is upright (yāšār).” Wāwāh (guilt, wrong — rare; cf. Job 15:5) combined with ḥᵃpakpak (double-twisted, very crooked — an intensive reduplicated form of hāpak, to overturn) describes the maximally inverted moral direction. Zak (pure, clean — cf. 20:9; Job 11:4) produces yāšār (straight, upright) — purity and moral straightness go together.
21.9: “It is better (ṭôb) to live in a corner of the roof (bᵉpinnat-gāg) than in a spacious house (bêt ḥāber) with a quarrelsome woman (ʾēšet midyānîm).” One of the domestic ṭôb-min (better-than) proverbs; cf. 21:19; 25:24 (near-identical); 19:13. Pinnat-gāg (corner of a flat roof — exposed, uncomfortable, crowded) versus the spacious shared house. ʾēšet midyānîm (a woman of quarreling/disputes — midyān from dîn, to judge/dispute; cf. 19:13; 27:15) — the structural parallel between domestic conflict and homelessness makes a sharp point about the value of peace over comfort.
21.10: “The wicked desire (awwᵉtāh nepeš rāšāʿ) evil; they show no kindness (lōʾ-yuḥam) even to their neighbor (bᵉʿênāyw rēʿēhû).” ʾāwāh (to desire, crave — the same root as taʾᵃwāh, desire; cf. 19:22) — the wicked’s appetite is oriented entirely toward evil. Ḥānan (to show grace, be kind — pual here: to be shown kindness) — in their neighbor’s eyes, they find no grace, show no grace. The total corruption of the social instincts: the wicked cannot be gracious to those nearest them.
21.11: “When a mocker (lēṣ) is punished (baʿᵃnāš), the naive (petaʾî) becomes wise (yaḥkam); when a wise person (ḥākām) receives instruction (bᵉhaśkîl), he gains knowledge (yiqqaḥ-daʿat).” Identical in structure to 19:25 — the three-level pedagogy of observing punishment (naive → wisdom), receiving direct instruction (wise → more knowledge). The mocker’s punishment is wasted on him but not on those watching; the wise person needs no theatrical punishment, only direct teaching.
21.12: “The righteous (ṣaddîq) perceive (maśkîl) the house of the wicked; God (ʾĕlōhîm) brings the wicked down (mᵉsallēp) to ruin.” The subject of “perceive” is ambiguous — “the Righteous One” (a title for God) or “the righteous person.” The MKT reads it as the righteous person who discerns what is happening in the wicked’s house; God acts as judge. Sālap (to overthrow, subvert — cf. 13:6; 19:3) — God is the agent of the wicked’s downfall, though the righteous perceives it.
21.13: “Whoever stops his ears (ʾōṭēm ʾoznô) at the cry of the poor (mizzaʿᵃqat-dāl) will himself cry out (gam-hûʾ yiqrāʾ) and not be heard (wᵉlōʾ yēʿāneh).” The retributive symmetry: the one who closes ears to the poor will find his own cries unanswered. ʾāṭam (to stop up, shut — cf. Isa 33:15; Prov 17:28 for stopping the ears as wisdom) — here used of a refusal to hear need. The verse draws a direct line from social deafness to unanswered prayer (cf. 1:24–28 where wisdom does not answer those who refused to hear).
21.14: “A secret gift (mattān basseter) calms (yikpeh) anger (ʾap), and a concealed bribe (šōḥad baḥēq) soothes fierce wrath (ḥēmāh ʿazzāh).” Kāpāh (to subdue, placate — rare; cf. Ps 78:31; Prov 26:20) — the quenching of anger by a discreet gift. The verse observes a social reality without explicit endorsement (cf. 17:8 for a similar observation). “In the bosom (baḥēq)” — tucked into the fold of the garment, out of sight. The secrecy is key: a private gift preserves face on both sides.
21.15: “It is a joy (śimḥāh) to the righteous (laṣṣaddîq) to do justice (ʿᵃśôt mišpāṭ), but it brings terror (mᵉḥittāh) to evildoers.” Śimḥāh (joy, gladness — cf. 10:28; 12:20; Deut 16:11) — justice is something the righteous delight in, not merely endure. Mᵉḥittāh (dismay, ruin, terror — from ḥātat, to be shattered, dismayed; cf. 10:14–15) — the same justice that is joy to the righteous is experienced as destruction by the wicked. Justice is not neutral: its effect depends entirely on one’s moral position relative to it.
21.16: “Whoever wanders (tōʿeh) from the way of understanding (midderek haśkēl) will come to rest (yānûaḥ) among the dead (biqhal rᵉpāʾîm).” Tāʿāh (to wander, go astray — cf. Isa 53:6; Ps 119:176; Prov 10:17) — the one who strays from wisdom’s way ends in Sheol. Rᵉpāʾîm (the shades of the dead — cf. 2:18; 9:18; Isa 14:9) — Proverbs uses the Sheol imagery to underscore that wisdom’s path is a literal life-or-death matter. Rest among the dead is the final “home” of the wisdom-abandoner.
21.17: “Whoever loves pleasure (ʾōhēb śimḥāh) will be a poor man; whoever loves wine (yayin) and olive oil (šemen) will not grow rich.” The three luxury goods — pleasure, wine, oil — represent the good life pursued for its own sake. Šemen (olive oil — the cooking fat, cosmetic oil, and wealth-indicator of the ancient world) combined with wine forms the grain-wine-oil prosperity triad (Deut 7:13). The lover of these things pursues consumption rather than production and becomes poor through expenditure without replenishment.
21.18: “The wicked (rāšāʿ) are a substitute (kōper) for the righteous, and the treacherous (bôgēd) take the place of the upright (yāšār).” Kōper (ransom, substitute — the payment that releases another from an obligation or penalty; cf. Exod 21:30; 30:12; Num 35:31) — the wicked function as the ransom/substitute for the righteous. The verse expresses a retributive economy: what the righteous escaped falls upon the wicked (cf. Isa 43:3–4 where God gives Egypt as Israel’s ransom). The treacherous receive what the upright were spared.
21.19: “It is better to live in a desert (ʾereṣ-midbār) than with a quarrelsome (midyānîm) and angry (kaʿas) woman.” The escalation from 21:9 (corner of a roof) to the desert — a place of total barrenness — shows how intolerable the quarrelsome domestic environment is by comparison. Kaʿas (vexation, anger — cf. 12:16; 17:25; 1 Sam 1:6) combined with constant quarreling creates a domestic environment worse than wilderness isolation. The proverb is not anti-woman but rather an extreme statement about the unbearability of constant conflict.
21.20: “Precious treasure (ʾôṣār neḥmād) and oil (šemen) are stored (linwēh) in the house of the wise (ḥākām), but a fool (ʾewîl) quickly squanders (yᵉballaʾennû) them.” Neḥmād (precious, desirable — from ḥāmad, to desire; cf. Gen 3:6; Exod 20:17) — the wise person accumulates desirable things through self-discipline. Bālaʿ (to swallow, consume quickly — cf. 1:12; 19:28) — the fool swallows his inheritance in a gulp. The contrast is between wise preservation and foolish consumption.
21.21: “Whoever pursues (rōdēp) righteousness (ṣᵉdāqāh) and steadfast love (ḥesed) will find life (yimṣāʾ ḥayyîm), righteousness, and honor (kābôd).” Rādap (to pursue, chase — the same verb used of enemies pursuing in warfare; Deut 30:7; Ps 23:6) — righteousness and ḥesed are not passive acquisitions but the objects of an active chase. The triad of rewards — life, righteousness, honor — echoes 3:16 (long life, wealth, honor in wisdom’s hands). The pursuit of virtue finds virtue itself, plus life and honor as the bonus.
21.22: “A wise man (ḥākām) can take (ʿālāh) a city of warriors (ʿîr gibbōrîm) and bring down (wayyōred) the stronghold (ʿōz mibtaḥô) in which they trust.” Wisdom over force — the intellectual over the military. ʿāzāz mibtāḥô (the strength in which they trust — mibtāḥ from bāṭaḥ, to trust) — the warriors trusted in their fortified strength; wisdom undermines that confidence. This wisdom-conquers-war motif recurs in Eccl 9:13–16 (the poor wise man who saved the city).
