23.1: כִּי-תֵשֵׁב לִלְחוֹם אֶת-מוֹשֵׁל (when you sit down to eat with a ruler) — מוֹשֵׁל (môšēl, ruler, one who exercises authority) is the powerful host; בִּין תָּבִין (you will certainly understand/consider carefully) — the infinitive absolute with the imperfect for emphasis: understanding here is a command, not a suggestion. אֵת אֲשֶׁר-לְפָנֶיךָ (what is before you) — pānîm (face, before) is what confronts you directly; the advice is total situational awareness, not mere table manners. This opens the Thirty Sayings collection's first instruction on court behavior.
23.2: וְשַׂמְתָּ שַׂכִּין בְּלֹעֶךָ (put a knife to your throat) — שַׂכִּין (śakkîn, knife) appears only here in the Hebrew Bible; the word may be a borrowing. The violence of the image is deliberately hyperbolic: self-control in the ruler's presence requires something approaching self-force. אִם-בַּעַל נֶפֶשׁ אָתָּה (if you are a person of appetite/nephesh) — bʿal nephesh (master/owner of appetite) is the person driven by desire; here nephesh = appetite, the inner craving that must be disciplined at a powerful man's table where gratitude and power politics complicate ordinary hunger.
23.3: אַל-תִּתְאָו לְמַטְעַמֹּתָיו (do not crave his delicacies) — תָּאָה (tāʾâ, to long for, desire) and מַטְעַמֹּת (maṭʿammôt, delicacies, choice foods, literally "tastings") recall Esau's fatal appetite (Gen 25:29–34). וְהוּא לֶחֶם כְּזָבִים (for it is deceptive bread) — כְּזָבִים (kəzābîm, lies, falsehoods) applied to food; the ruler's hospitality may contain unspoken obligations. The bread is a lure, not a gift.
23.4: אַל-תִּיגַע לְהַעֲשִׁיר (do not toil/wear yourself out to become rich) — יָגַע (yāgaʿ, to grow weary, to labor to exhaustion) frames the pursuit of wealth as a form of self-destruction through overwork. מִבִּינָתְךָ חֲדָל (cease from your understanding/wisdom) — the imperative ḥădal (desist, cease) is the call to stop; the phrase is compact: use your understanding (bînâ) to know when to quit the wealth-race. Wisdom here is self-regulating: it knows its own limits.
23.5: הֲתָעִיף עֵינֶיךָ בּוֹ וְאֵינֶנּוּ (you make your eyes fly at it, and it is gone) — עוּף (ʿûp̄, to fly) is used of the eyes darting after wealth; the irony is that wealth is the one that flies. כִּי-עָשֹׂה יַעֲשֶׂה לּוֹ כְנָפַיִם (for riches make wings for themselves) — the infinitive absolute ʿāśôh yaʿăśeh intensifies: money emphatically makes its own wings. כְּנֶשֶׁר יָעוּף הַשָּׁמָיִם (like an eagle it flies toward the sky) — the eagle image captures both swiftness and altitude: wealth disappears beyond recovery.
23.6: אַל-תִּלְחַם אֶת-לֶחֶם רַע עָיִן (do not eat the bread of an evil-eyed person) — רַע עַיִן (evil eye) is the Hebrew idiom for miserliness or stinginess (opposite of the generous "good eye," 22:9). Eating with a miser is a social trap; his hospitality is not generosity but performance. וְאַל-תִּתְאָו לְמַטְעַמֹּתָיו (do not desire his delicacies) — the repetition of maṭʿammôt (from v. 3) ties the instruction together: both the powerful ruler and the stingy man offer food that is not what it appears.
23.7: כִּי כְמוֹ-שָׁעַר בְּנַפְשׁוֹ כֵּן-הוּא (for as he calculates in his nephesh/mind, so he is) — שָׁעַר (šāʿar, to calculate, to estimate the price) is a commercial verb; the miser is always running a ledger in his inner being. nephesh here = the controlling inner self, the mind that determines behavior. אֱכֹל וּשְׁתֵה יֹאמַר לָךְ (eat and drink, he says to you) — the quoted speech contrasts with what he is actually thinking; his hospitality is verbal while his heart (lēb) is absent: וְלִבּוֹ בַּל-עִמָּךְ (his heart is not with you).
23.8: פִּתְּךָ-אָכַלְתָּ תְּקִיאֶנָּה (the bit you ate, you will vomit it up) — the physical imagery is deliberately crude: the body expels what cannot be truly received, food eaten under false pretenses that cannot be digested. וְשִׁחַתָּ דְבָרֶיךָ הַנְּעִימִים (and your pleasant words will be wasted/destroyed) — שָׁחַת (šāḥat, to destroy, ruin, corrupt) applied to speech; the pleasant conversation invested in the miser's table yields nothing. Both the food and the social capital evaporate.
23.9: בְּאָזְנֵי כְסִיל אַל-תְּדַבֵּר (in the ears of a fool do not speak) — the fool (כְּסִיל, kəsîl) is the one constitutionally closed to correction; the image of ears emphasizes that the problem is not acoustics but reception. כִּי-יָבוּז לְשֵׂכֶל מִלֶּיךָ (for he will despise the wisdom of your words) — בּוּז (bûz, to despise, hold in contempt) is the response of the mocker-fool to wisdom's content; wisdom is wasted on the one who despises it by constitution. The proverb counsels strategic silence: wisdom conserves itself.
23.10: אַל-תַּסֵּג גְּבוּל עוֹלָם (do not move the ancient boundary) — the repetition of 22:28's phrase in a new context is significant; here the orphan (יְתוֹמִים, yətômîm) is the victim. וּבִשְׂדֵי יְתוֹמִים אַל-תָּבֹא (and do not enter the fields of orphans) — בּוֹא (bôʾ, to enter, come into) here means illegal encroachment on property. The orphan, like the widow, is the paradigmatic vulnerable person in Israel's law (Exod 22:22; Deut 24:17), lacking legal advocates — which makes the following verse's assurance all the more powerful.
23.11: כִּי-גֹאֲלָם חָזָק הוּא (for their Redeemer is strong) — גֹּאֵל (gōʾēl, redeemer, kinsman-redeemer) is the legal-familial institution of Israel: the nearest male relative who steps in to restore what a family member has lost (land, freedom, life; cf. Lev 25:25–55; Ruth 2:20). Applied to YHWH, the institution is elevated into divine advocacy: God himself is the orphan's kinsman-redeemer. חָזָק הוּא (he is strong) — against the exploiter who counts on the orphan's legal helplessness, YHWH's strength reverses the power calculus. יָרִיב אֶת-רִיבָם אִתָּךְ (he will plead their case against you) — the courtroom language (rîb) of ch. 22 recurs.
23.12: הָבִיאָה לַמּוּסָר לִבֶּךָ (bring your heart to instruction) — the imperative הָבִיאָה (hābîʾâ, bring!) calls for an act of will: the student must direct the lēb toward מוּסָר (mûsār, discipline, instruction). This is not passive absorption but active redirection of the inner life. וְאָזְנֶיךָ לְאִמְרֵי-דָעַת (and your ears to words of knowledge) — the parallel pairs heart and ear: wisdom's formation works through both the volitional center and the auditory gateway, requiring the whole person.
23.13: אַל-תִּמְנַע מִנַּעַר מוּסָר (do not withhold discipline from a child) — מָנַע (mānaʿ, to withhold, restrain) implies that withholding correction is an active choice, not mere neglect; the parent must decide to apply the rod. כִּי-תַכֶּנּוּ בַשֵּׁבֶט לֹא יָמוּת (if you strike him with the rod he will not die) — the hyperbole counters overprotective hesitation; the feared outcome (death) is precisely what the rod prevents, not causes. The proverb addresses parental anxiety about harm through correction.
23.14: אַתָּה בַּשֵּׁבֶט תַּכֶּנּוּ (you will strike him with the rod) — the repetition of rod-discipline from v. 13 is emphatic; vv. 13–14 form a paired instruction reinforcing each other. וְנַפְשׁוֹ מִשְּׁאוֹל תַּצִּיל (and his nephesh/life you will rescue from Sheol) — שְׁאוֹל (šəʾôl, the realm of the dead) frames the consequence without correction as death itself. nephesh here = life in the mortal sense; discipline is life-saving, not life-threatening. The rod is presented as an instrument of rescue, not punishment for its own sake.
23.15: בְּנִי אִם-חָכַם לִבֶּךָ (my son, if your heart is wise) — the father-son address (בְּנִי, bənî, my son) recurs here after the impersonal second-person instructions of the court-wisdom section; the tone becomes warmly personal. יִשְׂמַח לִבִּי גַם-אָנִי (my heart will rejoice, even I) — the emphatic gam-ʾānî (even I myself) expresses parental delight; wisdom in the son produces joy in the father's inner life. The proverb creates a moral community of gladness.
23.16: וְתַעְלֹזְנָה כִלְיוֹתַי (yes, my kidneys/inmost being will rejoice) — כִּלְיָה (kilyâ, kidney) is used in Hebrew as the seat of the deepest emotions and moral sensitivity (cf. Ps 7:10; 16:7; 26:2); parallel to lēb (heart) as the center of inner life. בְּדַבֵּר שְׂפָתֶיךָ מֵישָׁרִים (when your lips speak what is right/straight things) — מֵישָׁרִים (mêšārîm, uprightness, right things) — plural noun of quality; the father's inner joy is specifically triggered by the child's righteous speech, the outward expression of inward wisdom.
23.17: אַל-יְקַנֵּא לִבְּךָ בַּחַטָּאִים (do not let your heart envy sinners) — קָנָא (qānāʾ, to be jealous, to envy) in the Piel; the heart is warned against the covert attraction to the apparent success of the wicked (cf. Ps 37:1; 73:2–3). כִּי אִם-בְּיִרְאַת יהוה כָּל-הַיּוֹם (but live in the fear of the LORD all the day) — kî ʾim introduces the positive alternative; continuous fear-of-the-LORD (kol-hayyôm, all the day) is the counterweight to the momentary attraction of sinners' prosperity.
23.18: כִּי אִם-יֵשׁ אַחֲרִית (for surely there is a future/end) — אַחֲרִית (ʾaḥărît, latter end, outcome, future) is the wisdom term for the eschatological horizon that gives present choices their weight (cf. 23:14; 24:14, 20). The fear of YHWH has a future; envy of sinners has no future that matters. וְתִקְוָתְךָ לֹא תִכָּרֵת (and your hope will not be cut off) — תִּקְוָה (tiqwâ, hope, expectation, a cord — same root as Rahab's scarlet thread in Josh 2:18) will not be כָּרַת (kārat, cut off, severed). The hope is structural, not merely emotional.
23.19: שְׁמַע-אַתָּה בְנִי וַחֲכָם (listen, you — my son — and be wise) — the direct pronoun אַתָּה (ʾattā) is emphatic: this instruction is personally directed, not general. The father calls the son to two acts simultaneously: שְׁמַע (hear, attend) and חֲכַם (be wise, become wise) — hearing is not enough without wisdom's transformation. וְאַשֵּׁר בַּדֶּרֶךְ לִבֶּךָ (and keep/direct your heart on the path) — אָשַׁר (ʾāšar, to go straight, to lead straight) in the Piel; the heart must be actively directed rather than allowed to drift.
23.20: אַל-תְּהִי בְסֹבְאֵי-יַיִן (do not be among those who drink wine to excess) — סָבָא (sābāʾ, to drink heavily, to be a winebibber) — the participial form marks a habitual lifestyle, not an occasional indulgence. בְּזֹלְלֵי בָשָׂר לָמוֹ (among those who gorge themselves on meat) — זָלַל (zālal, to be light, worthless, to squander, to be a glutton) combined with bāśār (flesh, meat) describes the excessive consumption associated with feasting. The warning pairs wine-excess with meat-excess as twin paths to self-destruction (cf. Deut 21:20 for the "stubborn and rebellious son" charge).
23.21: כִּי-סֹבֵא וְזֹלֵל יִוָּרֵשׁ (for the drunkard and the glutton will come to poverty) — יִוָּרֵשׁ (yiwwārēš, Niphal of yārash, to be dispossessed, brought to poverty) — the passive of dispossession; the glutton is robbed of his own substance by his appetites. וּקְרָעִים תַּלְבִּישׁ נוּמָה (and drowsiness will clothe them in rags) — קְרָעִים (qərāʿîm, torn garments, rags) as the clothing of poverty; נוּמָה (nûmâ, drowsiness, slumber) personified as the one who clothes the idle in poverty's uniform. Sleep and drink conspire against the person.
23.22: שְׁמַע לְאָבִיךָ זֶה יְלָדֶךָ (listen to your father, this one who gave you life) — יָלַד (yālad, to beget, give birth) emphasizes the father as life-giver; the relative clause zeh yəlādekā (this one who begot you) grounds the command in creaturely origin. וְאַל-תָּבוּז לְאִמֶּךָ כִּי זָקֵנָה (and do not despise your mother when she grows old) — בּוּז (bûz, to despise, treat with contempt) is what one might do to the no-longer-powerful elder; the proverb names this tendency and forbids it. Age does not diminish the honor due to parents.
23.23: אֱמֶת קְנֵה וְאַל-תִּמְכֹּר (buy truth and do not sell it) — קָנָה (qānâ, to buy, acquire) — the commercial metaphor treats truth as a precious commodity worth market-price acquisition. אֶמֶת (ʾĕmet) has its root in the stability of something reliable, trustworthy, and enduring — not merely factual accuracy but ontological solidity. חׇכְמָה מוּסָר וּבִינָה (wisdom, instruction/discipline, and understanding) — the three cognitive-formational gifts of wisdom literature are listed as what one buys along with truth; they are not separate items but facets of truth's purchase price and yield.
23.24: גּוֹל יָגִיל אֲבִי צַדִּיק (greatly rejoice will the father of a righteous one) — גּוּל (gûl, to rejoice, exult) with the infinitive absolute gôl for emphasis; the father's joy is emphatic and complete. וְיוֹלֵד חָכָם וְיִשְׂמַח-בוֹ (the one who begets a wise son will rejoice in him) — wisdom in a child produces joy; the parent-child bond is the social site of wisdom's transmission and confirmation. The verse is the positive counterpart to the shame that a foolish son brings (10:1; 17:21).
23.25: יִשְׂמַח-אָבִיךָ וְאִמֶּךָ (let your father and mother be glad) — the imperative is addressed to the son: make them glad through your wisdom. וְתָגֵל יוֹלַדְתֶּךָ (let her who bore you rejoice) — יָלַד returns; yôladtekā (the one who bore you) is an intimate designation for the mother. The father's joy is in the son's righteous character (v. 24); the mother's joy is expressed here through the birthing relationship — you came from her, return her investment with wisdom.
23.26: תְּנָה-לִי לִבְּךָ בְּנִי (give me your heart, my son) — נָתַן (nātan, to give) applied to the lēb (heart): the father asks for the son's volitional center, not mere compliance. This is the deepest request of wisdom instruction — not behavioral conformity but inner orientation toward the teacher's wisdom. וְעֵינֶיךָ דְּרָכַי תִּרְצֶינָה (and let your eyes keep watch over my ways) — רָצָה (rāṣâ, to be pleased with, delight in, approve) here means more than passive observation; the son is to find delight in the teacher's path, aligning vision with value.
23.27: כִּי-שׁוּחָה עֲמֻקָּה זוֹנָה (for a prostitute is a deep pit) — שׁוּחָה (šûḥâ, pit, deep trench) recalls 22:14's identical image for the adulterous woman's mouth; here the whole person of the זוֹנָה (zônâ, prostitute) is the pit. וּבְאֵר צָרָה נָכְרִיָּה (and the foreign/strange woman is a narrow well) — nākriyyâ (foreign, strange woman) is the moral outsider; בְּאֵר צָרָה (a narrow/constricted well) suggests a place that traps those who descend. Both images are spatial — downward movement, constriction, death.
23.28: אַף-הִיא כְּחֶתֶף תֶּאֱרֹב (indeed she lies in wait like a hunter/robber) — חֶתֶף (ḥetep̄, one who snatches, a robber) suggests predatory ambush; אָרַב (ʾārab, to lie in wait, to ambush) is the vocabulary of military ambush applied to seduction. וּבוֹגְדִים בְּאָדָם תּוֹסִף (and she increases the faithless/treacherous among men) — בָּגַד (bāgad, to deal treacherously, to be faithless) is covenant-betrayal language; she multiplies those who betray their covenantal commitments, turning men into oath-breakers.
23.29: לְמִי אוֹי לְמִי אֲבוֹי (who has woe, who has sorrow) — the catalogue of lemi (who has) questions is a rhetorical device listing the symptoms of wine's destruction: אוֹי (woe), אֲבוֹי (alas, lamentation), מִדְיָנִים (strife/quarreling), שִׂיחַ (complaining, anguish), פְּצָעִים חִנָּם (wounds without cause), חַכְלִילוּת עֵינַיִם (bleary/redness of eyes). The list is accumulative and clinical — a medical-social inventory of alcohol's consequences before the answer is given in v. 30.
23.30: לַמְאַחֲרִים עַל-הַיַּיִן (those who linger over wine) — אָחַר (ʾāḥar, to delay, tarry, be late) applied to sitting with wine: the damage is not in a single cup but in extended lingering. לַבָּאִים לַחְקֹר מִמְסָךְ (those who come to investigate/seek out mixed wine) — מִמְסָךְ (mimsāk, spiced/mixed wine) is wine blended with spices for stronger effect. The one who searches out the strongest mixture is the answer to v. 29's catalogue: the wine-obsessed is the source of all those symptoms.
23.31: אַל-תֵּרֶא יַיִן כִּי יִתְאַדָּם (do not look at wine when it turns red) — אָדַם (ʾādam, to be red) — wine's redness is its visual seductiveness; the prohibition begins with the gaze. כִּי-יִתֵּן בַּכּוֹס עֵינוֹ (when it gives its eye in the cup) — the wine is personified: it sparkles (ʿênô, its eye) in the cup, looking back at the drinker. יִתְהַלֵּךְ בְּמֵישָׁרִים (it goes down smoothly) — mêšārîm (uprightness, straight things) used ironically: what goes down straight destroys the person who swallows it straight.
23.32: אַחֲרִיתוֹ כְּנָחָשׁ יִשָּׁךְ (in the end it bites like a serpent) — אַחֲרִית (ʾaḥărît, the latter end, the outcome) — the wisdom term for eschatological consequence; wine's end is serpent-like. וּכְצִפְעֹנִי יַפְרִשׁ (and like a viper it spreads/strikes) — צִפְעֹנִי (ṣipʿônî, viper, adder) is a particularly dangerous snake; פָּרַשׁ (pāraš, to spread, to sting) suggests the poison dispersing through the body. The wine that looked like pleasure delivers venom as its true nature.
23.33: עֵינֶיךָ יִרְאוּ זָרוֹת (your eyes will see strange/foreign things) — זָרוֹת (zārôt, strange, foreign things) — hallucinations; the same word as the "strange woman" of the sexual danger passages; wine produces the same distorted perception. וְלִבְּךָ יְדַבֵּר תַּהְפֻּכוֹת (and your heart will speak perverse things) — תַּהְפֻּכוֹת (tahpukôt, perverse, twisted things; from hāp̄ak, to overturn) are what the intoxicated heart produces: speech that inverts right and wrong, wisdom and folly.
23.34: וְהָיִיתָ כְּשֹׁכֵב בְּלֶב-יָם (you will be like one lying down in the heart of the sea) — לֵב-יָם (heart of the sea) — the most dangerous place, exposed to all swells, with no stable ground; the drunk person has lost all orientation. וּכְשֹׁכֵב בְּרֹאשׁ חִבֵּל (like one lying on the top of a mast) — חִבֵּל (ḥibbēl, mast, rope?) — lying atop the ship's highest point in a storm is suicidal carelessness. Both images convey the drunk person's total loss of self-preservation instinct.
23.35: הִכּוּנִי בַל-חָלִיתִי ("they beat me but I felt no pain") — the drunkard speaks in first person for the first time; the quoted speech is darkly comic. The perfect verbs describe past beating (הִכּוּנִי) and insensibility (חָלִיתִי from ḥālâ, to be sick, to feel pain). מָתַי אָקִיץ אֲבַקְשֶׁנּוּ עוֹד ("when will I wake up so I can seek another drink?") — the final word is the tragic punchline: the cycle of intoxication, damage, and renewed craving is complete. Wisdom's portrait of the drunkard ends with his own voice asking for more poison.

24.1: אַל-תְּקַנֵּא בְּאַנְשֵׁי הָרָעָה (do not envy the men of evil) — קָנָא (qānāʾ, to be jealous/envious) is the affective disposition that must be actively resisted; the evil man's visible success generates a pull on the viewer. וְאַל-תִּתְאָו לִהְיוֹת אִתָּם (and do not desire to be with them) — the second prohibition targets not just envy of their goods but desire for their company; association is itself the danger. The proverb opens chapter 24's first section with the foundational warning against the attraction of wickedness.
24.2: כִּי-שֹׁד יֶהְגֶּה לִבָּם (for their heart meditates/murmurs destruction) — שֹׁד (šôd, ruin, devastation, violence) is what the wicked person's lēb (heart) continuously rehearses. הָגָה (hāgâ, to murmur, meditate, utter) is the same verb used of the righteous meditating on Torah (Ps 1:2); here it is applied to the wicked's constant rumination on harm. וְעָמָל שְׂפָתֵיהֶם תְּדַבֵּרְנָה (and their lips speak trouble/toil) — ʿāmāl (toil, mischief, trouble) is what their meditation produces in speech.
24.3: בְּחׇכְמָה יִבָּנֶה בָּיִת (by wisdom a house is built) — בָּנָה (bānâ, to build) in Niphal; the house (bayit) is both the physical dwelling and the family dynasty. Wisdom is the structural principle of lasting household establishment. וּבִתְבוּנָה יִתְכּוֹנָן (and by understanding it is established) — תְּבוּנָה (təbûnâ, understanding, discernment) from bîn; כּוּן (kôn, to be established, fixed) in the Hithpael — the house establishes itself through understanding, the internalized application of wisdom to lived decisions.
24.4: וּבְדַעַת חֲדָרִים יִמָּלְאוּ (and by knowledge the rooms are filled) — the house-building metaphor extends: after construction (v. 3) comes furnishing; חֲדָרִים (ḥădārîm, inner rooms, chambers) fill with כָּל-הוֹן יָקָר וְנָעִים (all precious and pleasant wealth). The triad of wisdom-understanding-knowledge (ḥokmâ-təbûnâ-daʿat) builds, establishes, and furnishes the house. The progression mirrors creation: God used wisdom to found the earth (3:19–20).
24.5: גֶּבֶר-חָכָם בַּעוֹז (a wise man is strong/in strength) — the connection between wisdom and power runs against the intuition that physical strength is primary. גֶּבֶר (geber, a strong man, a warrior) qualified by ḥākām (wise) — wisdom is the warrior's true resource. וְאִישׁ-דַּעַת מְאַמֶּץ-כֹּחַ (and a man of knowledge grows in might) — אָמַץ (ʾāmaṣ, to be strong, to confirm, to grow mighty) in the Piel; knowledge is a power-amplifier, increasing the person's effective capacity beyond raw force.
24.6: כִּי-בְתַחְבֻּלוֹת תַּעֲשֶׂה-לְּךָ מִלְחָמָה (for by wise guidance you make war for yourself) — תַּחְבֻּלוֹת (taḥbulôt, guidance, steersmanship — from ḥeβel, a rope/cord used in sailing) is the wisdom of strategic direction; the image is nautical before it is military. וּתְשׁוּעָה בְּרֹב יוֹעֵץ (and victory comes with many advisers) — this principle from 11:14 (with identical wording) is repeated; wisdom is distributed in community, not concentrated in one mind. The military context makes the political application concrete.
24.7: רָאמוֹת לָאֱוִיל חׇכְמוֹת (wisdom is heights/coral to the fool) — רָאמוֹת (rāʾmôt) is debated: "heights" (inaccessible elevations) or "coral" (precious deep-sea material, equally inaccessible). Either way: wisdom is beyond the fool's reach — spatially unattainable. בַּשַּׁעַר לֹא יִפְתַּח-פִּיהוּ (at the gate he will not open his mouth) — the civic gate is the court of public wisdom and justice; the fool's silence there is the practical consequence of wisdom's inaccessibility to him. He has nothing to contribute to the community's deliberation.
24.8: מְחַשֵּׁב לְהָרַע (whoever plans to do evil) — חָשַׁב (ḥāšab, to think, plan, reckon, devise) in the Piel; intentional, sustained scheming. בַּעַל מְזִמּוֹת יִקְרְאוּ-לוֹ (he will be called a master of schemes) — bʿal məzimmôt (master/owner of schemes); מְזִמָּה (məzimmâ) can be positive (prudent planning) or negative (evil schemes) depending on context; here unambiguously negative. The person becomes identified with his scheming — reputation as a troublemaker attaches to him publicly.
24.9: זִמַּת אִוֶּלֶת חַטָּאת (the scheme/purpose of folly is sin) — זִמָּה (zimmâ, scheme, plan, evil device) applied to ʾiwwelet (folly); folly is not merely stupid but sinful in its intention. וְתוֹעֲבַת לְאָדָם לֵץ (and the mocker is an abomination to people) — תּוֹעֵבָה (tôʿēbâ, abomination) applied to the lēṣ (scoffer/mocker): what is usually a cultic category (detestable to God) is here applied to the community's social judgment. The mocker evokes the same moral revulsion in humans that abomination evokes in God.
24.10: הִתְרַפִּיתָ בְּיוֹם צָרָה (if you falter in the day of trouble) — רָפָה (rāpâ, to sink, relax, go limp) in the Hithpael — letting oneself go slack; the day of צָרָה (ṣārâ, trouble, distress, tightness) is precisely when strength is tested. צַר כֹּחֶךָ (your strength is small) — ṣar (narrow, small, cramped) — strength that cannot hold under pressure was always small; adversity reveals what was there all along. The proverb challenges the person to know their real capacity before crisis arrives.
24.11: הַצֵּל לְקוּחִים לַמָּוֶת (rescue those being taken to death) — לָקַח (lāqaḥ, to take, seize) in the Qal passive participle — those already seized and being led; the urgency is immediate. וּמוֹטִים לַהֶרֶג אִם-תַּחְשׂוֹךְ (and those stumbling toward slaughter, if you hold back) — חָשַׂךְ (ḥāśak, to restrain, withhold) is the sin of inaction when intervention could save life. The proverb mandates active rescue as a moral obligation, not optional generosity.
24.12: כִּי-תֹאמַר הֵן לֹא-יָדַעְנוּ זֶה (if you say, "We did not know this") — the quoted speech is the self-exculpating claim of willful ignorance; the verse dismantles it by invoking divine omniscience. הֲלֹא-תֹכֵן לִבּוֹת יָבִין (does not he who weighs hearts understand?) — תָּכַן (tākan, to weigh, to measure precisely) applied to the lēb (hearts); God is the precisely calibrated scales of inner intention. וְנֹצֵר נַפְשְׁךָ הוּא יֵדָע (he who guards your nephesh/life — he knows) — nephesh here = life; the one who preserves your existence also observes your moral choices. וְהֵשִׁיב לְאָדָם כְּפָעֳלוֹ (and he will repay each person according to their deed) — the lex talionis principle at divine scale: kəpāʿŏlô (according to their work/deed).
24.13: אֱכָל-בְּנִי דְבַשׁ כִּי-טוֹב (eat honey, my son, because it is good) — the father-son address returns; the instruction becomes inviting rather than prohibitive. דְּבַשׁ (dəbaš, honey) is the paradigmatic good taste (Ps 19:11; 119:103); וְנֹפֶת מָתוֹק עַל-חִכֶּךָ (and the honeycomb is sweet to your palate) — nōp̄et (honeycomb, dripping honey) intensifies; ḥikkekā (your palate/taste) is the faculty of aesthetic discrimination. The honey sets up the analogy of v. 14.
24.14: כֵּן דְּעֵה חׇכְמָה לְנַפְשֶׁךָ (so know wisdom for your soul) — the comparative kēn (so, thus) draws the analogy: as honey is good to the taste, so wisdom is good to the nephesh. nephesh here = the inner person, the self that is nourished by wisdom as the body is nourished by honey. אִם-מָצָאתָ וְיֵשׁ אַחֲרִית (if you find it, there is a future) — אַחֲרִית (ʾaḥărît) recurs: the future/outcome that wisdom guarantees. וְתִקְוָתְךָ לֹא תִכָּרֵת (your hope will not be cut off) — identical to 23:18; wisdom's promise is structural: a future that cannot be severed.
24.15: אַל-תֶּאֱרֹב רָשָׁע לִנְוֵה צַדִּיק (do not lie in wait, O wicked one, against the dwelling of the righteous) — the verse addresses the wicked man directly — a rhetorical shift to second person that implicates anyone tempted to plot against the righteous. אַל-תְּשַׁדֵּד רִבְצוֹ (do not raid his resting place) — שָׁדַד (šādad, to ravage, spoil, destroy) is strong violence; ribṣô (his resting place) is domestic peace. The instruction implies that the righteous man's home is under his Redeemer's protection (cf. 23:11).
24.16: כִּי שֶׁבַע יִפּוֹל צַדִּיק וָקָם (for the righteous falls seven times and rises again) — שֶׁבַע (šebaʿ, seven) is the number of completeness: however many times the righteous person falls, he rises. נָפַל (fall) and קוּם (rise) are the two poles of the righteous life — not immunity from adversity but resurrection from it. וּרְשָׁעִים יִכָּשְׁלוּ בְרָעָה (but the wicked stumble in calamity) — contrast: the wicked fall when calamity comes and do not rise. The difference is not circumstance but the capacity for recovery that righteousness grants.
24.17: בִּנְפֹל אוֹיִבְךָ אַל-תִּשְׂמָח (when your enemy falls do not rejoice) — שָׂמַח (śāmaḥ, to rejoice) is forbidden at the enemy's fall; the impulse is natural but the proverb confronts it directly. וּבִכָּשְׁלוֹ אַל-יָגֵל לִבֶּךָ (and when he stumbles do not let your heart exult) — lēb (heart) is the seat of the attitude; even private celebration is wrong. The prohibition reaches inward, not just to public behavior.
24.18: פֶּן-יִרְאֶה יהוה וְרַע בְּעֵינָיו (lest the LORD see and it be evil in his eyes) — the LORD's gaze is the controlling concern: gloating at the enemy's fall is רַע בְּעֵינָיו (evil in his eyes), the standard of YHWH's moral evaluation. וְהֵשִׁיב מֵעָלָיו אַפּוֹ (and he turns his anger away from your enemy) — the ironic consequence: if you gloat, God redirects his anger from the enemy to you. The enemy's calamity is God's action; appropriating it as your satisfaction perverts its purpose.
24.19: אַל-תִּתְחַר בַּמְּרֵעִים (do not fret/be agitated because of evildoers) — חָרָה (ḥārâ, to burn, to be angry, to fret) in the Hithpael; self-generating agitation at the wicked, which Ps 37:1 opens identically. אַל-תְּקַנֵּא בָּרְשָׁעִים (do not envy the wicked) — qānāʾ (envy, jealousy) targets their apparent prosperity. Both fretting and envying are responses to perceiving the wicked as having something worth having; the proverb cuts the perception at the root.
24.20: כִּי לֹא-תִהְיֶה אַחֲרִית לָרָע (for the evil person has no future/outcome) — אַחֲרִית (ʾaḥărît) recurs for the third time in ch. 24 (vv. 14, 18, 20); the chapter organizes itself around the concept of future outcome. The wicked have no lasting future because their prosperity is structurally temporary. נֵר רְשָׁעִים יִדְעָךְ (the lamp of the wicked will be snuffed out) — the lamp (nēr) is a common image for life and household continuation (cf. 13:9; 20:20); its extinction is the end of legacy as well as life.
24.21: יְרָא-אֶת-יהוה בְּנִי וָמֶלֶךְ (fear the LORD, my son, and the king) — the father-son address returns for the final instruction of the "Words of the Wise" collection. The dual command pairs YHWH and king; both represent legitimate authorities that wisdom honors. עִם-שׁוֹנִים אַל-תִּתְעָרָב (do not associate with those who seek change/are different) — שׁוֹנִים (šônîm) — those who are different, who rebel against established order; עָרַב (ʿārab, to mix, to become surety, to associate) in the Hithpael warns against social mixing with the dangerously unstable.
24.22: כִּי-פִתְאֹם יָקוּם אֵידָם (for suddenly their disaster will rise) — פִּתְאֹם (pitʾôm, suddenly, unexpectedly) marks the characteristic swiftness of divine judgment on the rebellious; no warning, no gradual approach. וּפִיד שְׁנֵיהֶם מִי יוֹדֵעַ (and the ruin/calamity of both — who knows?) — פִּיד (pîd, ruin, calamity, destruction) is a rare word (cf. Job 12:5; 30:12, 24; 31:29); its scope extends to both king and rebel: both can bring sudden ruin. The uncertainty (mî yôdēaʿ, who knows?) underscores the unpredictability of those who hold power without wisdom.
24.23: גַּם-אֵלֶּה לַחֲכָמִים (these also are for the wise) — the superscription marks the opening of a second sub-collection (24:23–34), distinct from the "Thirty Sayings" of 22:17–24:22. הַכֵּר-פָּנִים בְּמִשְׁפָּט בַּל-טוֹב (to show partiality in judgment is not good) — הַכֵּר פָּנִים (to recognize the face, to show partiality) is the idiom for favoring based on appearance or social status rather than justice. Judicial impartiality is foundational to the wisdom tradition's ethics (Lev 19:15; Deut 16:19).
24.24: אֹמֵר לְרָשָׁע צַדִּיק אָתָּה (whoever says to the wicked, "You are righteous") — the false verdict in court; calling the guilty innocent inverts the entire judicial order. יִקְּבֻהוּ עַמִּים (peoples will curse him) — קָבַב (qābab, to curse, utter a curse against) — the universal social consequence: not just one wronged party but ʿammîm (peoples) condemn the corrupt judge. יִזְעָמוּהוּ לְאֻמִּים (nations will denounce him) — lēʾummîm (peoples, nations) parallel ʿammîm; the scope is universal: unjust acquittal of the wicked is an offense against humanity, not merely one case.
24.25: וְלַמּוֹכִיחִים יִנְעָם (but those who rebuke/reprove will find delight) — יָכַח (yākaḥ, to rebuke, reprove, argue a case, adjudicate) in the Hiphil participle — those who exercise the prophetic-judicial function of honest reproof. עֲלֵיהֶם תָּבוֹא בִרְכַּת-טוֹב (upon them will come a blessing of good) — the passive of divine blessing (tābôʾ, will come) settles on those who speak truth against the wicked. The good-blessing (birkat-ṭôb) is the counterpart to the peoples' curse on the corrupt judge.
24.26: שְׂפָתַיִם יִשָּׁק (he will kiss the lips) — the metaphor is one of the most striking in Proverbs: an honest answer is like a kiss. מֵשִׁיב דְּבָרִים נְכֹחִים (one who gives back right/direct words) — נָכוֹחַ (nākôaḥ, right, straight, before one) describes speech that goes directly where it should without deflection. The kiss metaphor suggests warmth, intimacy, and trust; honest speech creates and sustains the bond that sycophancy destroys.
24.27: הָכֵן בַּחוּץ מְלַאכְתֶּךָ (prepare your outdoor work) — כּוּן (kôn, to establish, prepare, make ready) in the Hiphil imperative; outdoor work (ḥûṣ, outside, field) comes first. וְאַחַר בָּנִיתָ בֵיתֶךָ (and afterward build your house) — the sequence is economic prudence: establish productive capacity before consuming it in construction. The proverb echoes Jesus's parable of the builder counting costs (Luke 14:28–30); wisdom calculates before committing.
24.28: אַל-תִּהְיֶה עֵד-חִנָּם בְּרֵעֶךָ (do not be a witness without cause against your neighbor) — חִנָּם (ḥinnām, for nothing, without cause, groundlessly) modifies the witness role; groundless testimony is the legal wrong named. וַהֲפִתִּיתָ בִּשְׂפָתֶיךָ (and do not deceive with your lips) — פָּתָה (pātâ, to open, to deceive, to entice) — verbal deception in legal context; the prohibition extends from false testimony to any form of speech designed to mislead in matters affecting another's legal standing.
24.29: אַל-תֹּאמַר אֶעֱשֶׂה-לוֹ כַּאֲשֶׁר עָשָׂה-לִי (do not say, "I will do to him as he did to me") — the quoted speech is the voice of human retributive instinct; the proverb forbids it by forbidding even its formulation in speech. אָשִׁיב לָאִישׁ כְּפָעֳלוֹ (I will repay the man according to his deed) — the self-assumed role of judge and avenger; kəpāʿŏlô (according to his deed) is exactly YHWH's prerogative (24:12). Taking it for oneself usurps the divine role. The New Testament's prohibition on revenge (Rom 12:17–19) has this exact proverb as background.
24.30: עַל שְׂדֵה אִישׁ-עָצֵל עָבַרְתִּי (I passed by the field of a lazy man) — the poem shifts to autobiographical first-person narrative, the sage as observer. וְעַל-כֶּרֶם אָדָם חֲסַר-לֵב (and by the vineyard of a man who lacked heart/sense) — חֲסַר-לֵב (ḥăsar-lēb, lacking heart, senseless) — the lēb (heart/mind) is the organ of wisdom; to lack it is to be constitutionally incapable of prudent planning. The field and vineyard are read as diagnostic landscapes.
24.31: וְהִנֵּה עָלָה כֻלּוֹ קִמְּשֹׂנִים (and behold, it was all overgrown with thorns) — the wəhinnēh (and behold) signals the vivid, surprising scene that confronts the observer. קִמָּשׂוֹן (qimmāśôn, weeds, nettles, thorns) — a term for invasive vegetation that colonizes untended ground. כִּסּוּ פָנָיו חֲרֻלִּים (nettles covered its face) — pānāyw (its face) is the surface of the field; ḥarullîm (nettles, weeds) have taken the face. וְגֶדֶר אֲבָנָיו נֶהֱרָסָה (and its stone wall was broken down) — the infrastructure of order (wall = property boundary + protection) has collapsed along with the crop.
24.32: וָאֶחֱזֶה אָנֹכִי אָשִׁית לִבִּי (I saw it, I took it to heart) — the observer's response: אָשִׁית לִבִּי (I set my heart) — active attention, not passive seeing. רָאִיתִי לָקַחְתִּי מוּסָר (I looked and received instruction) — לָקַח מוּסָר (to receive/take instruction) is the phrase for wisdom's appropriation (cf. 1:3; 8:10); the sage reads the landscape as a moral text. The neglected field is the medium of instruction; observation becomes formation.
24.33: מְעַט שֵׁנוֹת מְעַט תְּנוּמוֹת (a little sleep, a little slumber) — the triple diminutive (מְעַט, məʿaṭ, a little) is ironic; each "little" compounds. מְעַט חִבֻּק יָדַיִם לִשְׁכָּב (a little folding of hands to rest) — חָבַק יָדַיִם (to fold the hands) is the posture of inaction; the hands that should work are crossed in comfortable rest. Identical to 6:10, where the same picture opens the warning about the sluggard's self-destruction. The repetition of a known text signals that the wise man has absorbed a prior instruction and sees it confirmed in the field before him.
24.34: וּבָא-מִתְהַלֵּךְ רֵישֶׁךָ (then poverty will come on you like a wanderer) — מִתְהַלֵּךְ (mitəhallēk, Hithpael participle of hālak, to walk) — poverty that walks toward you like a drifter, arriving inevitably. וּמַחְסֹרְךָ כְּאִישׁ מָגֵן (and your need like an armed man) — māgēn is often "shield" but here the armed defender figure suggests poverty arriving with military force, not to be denied entry. Identical to 6:11; the poem ends with the same warning with which it began in chapter 6. The sage has observed it in the world and confirms what wisdom had already taught.

28.1: Nāsû rĕšāʿîm wĕ'ên rōdēp = “The wicked flee when no one pursues” — the guilty conscience creates its own pursuers. Wĕṣaddîqîm kikpîr yibṭāḥ = “but the righteous are as bold as a young lion.” Kĕpîr = a young, full-grown lion at peak strength — the image is not of aggression but of confidence: the righteous person does not bolt because there is nothing to flee from. Moral innocence and YHWH-trust together produce this unshakable bearing.
28.2: Bĕpeša' ʾereṣ rabbîm śārêhā = “In the transgression of a land many are its rulers” — national sin fragments political authority; too many competing rulers is itself a symptom of moral disorder. Wĕbĕ'ādām mêbîn yōdēaʿ — disputed: yādaʿ (H3045, dispute_level=2) with the range “know / understand / perceive / experience.” The MKT renders “through a man of understanding and knowledge its stability endures” — one person of discernment can stabilize what collective sin has shattered.
28.3: Geber-rāš wĕ'ōšēq dallîm = “A poor man who oppresses the poor” — the inversion is the point: the one with no resources to oppress still does so. Māṭār sōḥēp wĕ'ên lāḥem = “like a driving/sweeping rain that leaves no food” — the heavy rains that strip the soil and wash away crops rather than watering them. The rain is violent, not nourishing: destructive without remedy.
28.4: ʿŌzĕbê tôrāh yĕhallĕlû rāšāʿ = “Those who forsake the law (tôrāh) praise the wicked” — tôrāh is instruction/law, the foundational term for divine teaching. Those who abandon it lose the moral framework that enables them to identify wickedness as wicked; they end up praising it instead. Wĕšōmĕrê tôrāh yitgārû bām = “but those who keep the law contend with them” — law-keepers resist the wicked by definition.
28.5: ʾAnšê-rāʿ lō' yābînû mišpāṭ = “Evil men do not understand justice” — moral failing produces epistemic incapacity; the wicked cannot perceive right order because wickedness deforms the perceiving faculty. Wĕmĕbaqqĕšê YHWH yābînû kōl = “but those who seek YHWH understand everything.” YHWH (H3068, dispute_level=4) — the personal divine name; “LORD” in standard translations conceals this. Seeking YHWH is the comprehensive epistemological orientation that opens the full range of understanding.
28.6: 'Better is the poor man who walks in his integrity (bĕtummô) than the one who is perverse in his ways (wĕ'iqqēš dĕrākayim), even if he is rich' — tōm (integrity, wholeness) is the inner coherence of the person whose conduct matches their values. ʾIqqēš (crooked/twisted) is the antonym: a person whose paths are doubled/divided — saying one thing, doing another. The verse weighs character against wealth and finds wealth lacking.
28.7: Nôṣēr tôrāh bēn mêbîn = “Whoever keeps the law is a wise son” — nāṣar (keep/guard/watch) — the watchful guarding of the tôrāh. Wĕrōʿēh zôlĕlîm yaklîm ʾābîw = “but a companion of gluttons (zôlĕlîm) brings shame on his father” — zālal (be loose/worthless/prodigal) encompasses both excess eating and moral looseness; the gluttonous companion is the fool-company warning of 13:20.
28.8: Marbeh hônô bĕneśek ûbĕtarbît = “Whoever increases wealth by interest (neśek) and dishonest gain” — neśek (bite, interest on a loan) and tarbît (increase/usury) are the twin commercial vices forbidden in the Torah (Lev 25:36-37). Lĕḥônen dallîm yiqbĕṣennû = “gathers it for one who will be generous to the poor” — divine irony: the ill-gotten wealth is providentially redirected to the generous. YHWH ultimately controls the redistribution of wealth.
28.9: Mēsîr ʾoznô miššĕmōaʿ tôrāh = “Whoever turns his ear away from hearing the law” — the deliberate aversion of the ear from tôrāh. Gam-tĕpillātô tôʿēbāh = “even his prayer is detestable” (tôʿēbāh, abomination). The shocking inversion: the person who ignores divine instruction when it suits them cannot expect their requests to be received. Selective listening to God is answered by selective hearing of prayer.
28.10: Mašgeh yĕšārîm bĕderek rāʿ = “Whoever leads the righteous astray in an evil way” — šāgāh (lead astray, cause to wander) describes deliberate misdirection of those who are upright. Bišḥûtô hûʾ-yippôl = “into his own pit he will fall” — the trap prepared for others becomes the agent's own snare. Ûtĕmîmîm yinḥălû-ṭôb = “but the blameless will inherit good.”
28.11: Ḥākām bĕ'ênāyw ʾîš ʿāšîr = “A rich man is wise in his own eyes” — wealth creates the illusion of wisdom by providing access to results (people comply, projects succeed) that real wisdom would also produce. Wĕdal mêbîn yaḥqĕrennû = “but a poor man with discernment will see right through him.” Ḥāqar (search/investigate/probe) — the discerning poor person probes and exposes the hollow wisdom of the wealthy fool.
28.12: Baʿălôṣ ṣaddîqîm rabbāh tipʾāret = “When the righteous triumph, great is the glory/rejoicing” — tipʾāret (glory/beauty/splendor) describes the flourishing state of a community under righteous leadership. Ûbĕqûm rĕšāʿîm yĕḥuppas ʾādām = “but when the wicked rise to power, people go into hiding” — the social compression of wickedness in power: public life becomes dangerous, people withdraw. See v28 for the mirror image.
28.13: Mĕkasseh pĕšāʿāyw lō' yaṣlîaḥ = “Whoever conceals his sins will not prosper” — kāsāh (cover/conceal) here is self-protective hiding, not the generous “covering” of 17:9. Self-concealment of sin prevents the breakthrough that would lead to mercy. Ûmôdeh wĕ'ōzēb yĕruḥām = “but whoever confesses (yādāh) and forsakes them will find mercy.” Yādāh (confess/praise) is the same root as thanksgiving: confession is a kind of honoring of God's authority over the sin. The closest wisdom equivalent to the NT confession theology of 1 John 1:9.
28.14: ʾAšrê ʾādām mĕpahēd tāmîd = “Happy is the one who fears (pāḥad) the LORD continually” — pāḥad is the strong trembling-fear, more vivid than yārēʾ (the standard “fear of YHWH”); it suggests an ongoing disposition of awe before YHWH's holiness. Ûmaqšeh libbô yippōl bĕrāʿāh = “but whoever hardens his heart (qāšāh-lēb) will fall into calamity.” The hard heart is Pharaoh's condition (Exod 7:3); applying it to the wisdom setting grounds the two trajectories of Proverbs in covenantal history.
28.15: ʾAryēh nōhēm wĕdōb šōqēq = “A roaring lion or a charging/rushing bear (dōb šōqēq)” — šāqaq (rush headlong, roam recklessly) describes the bear's predatory charging motion. Mōšēl rāšāʿ ʿal-ʿam dāl = “so is a wicked ruler over a poor people” — the wicked ruler over the powerless combines both predatory animals: the lion's roar (intimidation) and the bear's charge (violence). The poor people have no means of resistance.
28.16: Nāgîd ḥăsar tĕbûnôt = “A ruler lacking discernment (tĕbûnôt)” — tĕbûnāh (discernment/insight) is the practical wisdom to understand situations. Wĕrab maʿăśaqqôt = “and a great oppressor.” The connection is diagnostic: lack of discernment and oppression are linked — the ruler who cannot think cannot govern and so resorts to force. Śônēʾ beṣaʿ yaʾărîk yāmîm = “but whoever hates unjust gain will prolong his days.”>
28.17: ʾĀdām ʿāšûq bĕdam-nepeš = “A man burdened with the guilt of blood (dam-nepeš) of another person” — nepeš (H5315) here is “life/person” — the whole living person whose blood has been shed. ʿAd-bôr yānûs ʾal-yitměkû-bô = “will flee to the pit; let no one help him.” The blood-guilt pursues the killer to Sheol (bôr, the pit/grave); the prohibition on helping him is a community protection against harboring a fugitive blood-guiltsman.
28.18: Hôlēk tāmîm yiwwāšēaʿ = “Whoever walks with integrity (tōm) will be saved” — yāšaʿ (be saved/delivered) — the salvation vocabulary applied to moral integrity; the root is the same as yēšûʿāh (salvation/Joshua/Jesus). Wĕniʿqaš dĕrākayim yippôl bĕ'aḥat = “but the one who is crooked in his ways (niʿqaš) will fall at once.”> Niʿqaš (crooked/twisted-double paths) is the literal image of walking a zig-zag path.
28.19: ʿŌbēd ʾadmātô yiśbaʿ lāḥem = “Whoever works his land will have plenty of bread” — agricultural labor as the paradigm of productive work. Ûmĕraddēp rêqîm yiśbaʿ rîš = “but whoever chases empty pursuits (rêqîm, empty things) will have plenty of poverty” — the bitter irony: the diligent worker has plenty of bread; the idle chaser has plenty of poverty. The parallel structure lets the word “plenty” (yiśbaʿ, be sated) carry the contrast.
28.20: ʾÎš ʾĕmûnôt rab-bĕrākôt = “A faithful man (ʾĕmûnôt, faithfulnesses) will abound with blessings” — ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness/reliability) is the character quality of consistent trustworthiness. Wĕʾāṣ lĕhāʿăšîr lō' yinnāqeh = “but whoever is eager/hasty to get rich will not go unpunished” — the urgency of wealth-seeking corrupts the faithful character; get-rich-quick schemes require cutting ethical corners.
28.21: Hakkēr-pānîm lō'-ṭôb = “To show partiality (hakkēr pānîm, recognize/acknowledge a face) is not good” — the idiom for favoritism: granting favorable treatment because you recognize (know) someone. Ûʿal-pat leḥem yipšaʿ-gāber = “yet for a mere piece of bread a man will commit a wrong.” The smallness of the inducement is the point: partiality is so deeply embedded that it takes almost nothing to activate it.
28.22: Niḇhāl lĕhôn ʾîš rāʿ ʿāyin = “A man who is eager/hasty for wealth is a man of an evil eye” — raʿ ʿāyin (evil eye) is the Hebrew idiom for stinginess/greed (contrast ṭôb ʿāyin, good eye = generosity, in 22:9). Wĕlō' yēdaʿ kî-ḥeser yĕbōʾennû = “and does not know (yādaʿ, H3045) that poverty is coming for him.”> His greed blinds him to the trajectory his greed creates.
28.23: Môkîaḥ ʾādām ʾaḥărāy ḥēn yimṣā' = “Whoever rebukes a person will afterward find more favor (ḥēn) than the one who flatters” — ḥēn (H2580, dispute_level=2): grace/favor/charm/kindness. The dispute is whether it describes the approval the rebuker receives from the rebuked person in retrospect, or from the community. The MKT “more favor” is right: honest rebuke, though painful initially, earns genuine relational capital that flattery never builds.
28.24: Gōzēl ʾābîw wĕʾimmô wĕʾōmēr ʾên-pāšaʿ = “Whoever robs his father or mother and says ‘There is nothing wrong with it’” — gāzal (rob/seize by violence) applied to parents; the rationalizing speech ‘ên pāšaʿ’ (no transgression) is the hallmark of hardened conscience. Ḥābēr hûʾ lĕʾîš mašḥît = “is a partner to a man who destroys” — the rationalizer is classified with the destroyer; the self-justification does not change the moral category.
28.25: Raḥab-nepeš yĕgāreh mādon = “A greedy person (raḥab-nepeš, wide/expanded of appetite) stirs up strife” — nepeš (H5315) here = appetite/desire; the “wide-souled” person whose desires have no limit. Ûbôṭēaḥ ʿal-YHWH yĕdušshan = “but whoever trusts in the LORD will prosper/be fat (dāšan).” The one who trusts YHWH (rather than expanding their own appetite) receives the prosperity the greedy person grasps after.
28.26: Bôṭēaḥ bĕlibbô hûʾ kĕsîl = “Whoever trusts his own heart is a fool” — the lēb (heart/mind) as the center of reasoning and willing; self-trust of this kind is specifically named folly. Wĕhôlēk bĕḥokmāh hûʾ yimmālēṭ = “but whoever walks in wisdom will be kept safe” — yimmālēṭ (escape/be delivered). The contrast is between two sources of guidance: self-referential trust vs. wisdom-shaped walking.
28.27: Nôtēn lārāš ʾên mahsôr = “Whoever gives to the poor will not lack (mahsôr)” — the generosity paradox: giving creates no deficit for the giver. Wĕmaʿlîm ʿênāyw rab-mĕʾērôt = “but whoever hides his eyes from them will get many curses.” Maʿlîm ʿênāyw = averting the gaze — the deliberate non-seeing that enables inaction. The curses are both social (from the poor) and divine (for ignoring covenant obligation).
28.28: Bĕqûm rĕšāʿîm yissātēr ʾādām = “When the wicked rise to power, people go into hiding” — the public withdrawal described in v12 now bookends the chapter. Ûbĕʾābĕdām yirbû ṣaddîqîm = “but when the wicked perish, the righteous multiply.”> The righteous community's growth is directly linked to the removal of wicked power: oppression suppresses flourishing; its removal allows multiplication. The chapter closes with this political-theological vision.

29.1: ʾÎš tôkaḥôt maqšeh-ʿōrep = “A man who is often reproved (tôkaḥôt, reproofs) yet stiffens his neck (maqšeh-ʿōrep)” — qāšāh ʿōrep (stiffen the neck) is the covenant-violation posture of Deut 31:27; the neck-stiffening of the wilderness generation. Peta' yiššābēr wĕʾên marpēʾ = “will suddenly be broken beyond remedy.”> Marpēʾ (healing, remedy) — the absence of remedy after the break; the repetition of reproof without response exhausts the window of healing.
29.2: Birĕbôt ṣaddîqîm yiśmaḥ hāʿām = “When the righteous are in authority, the people rejoice” — the political theology of joy: righteous rule produces communal flourishing that is experienced as joy. Ûbimšōl rāšāʿ yēʾānah ʿām = “when the wicked rule, the people groan.”> ʾĀnaḥ (groan/sigh) — the bodily expression of distress under oppressive governance. The groan of the people under the wicked is the inverse of their song under the righteous.
29.3: ʾÎš ʾōhēb ḥokmāh yĕśammaḥ ʾābîw = “Whoever loves wisdom brings his father joy” — the parent-child wisdom theme of 10:1. Wĕrōʿēh zônôt yĕʾabbēd hôn = “but a companion of prostitutes wastes his wealth (hôn).”> ʾābad (perish/destroy/lose) applied to hôn (wealth/substance) — the seductive woman of ch7 as an economic as well as spiritual destroyer.
29.4: Melek bĕmišpāṭ yaʿămîd ʾāreṣ = “A king (melek, H4428, dispute_level=2) establishes the land through justice” — šāphaṭ (justice) as the architectural force of political stability. Wĕ'îš tĕrûmôt yehersennāh = “but one who accepts bribes (tĕrûmôt, tribute-offerings/bribes) tears it down.”> The term tĕrûmāh normally means the priestly offering set apart; its ironic use for bribes suggests that a bribe is a tribute offered to the wrong altar — corrupting what should be holy judgment.
29.5: Geber meḥallēq ʿal-rēʿēhû = “A man who flatters his neighbor” — ḥālaqqāh (smooth/flattery) — the smooth speech that disarms honest assessment. Rešet pôrēś ʿal-pĕʿāmāyw = “spreads a net for his feet.”> The flatterer's smooth words are a trap laid under the feet of the one being praised; the danger of flattery is that it prevents the flattered person from seeing clearly and so they walk into what they cannot see.
29.6: Bĕpeša' ʾîš-rāʿ môqēš = “In the transgression of an evil man there is a snare” — the wicked person's sin is itself a trap that catches them. Wĕṣaddîq yārûn wĕyiśmāḥ = “but the righteous sing (rûn) and rejoice.”> Rûn (shout for joy/sing a ringing cry) — the vocal expression of liberation; the righteous person moves through the same territory the wicked stumbles in and sings rather than stumbles.
29.7: Yōdēaʿ ṣaddîq dîn dallîm = “The righteous man knows (yāddaʿ, H3045) the cause of the poor” — yādaʿ here carries the full weight of “care-about” (as in “the LORD knows the way of the righteous,” Ps 1:6): not mere intellectual knowledge but engaged concern. Rāšāʿ lō'-yābîn dāʿat = “the wicked man does not care to understand it.”> The refusal to understand is itself a moral act: willful epistemic distance from the poor's situation is wickedness.
29.8: ʾAnšê lāṣôn yāpîḥû qiryāh = “Scornful men inflame (pûaḥ, blow/breathe) a city” — pûaḥ (blow like wind) applied to city-inflaming: the scornful breathe on the coals of social conflict until a city burns. Waḥăkāmîm yāšîbû ʾāp = “but wise men turn away anger (ʾāp, nose/anger).”> The same nose-image: ʾāp (wrath, lit. the snorting nose) is turned back by wisdom; the hot breath of scorn is countered by the cooling counsel of the wise.
29.9: ʾÎš ḥākām niśpāṭ ʾet-ʾîš ʾĕwîl = “If a wise man contends in court (šāphaṭ, H8199, dispute_level=2) with a fool” — šāphaṭ carries the range of judge/govern/decide; in the court setting it is “to dispute/plead.”> Wĕrāgaz wĕśāḥaq wĕʾên nāḥat = “the fool only rages and laughs, and there is no quiet.”> The fool's courtroom behavior — alternating between rage and dismissive laughter — makes rational proceedings impossible; the wise man's case cannot be heard.
29.10: ʾAnšê dāmîm yiśnĕʾû-tām = “Bloodthirsty men hate the upright (tām, blameless/complete)” — the blameless person is the target of those who live by violence; innocence provokes those whose self-justification depends on everyone being as guilty as they are. Wîšārîm yĕbaqqĕšû napšô = “but the righteous seek to protect his life (nepeš, H5315).”> Nepeš here = life/person; the righteous protect the vulnerable blameless person.
29.11: Kol-rûḥô yôṣîʾ kĕsîl = “A fool vents all his spirit/feelings (rûaḥ, H7307)” — rûaḥ has the range: spirit / breath / wind / disposition / feeling. Here “all his spirit” = every impulse, emotion, and reaction immediately expressed. Wĕḥākām bĕʾāḥôr yĕšabbĕḥennāh = “but a wise man quietly holds it back.”> Šābaḥ (calm/still/hold back) — the same root used in Ps 89:9 of YHWH stilling the sea: wisdom rules the inner weather the way YHWH rules the storm.
29.12: Mōšēl maqšîb ʿal-dĕbar-šāqer = “If a ruler listens to lies” — the leader who opens himself to false reports has already compromised the governing function. Kol-mĕšārĕtāyw rĕšāʿîm = “all his officials become wicked.”> The moral ecology of leadership: a ruler who tolerates lying selects for wicked officials who survive by telling him what he will accept. Corruption propagates downward from receptive leadership.
29.13: Rāš wĕʾîš tĕkākîm nipgāšû = “The poor man and the oppressor have this in common” — the unexpected juxtaposition. Mĕʾîr ʿênê-šĕnêhem YHWH = “the LORD gives light to the eyes of both.”> The divine gift of sight (life-capacity) is democratically given — oppressor and oppressed both receive the same basic endowment from YHWH. This is not moral equivalence but creational equality: YHWH is Creator of both.
29.14: Melek šôpēṭ bĕʾĕmet dallîm = “A king (melek, H4428) who judges (šāphaṭ, H8199) the poor with faithfulness (ʾĕmet, H571, dispute_level=2)” — three disputed terms converge. ʾĕmet ranges across truth/faithfulness/reliability; here it describes the quality of the king's judgment toward the powerless. Kissĕʾô lāʿad yekkôn = “his throne will be established forever.”> The stability of the throne is directly proportional to the faithfulness of its justice toward the most vulnerable.
29.15: Šēbeṭ wĕtôkaḥat yittēn ḥokmāh = “The rod and reproof give wisdom” — the dual instruments of formation: šēbeṭ (rod/staff, physical correction) and tôkaḥat (verbal reproof). Wĕnaʿar mĕšullāḥ mēbîš ʾimmô = “but a child left to himself (mĕšullāḥ, sent away/released) brings shame to his mother.”> The released child — uncorrected, unformed — is a community liability; his mother bears the social cost of his formation failure.
29.16: Birĕbôt rĕšāʿîm yirbeh-pāšaʿ = “When the wicked increase (rābāh), transgression increases” — multiplication of wicked persons generates its own momentum: wickedness begets wickedness in a social amplification effect. Wĕṣaddîqîm bĕmappaltām yirʾû = “but the righteous will see their downfall.”> The righteous are spectators of divine judgment on the wicked; they do not need to avenge themselves.
29.17: Yassēr binkā wînîḥekā = “Discipline your son and he will give you rest” — yāsar (discipline/chastise) as the formative act that produces peace in the parent. Wĕyittēn maʿadannîm lĕnapšekā = “he will give delight (maʿadannîm, delicacies/luxuries) to your soul (nepeš, H5315).”> Nepeš here = self/inner life; the disciplined son's character is the parent's spiritual luxury — the inner satisfaction deeper than material pleasure.
29.18: Bĕʾên ḥāzôn yippāraʿ ʿām = “Where there is no prophetic vision (ḥāzôn), the people are unrestrained (pāraʿ)” — ḥāzôn is the technical prophetic term for divine revelation received in a vision (Isa 1:1; Obad 1:1). The absence of prophetic word dissolves social restraint; without revelation the moral framework loses its authoritative grounding. Wĕšōmēr tôrāh ʾašrêhû = “but blessed is the one who keeps the law.”> The pairing of prophetic vision and tôrāh-keeping as dual sources of order is the governing theme of the verse.
29.19: Bidbārîm lō'-yiwwāsēr ʿābed = “A servant cannot be corrected by mere words” — the limits of verbal instruction in certain contexts. Kî yābîn wĕʾên maʿăneh = “for he understands but will not respond.”> The servant who hears and understands but refuses to respond illustrates the gap between intellectual reception and behavioral change — understanding without transformed will.
29.20: Ḥāzîtā ʾîš ʾāṣ bidbārāyw = “Do you see a man who is hasty (ʾūṣ) in his words?”> — ʾūṣ (hasten/urge) — the urgency of words not thought through. Tiqwāh lĕkĕsîl mimmennû = “There is more hope for a fool than for him.”> The highest negative comparison in Proverbs: to be ranked worse than the fool is to be classified as a special case of recklessness. The hasty-tongued person is beyond the fool because the fool at least may be ignorant; the hasty speaker knows but does not pause.
29.21: Mĕpannēq minnōʿar ʿabdô = “Whoever pampers (pānaḳ) his servant from childhood” — pānaḳ (pamper/indulge/coddle) — the excessive softening of the servant's formation. Wĕʾaḥărîtô yihyeh māndôn = “will find that in the end he has become like a son.”> The indulged servant loses the working relationship and claims familial entitlements; overly soft treatment erodes proper distinctions.
29.22: ʾÎš-ʾap yĕgāreh mādon = “An angry man (ʾap, nose/anger) stirs up strife” — the nose-anger idiom again (cf. v8): the short-tempered person is a chronic strife-generator. Ûbaʿal ḥēmāh rab-pāšaʿ = “and a hot-tempered man (baʿal ḥēmāh, master of wrath) abounds in transgression.”> The hot-tempered person's transgression multiplies because anger bypasses the moral deliberation that restrains sin.
29.23: Gaʾăwat ʾādām tašpîlennû = “A man's pride (gaʾăwāh) will bring him low” — gaʾăwāh (pride/arrogance/majesty) — the same root as divine majesty when applied to YHWH (Ps 93:1), but here human presumption. Ûšĕpal rûaḥ yitmōk kābôd = “but the one who is humble of spirit (šĕpal rûaḥ) will obtain honor (kābôd).”> Both rûaḥ (H7307) and kābôd (H3519, dispute_level=2) are contested: rûaḥ = spirit/breath/disposition; kābôd = honor/glory/weight. The humble-spirited person receives the weighty honor that the proud person grasps after and loses.
29.24: Ḥôlēq im-gannāb śônēʾ napšô = “Whoever partners with a thief hates himself (śônēʾ napšô)” — the self-destructive logic of complicity: partnering with wickedness is hatred toward one's own life. ʾAlāh yišmaʿ wĕlō' yaggîd = “he hears the curse but does not disclose it.”> The silent witness who hears a public oath-curse (calling for disclosure of wrongdoing) and refuses to testify — both guilty participant and cowardly bystander.
29.25: Ḥardat ʾādām yittēn môqēš = “The fear of man (ḥardat ʾādām) lays a snare” — human-directed fear as a trap; the anxiety about what people will think or do constrains action and leads into self-protective compromise. Ûbôṭēaḥ baYHWH yĕśuggāb = “but whoever trusts in YHWH (H3068) is kept safe (śāgab, set on high/made inaccessible).”> Śāgab = to be placed on an inaccessible height — beyond the reach of the snare. The trust that transfers fear from humans to YHWH produces the elevation the snare cannot reach.
29.26: Rabbîm mĕbaqqĕšîm pĕnê-môšēl = “Many seek the ruler's favor (pĕnê-môšēl, the face of the ruler)” — court-seeking as the normal mode of obtaining advantage; bāqaš pānîm = to seek the face = to appeal for access/approval. Ûmêʾet YHWH mišpaṭ-ʾîš = “but justice for a man comes from YHWH.”> The verse redirects the petitioner: the court of ultimate appeal is not the human ruler but YHWH. Human rulers can be circumvented, corrupted, or inaccessible; YHWH's justice is the final and reliable recourse.
29.27: Tôʿăbat ṣaddîqîm ʾîš ʿāwel = “An unjust man is an abomination to the righteous” — the mutual repulsion of righteousness and wickedness: the tôʿēbāh (abomination) that YHWH feels toward wickedness (12:22; 15:8) is felt by the righteous as well — moral revulsion is a feature of righteous character. Wĕtôʿăbat rāšāʿ yĕšar-dārek = “and the one who walks uprightly is an abomination to the wicked.”> The mutual abomination is symmetric: righteousness is intolerable to wickedness as wickedness is to righteousness.

30.1: Dibrê ʾāgûr ben-yāqeh hammassāʾ = “The words of Agur son of Jakeh. The oracle (massāʾ).”> Massāʾ is both “burden” and “oracle/utterance” — the technical term for prophetic pronouncements (Isa 13:1; Nah 1:1). Agur and Jakeh do not appear elsewhere in the OT; the names and the following verses suggest a non-Israelite sage of recognized wisdom, or a pseudonymous framework. The speaker claims to have addressed ‘Ithiel’ and ‘Ucal’ (persons or place names debated). The oracle framework authorizes what follows as more than personal musing.
30.2: Kî baʿar ʾānōkî mêʾîš = “Surely I am more brutish (baʿar, like a beast/animal) than any man” — baʿar (animal/beast) as a self-descriptor: the radical self-deprecation of the sage who has reached the limits of wisdom and acknowledges the gap. Wĕlō' bînat ʾādām lî = “and I do not have the understanding of a human being.”> This is not false modesty but the apophatic starting point: wisdom begins with acknowledging what one does not and cannot know by human means alone.
30.3: Wĕlō'-lāmadtî ḥokmāh = “I have not learned wisdom” — Agur's disclaimer intensifies: not only brutish but untaught. Wĕdaʿat qĕdōšîm ʾēdāʿ = “nor have I knowledge (dāʿat) of the Holy One (qĕdōšîm).”> Qĕdōšîm (plural) applied to God — either an intensive plural of majesty (the uniquely holy One) or acknowledging the heavenly council; the MKT “Holy One” (singular) follows the most common interpretation. Yādaʿ (H3045, dispute_level=2) here = intimate relational knowledge, not merely propositional awareness.
30.4: Mî ʿālāh-šāmayim wayyērēd = “Who has ascended to heaven and come back down?”> — the rhetorical questions of vv4-5 are among the most theologically charged in Proverbs. The sequence — ascend heaven, gather wind in fists, wrap waters in garment, establish earth's ends — describes divine transcendence beyond all human capacity. Mah-šĕmô ûmah-šem-bĕnô = “What is his name, and what is his son's name (šem-bĕnô)?”> — the mention of a “son” alongside the Creator God is unique in the OT wisdom corpus; it generates Christological reflection in the Christian tradition (Heb 1:2-3 applies creation-language to the Son). The verse acknowledges that the answer to its own question exceeds human wisdom.
30.5: Kol-ʾimrat ʾĕlôah ṣĕrûpāh = “Every word of God (ʾĕlôah) proves true (ṣārap, is refined/tested)” — ṣārap (refine/smelt/test) used of divine speech: as silver is refined of dross in the crucible (Ps 12:6), so every divine utterance is pure of error. Māgēn hûʾ laḥōsîm bô = “he is a shield to those who take refuge in him.”> Confidence in the reliability of divine speech produces the trust that finds YHWH as shield — the epistemological trustworthiness of God's word is the foundation of personal refuge.
30.6: ʾAl-tôsēp ʿal-dĕbārāyw = “Do not add to his words (dĕbārāyw)” — the prohibition against supplementing divine speech with human invention echoes the Deuteronomic prohibition (Deut 4:2; 12:32). Pen-yôkîaḥ bĕkā wĕnikzabĕtā = “lest he rebuke you and you be found a liar.”> The addition of human words to divine speech invites divine rebuke — the human additions are measured against the pure divine word (v5) and found to be the lies they are.
30.7: Šĕtayim šāʾaltî mêʾittāk = “Two things I ask of you; do not deny them to me before I die” — the prayer formula that introduces vv8-9: a numbered-list prayer of specific requests. Agur's prayer is notable for its concrete, non-triumphalist character — asking not for greatness but for moral safety. The double petition is framed by mortality awareness: “before I die.”>
30.8: Šāwĕʾ ûdbar kāzāb harḥēq mimmennî = “Keep falsehood and lies (šāwĕʾ ûdbar kāzāb) far from me” — šāwĕʾ (emptiness/vanity/falsehood) and kāzāb (lie/deception) — the prayer to be kept from both empty vanity and deliberate lying. Rēš wĕʿōšer ʾal-tittĕn-lî = “give me neither poverty nor riches (ʿōšer).”> The prayer for the middle way — the rare wisdom petition that refuses both extremes, knowing that both carry spiritual risk.
30.9: Pen-ʾeśbaʿ wĕkiḥaštî wĕʾāmartî mî YHWH = “lest I be full and deny you, saying ‘Who is the LORD (YHWH, H3068)?’” — the danger of satiety: fullness produces the arrogant forgetting of God that Deut 8:12-14 warns Israel about. Û-pen-ʾiwwārēš wĕgānabĕtî wĕtāpaśtî šēm ʾĕlōhāy = “or lest I be poor and steal and profane (tāpas) the name of my God (ʾĕlōhāy, H430).”> ʾĕlōhîm (H430) — grammatically plural but taking singular verbs; the name “my God” is personalized. The prayer holds together two theological dangers: prosperity-induced atheism and poverty-induced theft that dishonors the divine name.
30.10: ʾAl-talsēn ʿebed ʾel-ʾădōnāyw = “Do not slander (lāšan, use the tongue against) a servant to his master” — lāšan is literally “to tongue” someone — to use speech as an instrument of harm. Pen-yĕqallĕlekā wĕʾāšāmĕtā = “lest he curse you and you be found guilty.”> The curse of the slandered servant (perhaps invoking divine justice) rebounds on the slanderer; interfering in the master-servant relationship carries its own accountability.
30.11: Dôr ʾābîw yĕqallēl = “There is a generation that curses its father” — the first of four ‘generation’ (dôr) portraits (vv11-14), each describing a type of moral failure. The father-cursing generation inverts the fifth commandment (Exod 20:12); wĕʾet-ʾimmô lō'-yĕbārēk = “and does not bless its mother.”> The double failure — active cursing of the father, passive withholding of blessing from the mother — covers both poles of parental dishonor.
30.12: Dôr ṭāhôr bĕ'ênāyw = “There is a generation that is pure in its own eyes” — the self-deception of the morally filthy who believe themselves clean. Ûmĕṣōʾātô lō' ruḥāṣ = “but has not been washed from its filth.”> The purification language (rāḥaṣ, wash) points to the ritual-purity framework: the generation that has never submitted to cleansing stands before God as genuinely dirty despite its own assessment. The verse anticipates the prophetic critique of self-righteous religion (Isa 1:16; Jer 2:22).
30.13: Dôr māh-rāmû ʿênāyw = “There is a generation — how lofty are their eyes (rāmû ʿênāyw)!”> — rûm ʿênayim (high/lifted eyes) is the idiom for arrogant disdain; the eyes that look down because they are looking down from a superior position. Wĕʿapʿappāyw yinnāśeʾû = “how high their eyelids are lifted!”> The eyelids (ʿapʿappayim, the fluttering eyelids) are another idiom for contemptuous staring. The generation portrait is one of physical pride-posturing that expresses inner arrogance.
30.14: Dôr ḥărābôt šinnāyw = “There is a generation whose teeth are swords” — the fourth and most violent generation: their teeth are swords and their fangs are knives, devouring the poor and needy from the earth. The eating-as-violence metaphor makes social oppression visceral: the powerful consume the weak the way predatory animals devour prey. Leʾăkōl ʿăniyyîm mêʾereṣ wĕʾebyônîm mêʾādām = “to devour the poor from the earth and the needy from among men.”>
30.15: Laʿălûqāh šĕtê bānôt hav hav = “The leech has two daughters: ‘Give’ and ‘Give’” — the leech (ʿalûqāh, a hapax legomenon; the blood-sucking worm or vampire) as an image of insatiable demand. The transition to numerical sayings: “Three things are never satisfied; four never say ‘Enough.’” The numerical formula (X+1) is characteristic of Agur's style (cf. vv18,21,24,29) — it creates anticipation and climax.
30.16: Šĕʾôl wĕʾōṣer reḥem = “Sheol, the barren womb, the earth that is never satisfied with water, and the fire” — the four things never satisfied: Sheol's appetite for the dead, the barren womb's longing for children, the earth's thirst that cannot be permanently quenched, and fire's insatiable consumption. The combination moves from death to fertility to earth to fire — the full range of natural forces that exceed human capacity to fill or satisfy.
30.17: ʿAyin tileʿag lĕʾāb = “The eye that mocks (lāʿag) a father and scorns to obey a mother” — the eye as the instrument of contempt (linking back to vv13-14). Wĕyiqrûhā ʿōrbê-naḥal wĕyōʾklûhā bĕnê-nāšer = “will be picked out by the ravens of the valley and eaten by the vultures.”> The graphic imagery of unburied death: the eye that showed contempt for parents will be plucked out and devoured by carrion birds — the ultimate reversal of honor and the completion of shame.
30.18: Šĕlōšāh hēmmāh niplĕʾû mimmennî = “Three things are too wonderful (niplāʾ) for me; four I do not understand (yādaʿ, H3045, dispute_level=2)”> — yādaʿ here = comprehend/master. The “wonderful” (pālāʾ) is the same root as God's works being “wonders” (Ps 77:11); Agur applies the vocabulary of divine mystery to natural phenomena. The four wonders are presented as things that exceed his understanding despite their visibility.
30.19: Derek hāneśer baššāmāyim = “the way of an eagle in the sky” — the four wonders: eagle in sky, serpent on rock, ship in sea, and man with young woman. What they share is the leaving of no trace: the eagle, snake, and ship pass through their medium without permanent mark; the way of a man with a maiden is similarly impenetrable in its movement. The fourth (“the way of a man with a young woman”) gives the series its climax and its interpretive key: the wonder of intimate relationship is as mysterious as natural phenomena.
30.20: Kēn derek ʾiššāh mĕnāʾāpet = “Such is the way of an adulteress” — the transition from wonder to warning: the adulteress mimics the wonder of the man-with-maiden passage but with moral inversion. ʾākĕlāh ûmāḥătāh pîhā wĕʾāmĕrāh lō'-pāʿaltî ʾāwen = “she eats and wipes her mouth and says, ‘I have done nothing wrong.’”> The wiping of the mouth erases the trace, exactly as the four wonders left no trace — but here the trace-erasure is self-serving denial of moral reality.
30.21: Taḥat šālôš rāgĕzāh ʾereṣ = “Under three things the earth trembles (rāgaz); under four it cannot bear up (śāʾ)” — the second four-part series in Agur's oracle, this time with negative-social focus. The earth's trembling is the physical response to the inversion of proper social order; the cosmos reacts to the violation of its built-in hierarchies.
30.22: Taḥat ʿebed kî yimlôk = “a servant when he reigns” — the first of four social inversions: servant become king. Wĕnābāl kî śābaʿ lāḥem = “and a fool when he is full of food.”> Nābāl (fool/knave) — the morally deficient person. The earth trembles not at the events themselves but at the disproportionate results they produce: a satiated fool is more dangerous than a hungry one.
30.23: Taḥat śĕnûʾāh kî tibbaʿēl = “an unloved woman when she gets a husband” — the third inversion: the socially overlooked woman who suddenly gains the position of wife and uses it for vengeance or domination. Wĕšipḥāh kî-tîrāš gĕbirtāh = “and a maidservant who displaces (yāraš, inherit/dispossess) her mistress.”> The servant who inherits the mistress's position — the final inversion of domestic order. All four earth-trembling situations involve the abrupt reversal of social position without the formation of character to match.
30.24: ʾArbaʿāh hēm qĕṭannê-ʾāreṣ = “Four things on earth are small (qāṭan), but they are exceedingly wise (mĕḥukkāmîm mĕʾōd)” — the third four-part series, this time celebratory: smallness with wisdom exceeds greatness without it. The four creatures will demonstrate that size and strength are not the measures of true wisdom.
30.25: Hanĕmālîm ʿam lō'-ʿāz = “the ants are a people not strong (ʿāz), yet they prepare their food in the summer” — the ant as the paradigm of foresight-wisdom: they plan for winter during summer abundance. The ant of 6:6-8 reappears; here in the context of small-but-wise creatures it demonstrates that wisdom-planning compensates for physical weakness.
30.26: Šĕpannîm ʿam lō'-ʿāṣûm = “the rock badgers are a feeble people (lō'-ʿāṣûm, not powerful), yet they make their homes in the cliffs” — the rock badger (coney/hyrax) compensates for vulnerability with strategic placement: they live in the inaccessible cliffs where predators cannot easily reach. Wisdom exploits environment; the weak creature uses terrain the strong creature ignores.
30.27: Hāʾarbeh ʾên melek (melek, H4428, dispute_level=2) = “the locusts have no king (melek)” — the absence of hierarchical leadership does not prevent the locusts from marching in perfect formation. Wĕyēṣēʾ ḥōṣēṣ kullô = “yet all of them march out in formation.”> The locust swarm without a king demonstrates that effective collective action can arise from distributed wisdom rather than central authority. The mention of melek is ironic in context: the animal without a king does what kings fail to achieve.
30.28: Śĕmāmît bĕyādayim tĕtappēś = “the lizard (śĕmāmît, a hapax — likely a small gecko or lizard) you can take in your hands, yet it is found in kings’ (mĕlek, H4428) palaces.”> The smallest and most easily caught creature reaches the highest human institution: the king's palace. The paradox of access: size and power do not determine where one can be found; the lizard's nimbleness takes it where strength cannot.
30.29: Šĕlōšāh hēmmāh mêṭîbê ṣāʿad = “Three things are majestic in their stride (ṭôb ṣāʿad); four are stately in their going” — the fourth four-part series, this time focused on dignity and bearing rather than wisdom or social inversion. Ṭôb ṣāʿad = good/fine of step; the dignity that communicates authority through movement.
30.30: Lāyiš gibbôr baббĕhēmāh = “the lion (lāyiš, a rare word for the great/old lion), which is mightiest among beasts and does not turn back before any.”> — lāyiš (only here and Isa 30:6; Job 4:11) is a more intensive lion term than ʾaryēh; the senior or great lion. The lion's dignity is its fearlessness: it does not retreat. The first in the series of stately things.
30.31: Zarzîr motnayim ʾô-ṭāyiš = “a strutting rooster or a he-goat (ṭāyiš)” — the word zarzîr (hapax; possibly “one girt in the loins”) is traditionally translated “strutting rooster” or “greyhound” (LXX) — an animal with visible physical readiness/bearing. Wĕmelek (melek, H4428, dispute_level=2) qûm ʿimmô = “and a king (melek) with his army arrayed before him.”> The king as the human example of stately bearing — regal authority visible in posture and movement. The series culminates in the king as the human counterpart to the lion.
30.32: ʾIm-nābaltā bĕhitnaaśēʾ = “If you have been foolish, exalting yourself (hitnaaśēʾ, lifting yourself up)” — the return to moral instruction after the observation sections. Wĕʾim-zammôtā yād lĕpeh = “or if you have devised evil, put your hand over your mouth.”> Yād lĕpeh (hand over the mouth) = the gesture of silencing oneself, stopping before the foolish exaltation or evil device is fully spoken. The gesture is a form of wisdom — catching oneself before words become action.
30.33: Kî mîṣ ḥālāb yôṣîʾ ḥemʾāh = “For as pressing (mîṣ) milk produces curds and pressing the nose produces blood, so pressing anger produces strife.”> The concluding proverb of the Agur oracle uses the same physical pressure image three times: the pressing/squeezing that produces inevitable products. Churning milk produces butter; pressing the nose produces blood; pressing anger produces strife. The wisdom application: stop pressing the anger before it produces strife, as you would stop before churning becomes spoilage. The oracle closes with this practical instruction for managing conflict.
